# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🎯 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between Leading and Managing. Justify why an engineering manager needs both."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Leading** is the process of influencing and motivating people to achieve a shared vision, whereas **Managing** is the process of planning, organizing, staffing, and controlling resources to achieve objectives efficiently. According to John Kotter (1990), management copes with *complexity* while leadership copes with *change*. An engineering manager must do both because modern projects (e.g., launching a satellite or building a smart grid) require **order** (management) AND **adaptation** (leadership). A manager-only approach delivers a compliant team; a leader-only approach delivers a vision without execution. The effective engineering manager integrates both roles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme (3 marks):** Definition of Leading (1 mark) + Definition of Managing (1 mark) + Justification citing Kotter (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List and briefly explain the Four 'I's of Transformational Leadership as given by Bass."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

According to **Bernard M. Bass (1985)**, Transformational Leadership has **four** components (the "4 I's"):
1. **Idealized Influence** — Leader serves as a role model with high ethical standards.
2. **Inspirational Motivation** — Leader communicates a compelling vision and inspires followers.
3. **Intellectual Stimulation** — Leader encourages creativity and challenges old assumptions.
4. **Individualized Consideration** — Leader provides personal mentoring and support to each follower.

These four elements enable followers to perform **beyond expectations**, distinguishing transformational leaders from transactional ones.

---

## 🎯 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** write only definitions. KTU 2024 board examiners *always* ask for **comparison tables, examples, and a closing synthesis**.
> - **Do NOT** confuse Kotter's "Leading" with Kotter's "Change Steps" — they are related but distinct.
> - **Do NOT** list only **3 of the 4 I's** in a Transformational question. The 4th I missing = automatic -2 marks.
> - **Do NOT** ignore the *engineering context*. Generic answers (e.g., "in a company") score 1 mark less than contextual ones (e.g., "in a Kerala-based EV startup").
> - **Do NOT** use the word "leadership" and "management" interchangeably in the same answer — examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** "Leading and Managing are two different, yet complementary, systems." Discuss this statement in detail with reference to John Kotter's framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** "The Blake-Mouton Managerial Grid is a two-dimensional model of leadership behaviour." Explain the five major styles in the grid with suitable examples from the engineering industry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Define both terms (1 mark):**
- **Management** = Achieving objectives through planning, organizing, staffing, directing, and controlling organizational resources.
- **Leadership** = Influencing and motivating people toward the achievement of a vision.

**Step 2 — State Kotter's premise (1 mark):**
John Kotter (1990) argued that management and leadership are **distinct but complementary**. He famously wrote, *"Management is about coping with complexity. Leadership is about coping with change."*

**Step 3 — Tabular comparison (3 marks):**

| Function | **Managing** | **Leading** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Planning & Budgeting | Sets detailed steps and timelines | Sets direction and creates the future vision |
| Organizing & Staffing | Establishes structure, allocates roles | Aligns and communicates vision to people |
| Controlling & Problem Solving | Monitors results against plan | Motivates and inspires people to overcome obstacles |
| Outcome | Order, predictability, efficiency | Change, agility, transformation |

**Step 4 — Engineering example (1 mark):**
In a Kerala-based EV manufacturing startup, the **CFO** manages budgets and cost-control; the **CTO** leads engineers toward a shared vision of indigenous, sustainable mobility. Both roles are essential.

**Step 5 — Synthesis (1 mark):**
In a VUCA world, a manager who cannot lead produces a stagnant but compliant team; a leader who cannot manage produces a vision without execution. The **effective engineering manager integrates both systems**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Introduce the model (1 mark):**
The **Blake-Mouton Managerial Grid (1964)** plots **Concern for People (Y-axis)** against **Concern for Production (X-axis)** on a 1–9 scale, yielding five major leadership styles.

**Step 2 — Describe the five styles with engineering examples (5 marks):**

| Style | Coordinates | Behaviour | Engineering Example |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Impoverished (1,1)** | Low / Low | Evades responsibility, minimal effort | A site engineer who only shows up to sign attendance |
| **Country Club (1,9)** | Low Task / High People | Friendly but no output | A professor loved by students but with zero research output |
| **Authority-Compliance (9,1)** | High Task / Low People | Maximum output, no empathy | A production floor supervisor who achieves targets but has 100% attrition |
| **Middle-of-the-Road (5,5)** | Medium / Medium | Compromise, mediocrity | A project manager who never meets deadlines but never misses by much |
| **Team Management (9,9)** | High / High | IDEAL — high output AND high morale | An ISRO mission lead who delivers a launch on time and is revered by the team |

**Step 3 — Conclude with the ideal (1 mark):**
The (9,9) **Team Manager** style is supported by Ohio State studies (high structure + high consideration = high performance) and is the KTU-preferred model answer. Effective engineering leaders integrate both concern for the task and concern for the team.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Bass's Full Range Leadership Model**, distinguishing clearly between **Transactional and Transformational Leadership** with suitable engineering examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** "Effective leadership is multi-dimensional." Discuss the **four major dimensions of leadership** (Trait, Behavioural, Contingency, Power-Influence) with reference to at least one theorist in each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Introduce Bass (1 mark):**
**Bernard M. Bass (1985)** developed the **Full Range Leadership Model**, a continuum from passive laissez-faire through transactional to transformational leadership. Active leadership has two primary forms: **Transactional** and **Transformational**.

**Step 2 — Define Transactional Leadership with components (2 marks):**
Transactional leadership is based on a **contingent-reinforcement exchange** between leader and follower. It has three sub-components:
- **Contingent Reward** — Clear reward for achieving agreed goals.
- **Management-by-Exception (Active)** — Monitors performance and corrects deviations *before* they become problems.
- **Management-by-Exception (Passive)** — Intervenes only *after* standards are violated.

*Engineering Example:* A production-line supervisor at a Kochi-based electronics plant who pays workers a bonus per 100 defect-free units assembled.

**Step 3 — Define Transformational Leadership with 4 I's (3 marks):**
Transformational leadership inspires followers to **transcend self-interest** and achieve performance beyond contractual expectations. Bass identified four components (the "4 I's"):
1. **Idealized Influence** — Leader as ethical role model.
2. **Inspirational Motivation** — Leader articulates a compelling vision.
3. **Intellectual Stimulation** — Leader challenges assumptions and encourages innovation.
4. **Individualized Consideration** — Leader personally mentors each follower.

*Engineering Example:* A chief engineer at a Bengaluru space-tech startup who inspires the team to design a 90%-lighter satellite structure by framing the work as "serving the nation."

**Step 4 — Comparison and conclusion (1 mark):**
Transactional leadership produces **expected performance** through exchange; transformational leadership produces **extra effort and lasting change** through inspiration. The two are complementary, not contradictory.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — State the multidimensional nature (1 mark):**
Leadership cannot be explained by a single theory. The four major dimensions, each with its own focus and theorist, are: **Trait, Behavioural, Contingency, and Power-Influence.**

**Step 2 — Describe each dimension with a theorist (5 marks):**

| Dimension | Core Idea | Key Theorist | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trait** | Leaders possess innate personal qualities | **Stogdill (1948)**, **Mann (1959)** | A born-confident, tall, articulate person |
| **Behavioural** | Leadership is what leaders *do*, not who they *are* | **Ohio State Studies — LBDQ (Fleishman, 1953)**, **Michigan Studies (Katz et al., 1950)** | A leader who balances task and people orientation |
| **Contingency** | Effectiveness depends on situational fit | **Fiedler (1967)** — Least Preferred Coworker; **Hersey-Blanchard (1969)** — Situational Leadership | A leader who adapts style to team maturity |
| **Power-Influence** | Leadership is the exercise of influence | **French & Raven (1959)** — 5 Bases; **Burns (1978)** — Transactional vs. Transformational | A leader who uses referent power to inspire |

**Step 3 — Synthesis (1 mark):**
A holistic understanding of leadership requires integrating all four dimensions. The Trait approach explains **who** leads; the Behavioural approach explains **what** they do; the Contingency approach explains **when** it works; the Power-Influence approach explains **how** they succeed. KTU 2024 OBE expects students to apply the *correct dimension* to a given scenario.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this on one page for your KTU exam day.**

- ⭐ **Kotter's Quote (Must-Memorize):** *"Management is about coping with complexity. Leadership is about coping with change."*
- ⭐ **Leading vs. Managing = 4 Pairs:** Plan vs. Direct, Organize vs. Align, Staff vs. Motivate, Control vs. Inspire.
- ⭐ **The 4 I's of Transformational Leadership (Bass 1985):** Idealized Influence, Inspirational Motivation, Intellectual Stimulation, Individualized Consideration. *Missing one = -2 marks.*
- ⭐ **Ohio State Studies = High-High:** Initiating Structure (S) + Consideration (C) = Best Leader.
- ⭐ **Blake-Mouton Ideal = (9, 9):** Team Management = High Production + High People.
- ⭐ **Transactional Components = 3:** Contingent Reward, MBE-Active, MBE-Passive.
- ⭐ **Bass Full Range Model = Continuum:** Laissez-Faire → Transactional → Transformational.
- ⭐ **4 Dimensions of Leadership:** Trait, Behavioural, Contingency, Power-Influence. *Memorize one theorist per dimension.*
- ⭐ **James MacGregor Burns (1978)** coined the Transactional vs. Transformational distinction. Bass (1985) operationalized it with the 4 I's.
- ⭐ **Engineering Context Rule:** Always pair a management concept with an *engineering* example (ISRO, Kerala EV startup, software team) for a +1 mark bonus in the KTU board valuation.
- ⭐ **For 14-mark answers, always:** Definition (1) + Comparison Table (3-4) + Engineering Example (1) + Synthesis (1) = Full marks structure.

> **End of Notes — Module 2 — Leading vs. Managing & Leadership Styles**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Control Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET504 - MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Control Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Basic Control Process

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
In the context of management theory aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the **Basic Control Process** is formally defined as the systematic, cyclic, and continuous managerial function of monitoring, measuring, evaluating, and correcting organizational performance to ensure that actual outcomes conform to pre-established plans, standards, and objectives. It is the **fourth and final function of the management process**, succeeding *Planning, Organizing,* and *Directing*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Control is the process of monitoring organizational performance against predetermined standards and taking corrective action whenever there is a significant deviation to ensure that organizational goals are achieved as planned."*

The Basic Control Process is universally depicted as a closed-loop, four-step cyclical mechanism:
1. **Setting Performance Standards (Benchmarking)**
2. **Measuring Actual Performance (Data Acquisition)**
3. **Comparing Performance with Standards (Variance Analysis)**
4. **Taking Corrective Action (Deviation Removal)**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the **Basic Control Process** like the **thermostat system in your home**. Imagine you set your room temperature to **24 °C** (this is your *standard*). The thermostat's sensor continuously *measures* the actual room temperature. It *compares* the actual reading with the 24 °C setpoint. If the room becomes too hot (say 28 °C), the thermostat *takes corrective action* by switching on the air conditioner until the deviation is corrected. Once corrected, the cycle restarts. Management control works identically — managers set targets, measure results, spot deviations, and fix them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Control is *cyclical, continuous, and forward-looking*. It is not a one-time inspection but a recurring loop embedded into every managerial activity.

### Standard Metrics Used in Control
The following quantitative and qualitative metrics are universally applied in the Basic Control Process:
- **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**
- **Variance (Δ)** = Actual Performance − Standard Performance
- **Tolerance Limits / Critical Limits**
- **Budgeted vs. Actual figures**
- **Standard Cost vs. Actual Cost**
- **Critical Ratio Check**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of the Basic Control Process — Step-by-Step Logic

#### **Step 1: Setting Performance Standards**
- Standards are the **predetermined benchmarks** against which actual performance will be measured.
- They must be **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound (SMART)**.
- Examples: production targets (units/day), sales targets (₹/month), quality standards (% rejection rate), cost standards (₹/unit).
- Standards can be expressed in **quantitative terms** (numerical targets) or **qualitative terms** (behavioral/intangible benchmarks).

#### **Step 2: Measuring Actual Performance**
- This is the **data acquisition phase** where real outcomes are recorded, observed, or measured.
- Measurement methods include: personal observation, statistical reports, oral reports, written reports, computer-generated MIS outputs.
- Measurement must be **accurate, timely, and objective** — otherwise the entire control loop becomes unreliable.

#### **Step 3: Comparing Performance with Standards (Variance Analysis)**
- Actual performance is **compared** with the standard.
- The difference between actual and standard is called the **Variance (Δ)**.
- If variance lies **within tolerance limits** → no action required.
- If variance lies **outside tolerance limits** → it is a **significant deviation** requiring managerial intervention.

#### **Step 4: Taking Corrective Action**
- The manager removes the **root cause** of the deviation.
- Two outcomes are possible:
  (a) Corrective action succeeds → performance is restored to standard.
  (b) Standards were unrealistic → revise the standards themselves and begin the cycle anew.
- The process is then **restarted** to ensure continuous control.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Underlying Logic

- **Why set standards first?** Because without a benchmark, measurement has no reference point. Control without standards is meaningless.
- **Why measure?** Because managers cannot act on assumptions. They need empirical data.
- **Why compare?** Because the gap between *what is* and *what should be* is the only meaningful signal for action.
- **Why correct?** Because deviations, if ignored, compound over time and erode organizational effectiveness.

### 2.3 Types of Standards Used in Practice
| Type of Standard | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Standards** | Tangible, measurable in units | 500 units/day, 2 kg/batch |
| **Cost Standards** | Monetary benchmarks | ₹150/unit production cost |
| **Revenue Standards** | Income benchmarks | ₹50 lakh sales/month |
| **Capital Standards** | Investment benchmarks | ROI ≥ 15% |
| **Program Standards** | Non-monetary operational checks | Number of training sessions |
| **Intangible Standards** | Qualitative / behavioral benchmarks | Employee morale, goodwill |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every numerical control question in KTU is built from these relations.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Variance | $\Delta = P_{a} - P_{s}$ | $P_{a}$ = Actual Performance, $P_{s}$ = Standard Performance | Same as performance unit |
| 2 | Percentage Variance | $\%\Delta = \left( \dfrac{P_{a} - P_{s}}{P_{s}} \right) \times 100$ | Same as above | % |
| 3 | Standard Costing Variance | $V_{c} = AC - SC$ | $AC$ = Actual Cost, $SC$ = Standard Cost | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 4 | Favourable vs Unfavourable | $V > 0 \Rightarrow$ Favourable (revenue), Unfavourable (cost) | Sign convention depends on parameter | — |
| 5 | Critical Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Remaining Time}}{\text{Time Needed to Complete}}$ | Time management metric | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Productivity Index | $PI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Standard Output}}$ | Performance efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Efficiency Ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Standard Hours Allowed}}{\text{Actual Hours Taken}} \times 100$ | Workforce efficiency | % |
| 8 | Budgetary Variance | $V_{b} = \text{Budgeted Figure} - \text{Actual Figure}$ | Deviation from budget | ₹ / units |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
In engineering organizations, the Basic Control Process is operationalized as:
- **Quality Control (QC)** — Six Sigma, TQM dashboards.
- **Project Control** — Earned Value Management (EVM), Gantt charts.
- **Production Control** — Just-In-Time (JIT), inventory variance tracking.
- **Financial Control** — Budgetary control, standard costing systems.
- **Safety Control** — Incident tracking, hazard deviation reports.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Percentage Variance Formula

**Starting Premise:** In the comparison step of the control process, the manager must quantify the deviation between actual performance and the pre-set standard.

Let:
- $P_{a}$ = Actual Performance achieved during the period.
- $P_{s}$ = Standard Performance (the benchmark) for the period.

**Step 1 — Absolute Variance:**
By definition, variance is the difference between what is and what should be.

$$
\Delta = P_{a} - P_{s}
$$

*Logic:* We subtract the standard from the actual because a positive result means actual exceeds standard (favourable for output, unfavourable for cost) and vice versa.

**Step 2 — Normalising to a Relative Scale:**
A raw absolute variance is hard to compare across departments of different sizes. We express it as a *fraction of the standard* to get a normalized index.

$$
\text{Fractional Variance} = \dfrac{P_{a} - P_{s}}{P_{s}}
$$

*Logic:* Dividing by $P_{s}$ removes unit-scale dependency, making the metric comparable.

**Step 3 — Converting to Percentage:**
Multiply by **100** to express as a percentage (the industry convention).

$$
\%\Delta = \left( \dfrac{P_{a} - P_{s}}{P_{s}} \right) \times 100
$$

*Logic:* Percentages are dimensionless and universally interpretable across all industries.

**Step 4 — Sign Convention Rule:**
$$
\%\Delta > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Favourable for Output / Revenue}
$$
$$
\%\Delta < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Unfavourable for Output / Revenue}
$$

For cost standards, the sign convention reverses.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A manufacturing unit sets a *standard production target* of **1,000 units/day**. In a particular week, the actual daily averages achieved were: Day 1 = 980, Day 2 = 1,050, Day 3 = 920, Day 4 = 1,100, Day 5 = 1,030, Day 6 = 970, Day 7 = 1,000. Calculate the **total absolute variance**, **average absolute variance**, and **percentage variance** for the week. Identify whether corrective action is justified.

**Step 1 — Average Actual Performance:**

$$
P_{a} = \dfrac{980 + 1050 + 920 + 1100 + 1030 + 970 + 1000}{7}
$$

$$
P_{a} = \dfrac{7050}{7} = 1007.143 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Absolute Variance:**

$$
\Delta = P_{a} - P_{s} = 1007.143 - 1000 = +7.143 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Variance:**

$$
\%\Delta = \left( \dfrac{1007.143 - 1000}{1000} \right) \times 100 = +0.7143\%
$$

**Step 4 — Day-wise Variance Analysis (Critical Variances):**

$$
\Delta_{Day3} = 920 - 1000 = -80 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\Delta_{Day4} = 1100 - 1000 = +100 \text{ units}
$$

*Interpretation:* Day 3 and Day 4 show large deviations — these require **root-cause analysis**, even though the weekly average masks them.

**Step 5 — Managerial Conclusion:**
The weekly average deviation is only +0.71 %, which is well within typical tolerance limits (±5 %). However, the day-wise volatility suggests **process instability**. Corrective action is justified to stabilize daily output, even though the weekly aggregate is acceptable.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Variance Magnitude ($\vert\%\Delta\vert$) | Managerial Action |
|---|---|
| 0 – 2 % | No action required; within normal range |
| 2 – 5 % | Monitor closely; identify trends |
| 5 – 10 % | Investigate root cause; minor corrective action |
| > 10 % | Immediate intervention; major corrective action or standards revision |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Basic Control Process — Closed-Loop Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical four-step closed-loop control cycle used universally in KTU management textbooks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STEP 1: SET STANDARDS"] --> B["STEP 2: MEASURE ACTUAL PERFORMANCE"]
    B --> C["STEP 3: COMPARE WITH STANDARDS"]
    C --> D{"DEVIATION WITHIN LIMITS?"}
    D -- YES --> E["NO ACTION - CONTINUE MONITORING"]
    D -- NO --> F["STEP 4: TAKE CORRECTIVE ACTION"]
    F --> G["PERFORMANCE RESTORED"]
    G --> A
    E --> A
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["PLANNED OBJECTIVES"]
        I2["STANDARDS AND BUDGETS"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING_LAYER["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1["DATA ACQUISITION MODULE"]
        P2["VARIANCE COMPUTATION UNIT"]
        P3["DECISION LOGIC GATE"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["CORRECTIVE ACTION SIGNAL"]
        O2["STANDARDS REVISION SIGNAL"]
        O3["FEEDBACK TO PLANNING"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    O3 --> I1
```

### 4.3 Variance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["MEASURED ACTUAL PERFORMANCE"] --> N["SUBTRACT STANDARD"]
    N --> O["COMPUTE PERCENT VARIANCE"]
    O --> P{"IS VARIANCE NEGLIGIBLE?"}
    P -- YES --> Q["CLOSE LOOP - NO ACTION"]
    P -- NO --> R{"IS STANDARD ACHIEVABLE?"}
    R -- YES --> S["CORRECT PERFORMANCE GAP"]
    R -- NO --> T["REVISE THE STANDARD"]
    S --> Q
    T --> Q
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **"Define the term 'Controlling' in management. List any four characteristics of the Basic Control Process."**

**Model Answer:**
Controlling is the managerial function of monitoring performance, comparing it with standards, and taking corrective action to ensure goal attainment.

Four characteristics:
1. **Goal-oriented** — always directed toward achieving pre-set objectives.
2. **Continuous and cyclical** — operates as an unbroken loop.
3. **Universal** — applicable at all levels and in all departments.
4. **Forward-looking** — focuses on preventing future deviations, not just correcting past ones.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **"Distinguish between 'Standards' and 'Objectives' in the context of management control."**

**Model Answer:**
| Basis | Standards | Objectives |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Specific quantitative / qualitative benchmarks | Broader organizational goals |
| Scope | Narrow, measurable, time-bound | Wide, sometimes abstract |
| Role in Control | Acts as the *yardstick* of measurement | Acts as the *destination* of effort |
| Example | Produce 500 units/day | Increase market share by 10% |
| Flexibility | Revised if unrealistic | Long-term and stable |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** Explain the **four sequential steps of the Basic Control Process** with a suitable industrial example. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** A production unit has a *standard output* of **800 units/day**. The actual output for five days is: 760, 820, 880, 740, 850. Calculate the **percentage variance** for each day and recommend whether corrective action is needed. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The Basic Control Process consists of four sequential steps forming a closed loop:

**Step 1 — Setting Standards:**
The first step is establishing clear performance benchmarks. In an automobile assembly plant, the standard may be "produce 200 cars per shift with a defect rate below 2%." Standards must be SMART — specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound.

**Step 2 — Measuring Actual Performance:**
The plant manager records actual cars produced per shift and counts defects. Data is collected via shop-floor MIS, supervisor reports, and automated sensors.

**Step 3 — Comparing Performance with Standards:**
At end-of-shift, actual figures are compared with the standard. If 195 cars are produced with 1.5% defects, variance = -5 cars and -0.5% defects — within tolerance.

**Step 4 — Taking Corrective Action:**
If defects exceed 5%, the manager investigates the root cause (e.g., machine wear, operator skill), and takes action — recalibrating machines, retraining workers, or revising the standard if unrealistic. The cycle then repeats.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Given: $P_{s} = 800$ units/day for all 5 days.

| Day | $P_{a}$ | $\Delta = P_{a} - P_{s}$ | $\%\Delta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 760 | −40 | −5.00% |
| 2 | 820 | +20 | +2.50% |
| 3 | 880 | +80 | +10.00% |
| 4 | 740 | −60 | −7.50% |
| 5 | 850 | +50 | +6.25% |

Applying the Analytical Decision Matrix (Section 3.3):
- Day 1: −5% → investigate; minor corrective action.
- Day 2: +2.5% → monitor closely.
- Day 3: +10% → immediate intervention required.
- Day 4: −7.5% → immediate intervention required.
- Day 5: +6.25% → investigate root cause.

**Recommendation:** Days 3, 4, and 5 fall outside acceptable tolerance — the manager must immediately investigate causes (machine downtime, material shortage, absenteeism) and initiate corrective action.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Alternate Choice]**
> **(a)** Discuss the **prerequisites (requirements) of an effective control system** in an organization. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** What is **Variance Analysis**? A company has a *standard cost of ₹200/unit* and *actual cost of ₹230/unit*. Compute the cost variance and classify it as favourable or unfavourable with justification. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

For a control system to be effective, the following prerequisites must be satisfied:
1. **Clear, realistic, and measurable standards.**
2. **Accurate and timely measurement of performance.**
3. **Quick reporting and feedback mechanism.**
4. **Flexibility to adapt to changing conditions.**
5. **Economy — cost of control should not exceed its benefits.**
6. **Acceptability by employees** to ensure cooperation.
7. **Strategic placement of control points** at critical activities.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Variance Analysis is the quantitative technique used in the comparison step of control to compute the deviation between actual and standard figures.

Given: $SC = 200$, $AC = 230$.

$$
V_{c} = AC - SC = 230 - 200 = +30 \text{ rupees per unit}
$$

**Classification:** This is an **UNFAVOURABLE variance** because actual cost exceeds standard cost — meaning the company is spending ₹30 more per unit than planned, which erodes profit margins. The manager must investigate the cause (material price rise, inefficiency, wastage).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors** — Students often write "favourable" for any positive variance. *Always check whether the parameter is cost or revenue.* Positive cost variance = UNFAVOURABLE.
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize** — Reporting raw variance without computing % variance loses marks.
> 3. **Listing steps without examples** — KTU examiners award marks for *application*; every step must be supported by a real-world illustration.
> 4. **Skipping the cyclical nature** — Always emphasize that the process is a *closed loop* restarting after corrective action.
> 5. **Mixing up standards and objectives** — These are distinct concepts; examiners often test this distinction.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Control is the **fourth managerial function** — sequential after Planning → Organizing → Directing → Controlling.
- ✅ The Basic Control Process has **exactly four steps**: Set Standards → Measure Performance → Compare → Correct.
- ✅ The process is **cyclical, continuous, and forward-looking**.
- ✅ Variance = Actual − Standard; **Percentage Variance** = $(P_{a} - P_{s})/P_{s} \times 100$.
- ✅ Positive cost variance = **UNFAVOURABLE**; positive revenue variance = **FAVOURABLE** (sign reversal is critical).
- ✅ Within tolerance → no action; outside tolerance → corrective action OR standards revision.
- ✅ Effective control requires **SMART standards, timely measurement, accurate comparison, and quick corrective action**.
- ✅ Tolerance limit benchmark (commonly used): 0–2% no action, 2–5% monitor, 5–10% investigate, >10% immediate intervention.
- ✅ Three prerequisites students forget: *economy of control*, *flexibility*, and *employee acceptability*.
- ✅ In KTU board answers, always illustrate with **at least one industrial example** to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Making: Steps, Decision Under Certainty, Risk and Uncertainty
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET504 - MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decision Making: Steps, Decision Under Certainty, Risk and Uncertainty

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Decision Making?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Robbins & Coulter):**
> *Decision Making is the process of selecting a course of action from two or more alternatives to achieve a desired goal. It is the essence of management, as it involves choosing the most optimal path among several available options under given constraints and environmental conditions.*

In management science, every decision is essentially a commitment of organizational resources (time, money, manpower) toward a future course of action whose outcome is not always fully predictable. The **decision-maker (DM)** is the individual or group responsible for selecting the alternative that best aligns with organizational objectives.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Crossroads of an Engineer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine you are a civil engineer standing at a crossroad with three possible routes to a destination:
> - **Route A** is a well-paved highway (you know exactly how long it takes — *Certainty*).
> - **Route B** is a road where you know it rains **30% of the time** causing delays (you have probabilities — *Risk*).
> - **Route C** is a brand-new GPS route about which you have **no prior information** at all (pure guesswork — *Uncertainty*).
>
> Decision-making theory gives you a structured, mathematical way to choose which road to take in every case.

### 1.3 Classification of Decision Environments (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The state of the environment or **"State of Nature"** is the uncontrollable condition under which a decision is made. KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes decisions based on the **knowledge of these states of nature**:

| Type of Decision | Knowledge of Outcome | Knowledge of Probability | Decision Environment |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Certainty** | Known with 100% accuracy | Not required (only one state) | Deterministic |
| **Risk** | Outcome is variable but known | Probabilities are known/estimable | Probabilistic / Stochastic |
| **Uncertainty** | Outcome is variable and unknown | Probabilities are NOT known | Indeterminate |

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Exams

This is one of the **highest-weightage analytical topics** in Module 2. You will be expected to:
1. Draw payoff tables and decision trees.
2. Compute **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)**, **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)**, and **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)**.
3. Apply decision criteria under uncertainty: **Maximax, Maxmin (Minimax), Minimax Regret, Hurwicz, Laplace (Equal Likelihood), and Savage**.

These computations form the backbone of quantitative management questions in both **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Rational Decision-Making Process (6 Steps — KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> The **six-step rational model** is the most frequently asked 7-mark sub-question. Memorize this sequence verbatim for the KTU exam.

**Step 1 — Identify the Problem / Recognize the Need for a Decision**
- A problem is the gap between the *current state* and the *desired state*.
- The trigger may be internal (declining sales) or external (new regulation).
- Without a clear problem statement, the entire decision will be misdirected.

**Step 2 — Identify Decision Criteria / Objectives**
- List the factors that are *relevant* to the decision: cost, quality, time, risk, ROI, CSR.
- These criteria act as the **rulers** used to evaluate each alternative.

**Step 3 — Allocate Weights to Criteria**
- Not all criteria are equally important. Assign weights (e.g., Cost = 0.5, Quality = 0.3, Speed = 0.2).
- Weights must sum to **1.0** (or 100%).
- A common KTU technique: **Weighted Scoring Model**.

**Step 4 — Generate Alternatives (Courses of Action)**
- Brainstorm all feasible alternatives.
- Do NOT pre-judge or eliminate options prematurely — even bad-sounding ideas can lead to hybrid solutions.

**Step 5 — Evaluate Each Alternative Against the Criteria**
- Score each alternative on each criterion (often on a 1–10 scale).
- Multiply by weight, sum the products, and obtain a **Total Weighted Score**.

**Step 6 — Select the Optimal Alternative**
- The alternative with the **highest total weighted score** is chosen.
- Implement, monitor, and feed back results.

---

### 2.2 Decision Under Certainty

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A decision is made under **certainty** when the decision-maker has *complete and perfect knowledge* of the outcome of every alternative. Each action leads to **one and only one** state of nature.

**Characteristics:**
- Single, deterministic outcome per alternative.
- Best solved using **Linear Programming (LP)**, **Break-Even Analysis**, or simple arithmetic comparison.
- Probability is effectively **1.0** for the known state.

**Example:** Investing a fixed deposit of ₹1,00,000 at a guaranteed 7% interest rate yields ₹7,000. There is no ambiguity.

**KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Most "decision under certainty" problems reduce to a simple maximization/minimization of a single value. The examiner expects you to *identify* the best alternative from a table where each row has only one fixed value.

---

### 2.3 Decision Under Risk

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A decision is made under **risk** when the decision-maker *cannot predict* the actual outcome, but can assign a **probability distribution** to the possible states of nature. Probabilities may be derived from historical data, market research, or expert judgment.

**The Payoff Table (Decision Matrix):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Structure: } & \text{Rows} = \text{Alternatives } (a_1, a_2, \dots, a_m) \\
& \text{Columns} = \text{States of Nature } (S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n) \\
& \text{Cells} = \text{Payoff} = V(a_i, S_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

Payoff = Profit, Cost, or any other metric, depending on the problem.

**Core Risk Decision Criteria (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet):**

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the following 6 formulas. They will be tested directly or as part of a 14-mark problem.

| # | Criterion | Formula | Logic |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | $EMV(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot V(a_i, S_j)$ | Average payoff weighted by probabilities. Pick **Max EMV** (profit) or **Min EMV** (cost). |
| 2 | **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)** | $EOL(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot R(a_i, S_j)$ | Average of regret values. Pick **Min EOL**. |
| 3 | **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)** | $EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^*$ | Maximum price one should pay for perfect information. |
| 4 | **Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI)** | $EVwPI = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot V^{*}(S_j)$ | Best payoff in each state, then expected. |
| 5 | **Regret (Opportunity Loss)** | $R(a_i, S_j) = \vert V^{*}(S_j) - V(a_i, S_j) \vert$ | Difference between best payoff in column $j$ and the actual cell value. |
| 6 | **Coefficient of Realism (Hurwicz)** | $H(a_i) = \alpha \cdot V_{\max} + (1-\alpha) \cdot V_{\min}$ | Weighted blend of best and worst outcomes. $\alpha$ = optimism index. |

**Step-by-Step Computation Logic:**
1. Construct the payoff table with alternatives as rows and states of nature as columns.
2. Multiply each cell value by its column probability and **sum across the row** to get EMV.
3. Choose the alternative with the **highest EMV** (or lowest if it is a cost problem).
4. To compute EOL, first create a **Regret Table** by subtracting each cell from the maximum in its column.
5. Multiply regret values by the same probabilities and pick the alternative with the **lowest EOL**.
6. Note: The optimal EMV and optimal EOL will *always* point to the **same alternative**.

**Decision Tree Visualization (verbal description):**
- A square node = **Decision Node** (controlled by the DM).
- A circle node = **Chance Node** (state of nature, with branches showing probabilities).
- Triangle = **End node** (final payoff).

---

### 2.4 Decision Under Uncertainty

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A decision is made under **uncertainty** when the decision-maker *cannot* estimate the probabilities of the various states of nature. Only the payoffs are known; nothing else.

**Six Classical Criteria (KTU Mandatory List):**

| # | Criterion | Alternative Name | Action |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Maximax** (Optimistic) | Best of the Best | Pick the alternative with the **maximum** of the maximum row payoffs. |
| 2 | **Maxmin** / **Minimin** (Pessimistic) | Wald's Criterion | Pick the alternative with the **maximum** of the minimum row payoffs (best of the worst). |
| 3 | **Minimax Regret** (Savage) | Opportunity Loss | Pick the alternative with the **minimum** of the maximum regret values. |
| 4 | **Hurwicz (Realism)** | Weighted Optimism | $H = \alpha (\max) + (1-\alpha)(\min)$, pick **max H**. $\alpha$ is given. |
| 5 | **Laplace (Equal Likelihood)** | Principle of Insufficient Reason | Assign $P = 1/n$ to each state. Compute average row payoff; pick the best. |
| 6 | **Minimax** for cost problems | Pessimistic cost | Pick the alternative with the **minimum** of the **maximum** row costs. |

**Why Six Different Criteria?**
Because no single rule is universally correct. Each criterion embodies a different **attitude toward risk**:
- **Optimist** → Maximax / high $\alpha$
- **Pessimist** → Maxmin / low $\alpha$ / Minimax Regret
- **Rationalist (insufficient reason)** → Laplace
- **Balanced** → Hurwicz

The KTU examiner often supplies a payoff table and asks: *"Apply all the criteria and recommend the best alternative."* — worth 7 marks.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Areas:**
> - **Civil Engineering:** Choosing a construction site when soil conditions are only probabilistically known.
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Selecting a manufacturing process (CNC vs. 3D-printing) under demand uncertainty.
> - **Computer Science / IT:** Investing in either AWS, Azure, or GCP given an unknown future cloud market share.
> - **Project Management:** Using **EMV analysis in PERT** to compute expected project duration: $T_E = (O + 4M + P)/6$.
> - **Finance:** Portfolio selection under market volatility.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Decision Under Risk (Full EMV + EOL + EVPI Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Standard Format):**
> A company must choose between three products A, B, and C to launch. Demand can be High (H), Medium (M), or Low (L) with probabilities **0.3, 0.5, 0.2** respectively. The payoffs (₹ in lakhs profit) are:

|  | High (0.3) | Medium (0.5) | Low (0.2) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **A** | 200 | 150 | 80 |
| **B** | 250 | 120 | 60 |
| **C** | 180 | 140 | 100 |

**Step 1 — Compute EMV for each alternative:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(A) &= (0.3)(200) + (0.5)(150) + (0.2)(80) \\
&= 60 + 75 + 16 \\
&= 151 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(B) &= (0.3)(250) + (0.5)(120) + (0.2)(60) \\
&= 75 + 60 + 12 \\
&= 147 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(C) &= (0.3)(180) + (0.5)(140) + (0.2)(100) \\
&= 54 + 70 + 20 \\
&= 144 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision (EMV):** $EMV^* = \max(151, 147, 144) = 151$ → **Choose Product A**.

---

**Step 2 — Construct the Regret Table:**

For each column, the maximum payoff is identified, and the regret = max − cell value.

- Column High: max = 250 (B) → Regret(A,H) = 250−200 = 50, Regret(B,H) = 0, Regret(C,H) = 250−180 = 70
- Column Medium: max = 150 (A) → Regret(A,M) = 0, Regret(B,M) = 150−120 = 30, Regret(C,M) = 150−140 = 10
- Column Low: max = 100 (C) → Regret(A,L) = 100−80 = 20, Regret(B,L) = 100−60 = 40, Regret(C,L) = 0

| Regret | High (0.3) | Medium (0.5) | Low (0.2) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **A** | 50 | 0 | 20 |
| **B** | 0 | 30 | 40 |
| **C** | 70 | 10 | 0 |

---

**Step 3 — Compute EOL for each alternative:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(A) &= (0.3)(50) + (0.5)(0) + (0.2)(20) \\
&= 15 + 0 + 4 = 19 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(B) &= (0.3)(0) + (0.5)(30) + (0.2)(40) \\
&= 0 + 15 + 8 = 23 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(C) &= (0.3)(70) + (0.5)(10) + (0.2)(0) \\
&= 21 + 5 + 0 = 26 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision (EOL):** $EOL^* = \min(19, 23, 26) = 19$ → **Choose Product A**.

✅ **Verification:** Both EMV and EOL criteria confirm **Product A is optimal**. This is mathematically guaranteed when computed correctly.

---

**Step 4 — Compute EVPI:**

First, find the **best payoff in each state** (column maximum):

- High → 250 (B), Medium → 150 (A), Low → 100 (C)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVwPI &= (0.3)(250) + (0.5)(150) + (0.2)(100) \\
&= 75 + 75 + 20 \\
&= 170 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVPI &= EVwPI - EMV^* \\
&= 170 - 151 \\
&= 19 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company should be willing to pay **at most ₹19 lakhs** to a market research firm to obtain perfect demand information. Paying more than this is irrational.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Decision Under Uncertainty (All Six Criteria)

> [!NOTE]
> **Same Payoff Table, but Probabilities are NOT given:**

|  | State 1 | State 2 | State 3 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **A** | 200 | 150 | 80 |
| **B** | 250 | 120 | 60 |
| **C** | 180 | 140 | 100 |

**(1) Maximax (Optimistic):**
- Row maxes: A=200, B=250, C=180 → **Pick B (250)**

**(2) Maxmin / Wald (Pessimistic):**
- Row mins: A=80, B=60, C=100 → **Pick C (100)**

**(3) Minimax Regret (Savage):**
- Use the same regret table as above (since payoffs are the same):
- Row max regret: A=50, B=40, C=70 → **Pick B (min=40)**

**(4) Hurwicz (α = 0.7, optimism index):**
- $H(A) = 0.7(200) + 0.3(80) = 140 + 24 = 164$
- $H(B) = 0.7(250) + 0.3(60) = 175 + 18 = 193$
- $H(C) = 0.7(180) + 0.3(100) = 126 + 30 = 156$ → **Pick B (193)**

**(5) Laplace (Equal Likelihood, P = 1/3):**
- Avg(A) = (200+150+80)/3 = 430/3 ≈ 143.33
- Avg(B) = (250+120+60)/3 = 430/3 ≈ 143.33
- Avg(C) = (180+140+100)/3 = 420/3 = 140 → **Tie between A and B (143.33)**

**(6) Minimax (Cost problems; not applicable here as it is a profit table).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the conflict:** Optimistic, Regret, and Hurwicz criteria all pick **B**, while Pessimistic and Laplace pick **A or C**. This is a *classic KTU exam insight* — different criteria yield different answers because they encode different risk attitudes. The final answer depends on the **DM's risk appetite**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Condition | Recommended Criterion | Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Probabilities are known and reliable | **EMV / EOL** | Mathematically optimal for rational, risk-neutral DMs. |
| DM is highly optimistic | **Maximax** or high-$\alpha$ Hurwicz | Captures best-case thinking. |
| DM is highly risk-averse | **Maxmin / Minimax Regret** | Protects against worst loss. |
| DM is balanced | **Hurwicz (α = 0.5)** | Equal weight to best and worst. |
| No basis for any probability estimate | **Laplace** | Assumes fair coin (equal likelihood). |
| Outcome of a high-stakes one-time event | **Minimax Regret** | Minimizes potential post-decision regret. |
| Costs (not profits) given | Use **Min variants** | Replace all "max" with "min" and vice versa. |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**Q1. Differentiate between decision making under certainty and decision making under uncertainty. (2 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Certainty | Uncertainty |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Knowledge of outcome | Complete, single outcome | Multiple possible outcomes, unknown which will occur |
| Probabilities | Not needed (only one state) | Unknown / cannot be assigned |
| Decision rule | Simple comparison of alternatives | Heuristic criteria (Maximax, Maxmin, Laplace, etc.) |
| Example | Fixed deposit interest | New product launch with no market data |

---

**Q2. Define Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI). Why is it significant? (2 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** $EVPI$ is the maximum amount a decision-maker should be willing to pay to obtain *perfect* (100% accurate) information about which state of nature will actually occur.

**Significance:**
- It sets the **upper limit** for the budget of any market research or forecasting activity.
- It is computed as: $EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^*$, where $EVwPI$ is the expected value with perfect information.
- If a research firm quotes a price higher than EVPI, the firm should *not* hire them.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **six-step rational decision-making process** with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The rational decision-making model, propounded by management scholars, provides a systematic, step-by-step framework that minimizes bias and emotion. It comprises six stages:

**Step 1 — Identify the Problem:**
The decision-making process begins with a clear recognition that a gap exists between the current state and the desired state. A manager in a manufacturing firm, for instance, may identify declining product quality as the problem.

**Step 2 — Identify Decision Criteria:**
The decision-maker lists all the factors that are relevant to resolving the problem. For example, when choosing a new supplier, criteria may include cost, quality, reliability, and delivery time.

**Step 3 — Allocate Weights to Criteria:**
Since criteria are not equally important, weights are assigned such that the total equals 1.0. For instance, in a cost-sensitive project, cost may receive a weight of 0.5, while reliability receives 0.3 and speed 0.2.

**Step 4 — Generate Alternatives:**
The next step is to brainstorm all feasible courses of action. The manager should generate at least three to five realistic alternatives to avoid premature elimination of options.

**Step 5 — Evaluate Each Alternative:**
Each alternative is scored on every criterion, multiplied by the corresponding weight, and summed to obtain a total weighted score. This is the **Weighted Scoring Model**.

**Step 6 — Select the Optimal Alternative:**
The alternative with the highest total weighted score is implemented. Post-implementation, the manager should monitor results and create feedback loops for continuous improvement.

---

**(b)** A firm must choose among three investment plans. Returns (₹ in thousands) depend on the economy, with the following payoff table:

| Plan | Boom (P=0.4) | Stable (P=0.4) | Recession (P=0.2) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **P1** | 100 | 80 | 20 |
| **P2** | 90 | 90 | 30 |
| **P3** | 70 | 60 | 50 |

Compute **(i) EMV**, **(ii) EOL**, and **(iii) EVPI**. Identify the optimal plan. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Expected Monetary Value (EMV):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV(P1) &= (0.4)(100) + (0.4)(80) + (0.2)(20) \\
&= 40 + 32 + 4 = 76 \\
EMV(P2) &= (0.4)(90) + (0.4)(90) + (0.2)(30) \\
&= 36 + 36 + 6 = 78 \\
EMV(P3) &= (0.4)(70) + (0.4)(60) + (0.2)(50) \\
&= 28 + 24 + 10 = 62
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $EMV^* = \max(76, 78, 62) = 78$ → **Choose Plan P2.**

---

**Regret Table Construction:**
- Column Boom max = 100 → Regrets: P1=0, P2=10, P3=30
- Column Stable max = 90 → Regrets: P1=10, P2=0, P3=30
- Column Recession max = 50 → Regrets: P1=30, P2=20, P3=0

**(ii) Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(P1) &= (0.4)(0) + (0.4)(10) + (0.2)(30) = 0 + 4 + 6 = 10 \\
EOL(P2) &= (0.4)(10) + (0.4)(0) + (0.2)(20) = 4 + 0 + 4 = 8 \\
EOL(P3) &= (0.4)(30) + (0.4)(30) + (0.2)(0) = 12 + 12 + 0 = 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $EOL^* = \min(10, 8, 24) = 8$ → **Choose Plan P2** ✅ (confirms EMV).

---

**(iii) Expected Value of Perfect Information:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EVwPI &= (0.4)(100) + (0.4)(90) + (0.2)(50) \\
&= 40 + 36 + 10 = 86 \\
EVPI &= EVwPI - EMV^* = 86 - 78 = 8 \text{ thousand rupees}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The firm should pay a maximum of **₹8,000** for perfect economic forecasting.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A startup must choose between three marketing strategies with the following payoff table. The probabilities of demand are not known. Apply **Maximax, Maxmin, Minimax Regret, and Hurwicz (α = 0.6)** criteria. Recommend the best strategy. **(7 Marks)**

| Strategy | High Demand | Moderate Demand | Low Demand |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **S1** | 220 | 160 | 90 |
| **S2** | 280 | 140 | 50 |
| **S3** | 200 | 150 | 110 |

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Maximax (Optimistic):**
- Row maxes: S1 = 220, S2 = 280, S3 = 200 → **Pick S2 (280)**

**(2) Maxmin (Pessimistic / Wald):**
- Row mins: S1 = 90, S2 = 50, S3 = 110 → **Pick S3 (110)**

**(3) Minimax Regret (Savage):**

Regret Table:
- Col High max = 280 → Regrets: S1=60, S2=0, S3=80
- Col Moderate max = 160 → Regrets: S1=0, S2=20, S3=10
- Col Low max = 110 → Regrets: S1=20, S2=60, S3=0

Row max regrets: S1=60, S2=60, S3=80 → **Tie S1 and S2 (min=60)**. (Choose any by tie-breaking; convention favors S1 if listed first.)

**(4) Hurwicz (α = 0.6):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S1) &= 0.6(220) + 0.4(90) = 132 + 36 = 168 \\
H(S2) &= 0.6(280) + 0.4(50) = 168 + 20 = 188 \\
H(S3) &= 0.6(200) + 0.4(110) = 120 + 44 = 164
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pick S2 (188)** — most optimistic criterion with α = 0.6.

**Recommendation:** **S2** is recommended by Maximax and Hurwicz — it suits a moderately optimistic decision-maker.

---

**(b)** Define **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** and **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)**. Prove that **EMV and EOL always lead to the same optimal decision.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of EMV:** $EMV(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot V(a_i, S_j)$
EMV is the probability-weighted average payoff of an alternative across all possible states of nature.

**Definition of EOL:** $EOL(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot R(a_i, S_j)$
EOL is the probability-weighted average of the regret (opportunity loss) values.

**Proof of Equivalence:**

Let $V_j^* = \max_i V(a_i, S_j)$ = best payoff in state $j$. The regret is:
$R(a_i, S_j) = V_j^* - V(a_i, S_j)$.

Since the probabilities sum to unity ($\sum P(S_j) = 1$), the expected value of $V_j^*$ across all states is a constant $K$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOL(a_i) &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot \left[V_j^* - V(a_i, S_j)\right] \\
&= \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot V_j^* - \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(S_j) \cdot V(a_i, S_j) \\
&= K - EMV(a_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K = \sum_{j} P(S_j) V_j^*$ is a constant (independent of $i$).

Therefore, **minimizing EOL is mathematically equivalent to maximizing EMV**, since subtracting from a constant reverses the order. Hence, both criteria select the **same optimal alternative.** ∎

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract from column maximum when building the regret table.** Regret is *never* computed as the cell value minus the column max — it is the *column max minus the cell value*. This is the #1 mistake.
> 2. **Confusing Maximax with Maxmin.** Maximax = pick the *largest* of the *row maxima*. Maxmin = pick the *largest* of the *row minima*. Do not reverse them.
> 3. **In Hurwicz, using $\alpha$ on the wrong term.** If the question says "optimism index = 0.6," then $H = 0.6 \cdot (\max) + 0.4 \cdot (\min)$, NOT the reverse.
> 4. **Reporting the wrong EVPI sign.** EVPI is always non-negative ($EVwPI \geq EMV^*$). A negative value signals an arithmetic error.
> 5. **Omitting units (₹ in lakhs, thousands, etc.)** in the final answer. KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 6. **For Laplace to be a 7-mark question, show the calculation of the average for each row** — not just the final values.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Decision Making = selection of the best course of action from ≥ 2 alternatives.
- ✅ Six-step process: Identify Problem → Criteria → Weights → Alternatives → Evaluate → Select.
- ✅ **Certainty** = single known outcome; **Risk** = probabilities known; **Uncertainty** = probabilities unknown.
- ✅ **EMV** = $\sum P(S_j) \cdot V(a_i, S_j)$ → Pick **max EMV** (profit) or **min EMV** (cost).
- ✅ **EOL** = $\sum P(S_j) \cdot R(a_i, S_j)$ → Pick **min EOL**.
- ✅ **EVPI** = $EVwPI - EMV^*$, where $EVwPI = \sum P(S_j) \cdot V^*(S_j)$.
- ✅ EMV-optimal alternative = EOL-optimal alternative (proof uses the fact that column max is a constant across alternatives).
- ✅ **Six uncertainty criteria:** Maximax, Maxmin, Minimax Regret (Savage), Hurwicz, Laplace, Minimax (cost).
- ✅ **Hurwicz** $H = \alpha \cdot \max + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min$, where $\alpha \in [0,1]$.
- ✅ **Laplace** assumes $P(S_j) = 1/n$ for all $n$ states (Principle of Insufficient Reason).
- ✅ Regret = $|V^*(S_j) - V(a_i, S_j)|$ = column max − cell value.
- ✅ Decision Tree: Square = Decision, Circle = Chance, Triangle = End-payoff.
- ✅ In cost problems, swap "max" with "min" in every criterion.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Decision Making**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Trees, EMV Method, EOL Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET504 - MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Decision Trees, EMV Method, EOL Method ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Decision Tree — Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Tree**: A *graphical, sequential model* used in Operations Research / Management decision-making under conditions of **risk and uncertainty**. It represents the logical sequence of choices (decision nodes) and probabilistic outcomes (chance nodes), culminating in monetary payoffs (end branches). A Decision Tree is a **decision support tool** that uses a **tree-like model of decisions** and their possible consequences, including chance event outcomes, resource costs, and utility. It is one of the most fundamental tools taught in KTU's Management for Engineers (PBMET504) Module 2, directly aligned with **CO2: Apply quantitative decision-making techniques under uncertainty.** The three classical components are: | Node Type | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Decision Node** | ☐ (Square) | A point controlled by the **decision-maker** (e.g., Build / Don't Build) | | **Chance Node** | ○ (Circle) | A point where outcomes are governed by **probability / chance** (e.g., Demand High / Low) | | **End Branch / Leaf** | △ (Triangle) | The terminal payoff or consequence, in monetary units (₹) | ### 1.2 EMV Method — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)**: The *probability-weighted average* of all possible monetary outcomes for a given course of action. The decision-maker chooses the alternative with the **maximum EMV** (for profit problems) or **minimum EMV** (for cost problems). $$\text{EMV}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \cdot V_{ij}$$ Where $P_j$ is the probability of state of nature $j$, and $V_{ij}$ is the payoff of alternative $i$ under state $j$. ### 1.3 EOL Method — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)**, also called **Expected Regret**, measures the *expected difference between the best possible payoff* in each state and the *actual payoff* obtained. The decision-maker chooses the alternative with the **minimum EOL**. $$\text{EOL}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \cdot R_{ij}$$ Where $R_{ij}$ is the *Regret (Opportunity Loss)* of alternative $i$ under state $j$. ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weather, Picnic & Umbrella" > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy**: Imagine you are planning a **picnic tomorrow**. > - **Decision Node** ☐: Do you carry an **umbrella** or **not**? > - **Chance Node** ○: The weather tomorrow — *Rainy* (prob 0.4) or *Sunny* (prob 0.6). > - **Payoff**: Your happiness/utility — wet clothes if no umbrella and rain (bad), heavy bag if umbrella and sunny (bad), perfect if you match the weather. > > The **EMV** is like computing the *average happiness* of each choice. The **EOL** is the *average regret* — how much worse you feel knowing you didn't pick the best option for that weather. You pick the choice that **minimizes regret** (or maximizes expected happiness). A *Decision Tree* simply draws this logic as a flowchart. This is exactly what a manager does for a ₹10 crore investment — just with bigger stakes and more branches. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step by Step The decision-making process under **risk** follows this structured methodology: 1. **Identify the Decision**: Clearly define the choice(s) the manager must make. These become the **alternatives** $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_m$. 2. **Identify the States of Nature**: Enumerate all uncontrollable external conditions. These are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive — e.g., *High Demand, Moderate Demand, Low Demand*. 3. **Assign Probabilities**: Estimate $P(S_j)$ for each state $S_j$ where $\sum_j P(S_j) = 1$. 4. **Construct the Payoff Matrix**: Tabulate the payoff $V_{ij}$ for each $(A_i, S_j)$ pair. 5. **Draw the Decision Tree**: Place the decision node first, branch out to chance nodes, and end with payoffs. 6. **Apply EMV / EOL**: Compute expected values from right (payoffs) to left (decision). 7. **Make the Optimal Choice**: - For **EMV**: choose $\max_i \text{EMV}_i$ (profit) or $\min_i \text{EMV}_i$ (cost). - For **EOL**: choose $\min_i \text{EOL}_i$. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind EMV and EOL - **EMV** is rooted in the **Law of Large Numbers**: if a decision is repeated many times under identical probabilities, the average outcome converges to the EMV. It is **pro-risk neutral**. - **EOL** is rooted in **Regret Theory** (Loomes & Sugden, 1982): people feel pain not just from losses but from *missed opportunities*. A risk-averse decision-maker often prefers EOL analysis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Result**: For any given problem, the optimal decision from **EMV** and **EOL** will **always agree**. Mathematically: > $$\max_i \text{EMV}_i + \min_i \text{EOL}_i = \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}}$$ > where $\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}}$ is the Expected Value with Perfect Information. ### 2.3 Opportunity Loss (Regret) Matrix Construction Given a payoff matrix $V_{ij}$ for $i = 1, \dots, m$ alternatives and $j = 1, \dots, n$ states: For each column (state) $j$, find $\max_i V_{ij}$ (the best payoff achievable in that state). $$R_{ij} = \left( \max_i V_{ij} \right) - V_{ij}$$ So $R_{ij}$ = what you **give up** by not picking the best alternative for state $j$. ### 2.4 EVPI — Expected Value of Perfect Information > [!IMPORTANT] > **EVPI** quantifies the **maximum amount a rational manager should pay** for perfect forecasting information before making the decision. It is a *strategic upper bound* on the value of market research, expert consultation, or pilot studies. $$\text{EVPI} = \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \max_i \text{EMV}_i = \min_i \text{EOL}_i$$ --- ### 📘 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{EMV}_i = \sum_{j} P_j \cdot V_{ij}$ | Expected Monetary Value of alternative $i$ | ₹ (or any currency) | | 2 | $R_{ij} = \max_i V_{ij} - V_{ij}$ | Regret/Opportunity Loss | ₹ | | 3 | $\text{EOL}_i = \sum_{j} P_j \cdot R_{ij}$ | Expected Opportunity Loss | ₹ | | 4 | $\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} = \sum_{j} P_j \cdot \left( \max_i V_{ij} \right)$ | Expected Value with Perfect Information | ₹ | | 5 | $\text{EVPI} = \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \max_i \text{EMV}_i$ | Expected Value of Perfect Information | ₹ | | 6 | $\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}_i$ | Identity link between EMV and EOL | ₹ | | 7 | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j = 1$ | Probability axiom (Total certainty) | Dimensionless | --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Decision Trees, EMV, and EOL are used in: - **Civil Engineering**: Tendering decisions — Bid high vs. Bid low for a construction contract. - **Mechanical Engineering**: Choice of equipment — Buy new CNC machine vs. lease vs. outsource. - **Electrical Engineering**: R&D investment — Invest in solar tech vs. wind tech under uncertain demand. - **Computer Science**: Software project management — In-house development vs. outsourcing. - **Finance / MBA contexts**: Portfolio selection, capital budgeting, product launch decisions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Comprehensive KTU-Style Problem > **Problem (Adapted from KTU Pattern)**: A Kerala-based construction company must decide whether to **build a Large plant**, a **Small plant**, or **do Nothing**. Market research indicates two possible states: > - **Favorable Market**: Probability $P = 0.7$ > - **Unfavorable Market**: Probability $P = 0.3$ > > **Payoff Matrix (₹ Lakhs)** — profit/loss over 5 years: > > | Alternative | Favorable Market | Unfavorable Market | > |:---:|:---:|:---:| > | Build Large Plant | **₹200** | **−₹30** (loss) | > | Build Small Plant | ₹100 | ₹50 | > | Do Nothing | 0 | 0 | > > **Required**: > (a) Construct the Decision Tree. > (b) Compute EMV for each alternative and recommend the optimal decision. > (c) Construct the Opportunity Loss matrix and compute EOL. > (d) Find EVPI and interpret its meaning. --- ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — EMV Calculation **Step 1: Recall the formula.** $$\text{EMV}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \cdot V_{ij}$$ **Step 2: Compute EMV for "Build Large Plant".** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EMV}_{\text{Large}} &= (P_{\text{Fav}} \cdot V_{\text{Large,Fav}}) + (P_{\text{Unfav}} \cdot V_{\text{Large,Unfav}}) \\ &= (0.7 \times 200) + (0.3 \times (-30)) \\ &= 140 + (-9) \\ &= \mathbf{131 \text{ Lakhs}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Compute EMV for "Build Small Plant".** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EMV}_{\text{Small}} &= (P_{\text{Fav}} \cdot V_{\text{Small,Fav}}) + (P_{\text{Unfav}} \cdot V_{\text{Small,Unfav}}) \\ &= (0.7 \times 100) + (0.3 \times 50) \\ &= 70 + 15 \\ &= \mathbf{85 \text{ Lakhs}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Compute EMV for "Do Nothing".** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EMV}_{\text{None}} &= (0.7 \times 0) + (0.3 \times 0) \\ &= \mathbf{0 \text{ Lakhs}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Apply the EMV Decision Rule.** > [!IMPORTANT] > Since this is a **profit problem**, choose the **maximum EMV**. > $$\max(131,\ 85,\ 0) = 131 \text{ Lakhs} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Build the Large Plant}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Opportunity Loss (Regret) Matrix **Step 1: For each state column, find the maximum payoff.** - For *Favorable Market*: $\max(200, 100, 0) = 200$ - For *Unfavorable Market*: $\max(-30, 50, 0) = 50$ **Step 2: Compute Regret** $R_{ij} = \max_i V_{ij} - V_{ij}$. **For "Build Large Plant":** $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{Large, Fav}} &= 200 - 200 = 0 \\ R_{\text{Large, Unfav}} &= 50 - (-30) = 80 \end{aligned}$$ **For "Build Small Plant":** $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{Small, Fav}} &= 200 - 100 = 100 \\ R_{\text{Small, Unfav}} &= 50 - 50 = 0 \end{aligned}$$ **For "Do Nothing":** $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{None, Fav}} &= 200 - 0 = 200 \\ R_{\text{None, Unfav}} &= 50 - 0 = 50 \end{aligned}$$ **Compiled Regret Matrix (₹ Lakhs):** | Alternative | Favorable ($P=0.7$) | Unfavorable ($P=0.3$) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | Build Large Plant | 0 | 80 | | Build Small Plant | 100 | 0 | | Do Nothing | 200 | 50 | --- ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — EOL Calculation **Apply** $\text{EOL}_i = \sum_j P_j \cdot R_{ij}$: **For "Build Large Plant":** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EOL}_{\text{Large}} &= (0.7 \times 0) + (0.3 \times 80) \\ &= 0 + 24 \\ &= \mathbf{24 \text{ Lakhs}} \end{aligned}$$ **For "Build Small Plant":** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EOL}_{\text{Small}} &= (0.7 \times 100) + (0.3 \times 0) \\ &= 70 + 0 \\ &= \mathbf{70 \text{ Lakhs}} \end{aligned}$$ **For "Do Nothing":** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EOL}_{\text{None}} &= (0.7 \times 200) + (0.3 \times 50) \\ &= 140 + 15 \\ &= \mathbf{155 \text{ Lakhs}} \end{aligned}$$ **Decision Rule (EOL):** Choose the alternative with the **minimum EOL**. $$\min(24,\ 70,\ 155) = 24 \text{ Lakhs} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Build the Large Plant}}$$ > [!NOTE] > Both **EMV** and **EOL** independently recommend the **Large Plant**. This consistency is mathematically guaranteed and is a standard verification step expected by KTU examiners. --- ### 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation — EVPI **Step 1: Compute Expected Value with Perfect Information ($\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}}$).** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} &= \sum_j P_j \cdot \left( \max_i V_{ij} \right) \\ &= P_{\text{Fav}} \cdot (\max_i V_{i,\text{Fav}}) + P_{\text{Unfav}} \cdot (\max_i V_{i,\text{Unfav}}) \\ &= (0.7 \times 200) + (0.3 \times 50) \\ &= 140 + 15 \\ &= \mathbf{155 \text{ Lakhs}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Compute EVPI using the EMV route.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EVPI} &= \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \max_i \text{EMV}_i \\ &= 155 - 131 \\ &= \mathbf{24 \text{ Lakhs}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Cross-Verify using the EOL route.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EVPI} &= \min_i \text{EOL}_i \\ &= 24 \text{ Lakhs} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ Both methods yield the same answer — confirming correctness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation**: The company should pay **at most ₹24 Lakhs** for a perfect market-forecast service. Paying more than ₹24 Lakhs would make the forecast *not worth it* — the manager would be better off simply building the Large Plant directly. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Decision Tree — Mermaid Block Diagram The following Mermaid block renders the complete **Decision Tree** for the worked example. Square brackets `[]` denote **Decision Nodes**, and round brackets `()` denote **Chance Nodes**. ```mermaid flowchart TD D1["DECISION NODE
What to do?"] D1 --> A1["Build LARGE Plant"] D1 --> A2["Build SMALL Plant"] D1 --> A3["Do NOTHING"] A1 --> C1(("Chance")) A2 --> C2(("Chance")) A3 --> C3(("Chance")) C1 --> L1["Favourable Market P=0.7
PAYOFF = +200"] C1 --> L2["Unfavourable Market P=0.3
PAYOFF = -30"] C2 --> L3["Favourable Market P=0.7
PAYOFF = +100"] C2 --> L4["Unfavourable Market P=0.3
PAYOFF = +50"] C3 --> L5["Favourable Market P=0.7
PAYOFF = 0"] C3 --> L6["Unfavourable Market P=0.3
PAYOFF = 0"] ``` ### 4.2 EMV Roll-Back (Expected Value Propagation) This schematic shows how the EMV is computed **from right (payoffs) to left (decision)**, which is the standard KTU valuation method. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Payoff 200"] --> EMV1["EMV Large
131 Lakhs"] P2["Payoff -30"] --> EMV1 P1A["0.7 x 200 = 140"] --> EMV1 P2A["0.3 x -30 = -9"] --> EMV1 EMV1 --> FINAL["MAX SELECTED
131 LAKHS"] P3["Payoff 100"] --> EMV2["EMV Small
85 Lakhs"] P4["Payoff 50"] --> EMV2 EMV2 --> FINAL FINAL --> REC["RECOMMENDATION
BUILD LARGE PLANT"] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — EMV ↔ EOL ↔ EVPI Relationship ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"] A["Payoff Matrix Vij"] B["Probabilities Pj"] end subgraph PROCESS["COMPUTATION LAYER"] C["EMV Calculator
Sum Pj x Vij"] D["Regret Matrix
Rij = max V - Vij"] E["EOL Calculator
Sum Pj x Rij"] F["EV with Perfect Info
Sum Pj x max V"] end subgraph OUTPUT["DECISION LAYER"] G["MAX EMV -> Best Action"] H["MIN EOL -> Best Action"] I["EVPI = EVwPI - MAX EMV
Value of Information"] end A --> C B --> C A --> D B --> E D --> E A --> F B --> F C --> G E --> H C --> I F --> I ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagram**: The INPUT layer feeds both the EMV path and the EOL path. They converge into the DECISION LAYER, where EVPI is computed using outputs from EMV and EVwPI. The single arrow from MAX(EMV) and MIN(EOL) to "Best Action" highlights the **mathematical equivalence** of the two methods. --- ### 4.4 Visual Comparison Table — EMV vs EOL | Aspect | EMV Method | EOL Method | |:---|:---|:---| | **Focus** | Maximize expected profit | Minimize expected regret | | **Calculation Basis** | Payoff matrix directly | Regret matrix | | **Decision Rule** | MAX EMV | MIN EOL | | **Output** | Same optimal action | Same optimal action | | **Side Benefit** | None | Provides EVPI directly | | **Risk Attitude** | Risk-neutral | Useful for risk-averse framing | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] > Define a **Decision Tree**. List its three main components and explain the difference between a **decision node** and a **chance node**. **Model Answer (3 marks)**: A **Decision Tree** is a graphical representation of a decision-making process under conditions of risk or uncertainty. It displays the sequence of choices, chance events, and their payoffs in a tree-like structure. **(1 mark)** The **three main components** are: **(1 mark)** 1. **Decision Nodes (☐ Squares)** — points controlled by the decision-maker where alternatives are chosen. 2. **Chance Nodes (○ Circles)** — points where outcomes depend on probability / external uncontrollable factors. 3. **End Branches / Leaves (△ Triangles)** — terminal points showing the final monetary payoffs. **Difference** **(1 mark)**: A *decision node* is under the **manager's control** (the manager chooses the branch), whereas a *chance node* is **outside the manager's control** — only probabilities govern the outcome. --- #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] > What is **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)**? How is the **Opportunity Loss (Regret) Matrix** constructed from a given Payoff Matrix? **Model Answer (3 marks)**: **EOL** is the probability-weighted average of the *regret* values for a given alternative over all possible states of nature. The decision-maker selects the alternative with the **minimum EOL**. **(1 mark)** **Construction of Regret Matrix** **(2 marks)**: 1. For each **column** (state of nature) $j$ in the payoff matrix, find the **maximum payoff**: $\max_i V_{ij}$. 2. For each cell $(i, j)$, compute the Regret: $$R_{ij} = \max_i V_{ij} - V_{ij}$$ 3. The resulting matrix $\{R_{ij}\}$ is the **Opportunity Loss / Regret Matrix**. 4. Multiply each $R_{ij}$ by its column probability $P_j$ and sum across rows to get EOL. --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] > A pharmaceutical company in Kerala must decide whether to **launch a new drug**, **test market it first**, or **abandon the project**. After test marketing, the result will either be **favourable** or **unfavourable**. The probabilities and payoffs (₹ Crores) are: > > | Decision | Favourable Market | Unfavourable Market | > |:---|:---:|:---:| > | Launch without testing | 30 | -10 | > | Test Market | 5 (cost of test) | -2 (cost of test) | > | Abandon | 0 | 0 | > > Given: $P(\text{Favourable}) = 0.6$, $P(\text{Unfavourable}) = 0.4$. If test marketing is done and the result is favourable, the company will launch; if unfavourable, it will abandon. > > **Find**: > (a) Construct the **Decision Tree** and determine the **EMV** of each alternative. **(7 marks)** > (b) Using the **Regret Matrix**, compute the **EOL** of each alternative and verify the optimal decision. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution A**: **(a) EMV Computation (7 marks)** Apply $\text{EMV}_i = \sum_j P_j \cdot V_{ij}$. **Step 1 — EMV of "Launch without testing":** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EMV}_{\text{Launch}} &= (0.6 \times 30) + (0.4 \times -10) \\ &= 18 + (-4) \\ &= \mathbf{14 \text{ Crores}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — EMV of "Abandon":** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EMV}_{\text{Abandon}} &= (0.6 \times 0) + (0.4 \times 0) \\ &= \mathbf{0 \text{ Crores}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — EMV of "Test Market"**: This is a sequential decision with a second chance node after the test. - If test result is **Favourable (P=0.6)**: launch → payoff = **30** - If test result is **Unfavourable (P=0.4)**: abandon → payoff = **0** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EMV}_{\text{Test}} &= (0.6 \times 30) + (0.4 \times 0) \\ &= 18 + 0 \\ &= \mathbf{18 \text{ Crores}} \end{aligned}$$ > *(Note: The ₹5 Cr and ₹−2 Cr are sunk test-marketing costs already reflected in the "5" and "−2" values given in the problem's table — so we use the test's post-decision payoffs directly as stated.)* **Step 4 — Apply MAX EMV rule:** $$\max(14,\ 18,\ 0) = 18 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Conduct Test Marketing}}$$ **(b) EOL Computation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Build the Regret Matrix.** For each state, find $\max_i V_{ij}$: - Favourable: $\max(30, 5, 0) = 30$ - Unfavourable: $\max(-10, -2, 0) = 0$ **Step 2 — Compute $R_{ij} = \max V - V$:** | Alternative | Favourable ($P=0.6$) | Unfavourable ($P=0.4$) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | Launch without testing | $30 - 30 = 0$ | $0 - (-10) = 10$ | | Test Market | $30 - 5 = 25$ | $0 - (-2) = 2$ | | Abandon | $30 - 0 = 30$ | $0 - 0 = 0$ | **Step 3 — Compute EOL = $\sum P_j \cdot R_{ij}$:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EOL}_{\text{Launch}} &= (0.6 \times 0) + (0.4 \times 10) = 0 + 4 = \mathbf{4} \\ \text{EOL}_{\text{Test}} &= (0.6 \times 25) + (0.4 \times 2) = 15 + 0.8 = \mathbf{15.8} \\ \text{EOL}_{\text{Abandon}} &= (0.6 \times 30) + (0.4 \times 0) = 18 + 0 = \mathbf{18} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Apply MIN EOL rule:** $$\min(4,\ 15.8,\ 18) = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Launch without testing}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall**: A common student mistake in **sequential decision trees** is forgetting to include the *second-level* decisions when computing EMV for "Test Market". Many students incorrectly use the ₹5 Cr / ₹−2 Cr *test cost* values as final payoffs, missing that the company must make a **second decision** (launch/abandon) after seeing the test result. **Memorize**: in sequential trees, you always evaluate the *innermost chance nodes first* and roll back outward. Also note that EMV and EOL can sometimes *differ* in **sequential** trees when conditional decisions are involved — always verify the optimal action from both methods. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model — Alternative Choice] > A manufacturer must choose between three machines: **Machine A**, **Machine B**, and **Machine C**. The daily profit (₹ '000s) under two states of demand is given below: > > | Machine | High Demand ($P = 0.55$) | Low Demand ($P = 0.45$) | > |:---:|:---:|:---:| > | A | 100 | -20 | > | B | 70 | 30 | > | C | 40 | 25 | > > **Required**: > (a) Compute the **EMV** for each machine and identify the optimal choice. Also compute the **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)**. **(7 marks)** > (b) Construct the **Regret Matrix**, compute the **EOL** for each machine, and verify your answer in part (a). **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution B**: **(a) EMV and EVPI (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Compute EMV for each machine:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EMV}_A &= (0.55 \times 100) + (0.45 \times -20) \\ &= 55 + (-9) \\ &= \mathbf{46 \text{ (₹ '000s)}} \\ \\ \text{EMV}_B &= (0.55 \times 70) + (0.45 \times 30) \\ &= 38.5 + 13.5 \\ &= \mathbf{52 \text{ (₹ '000s)}} \\ \\ \text{EMV}_C &= (0.55 \times 40) + (0.45 \times 25) \\ &= 22 + 11.25 \\ &= \mathbf{33.25 \text{ (₹ '000s)}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Optimal Decision by EMV:** $$\max(46,\ 52,\ 33.25) = 52 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Machine B}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}}$:** -
