Max payoff under High Demand: $\max(100, 70, 40) = 100$ - Max payoff under Low Demand: $\max(-20, 30, 25) = 30$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} &= (0.55 \times 100) + (0.45 \times 30) \\ &= 55 + 13.5 \\ &= \mathbf{68.5} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Compute EVPI:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EVPI} &= \text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \max_i \text{EMV}_i \\ &= 68.5 - 52 \\ &= \mathbf{16.5 \text{ (₹ '000s)}} \end{aligned}$$ > The firm should not pay more than **₹16,500** for perfect demand-forecast information. **(b) EOL and Verification (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Build the Regret Matrix** $R_{ij} = \max_i V_{ij} - V_{ij}$: | Machine | High Demand ($P=0.55$) | Low Demand ($P=0.45$) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | $100 - 100 = 0$ | $30 - (-20) = 50$ | | B | $100 - 70 = 30$ | $30 - 30 = 0$ | | C | $100 - 40 = 60$ | $30 - 25 = 5$ | **Step 2 — Compute EOL = $\sum P_j \cdot R_{ij}$:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EOL}_A &= (0.55 \times 0) + (0.45 \times 50) \\ &= 0 + 22.5 \\ &= \mathbf{22.5} \\ \\ \text{EOL}_B &= (0.55 \times 30) + (0.45 \times 0) \\ &= 16.5 + 0 \\ &= \mathbf{16.5} \\ \\ \text{EOL}_C &= (0.55 \times 60) + (0.45 \times 5) \\ &= 33 + 2.25 \\ &= \mathbf{35.25} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Optimal Decision by EOL:** $$\min(22.5,\ 16.5,\ 35.25) = 16.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Machine B}}$$ > **Verification ✓**: Both EMV and EOL agree on **Machine B** as optimal. **Step 4 — Cross-Check EVPI via EOL route:** $$\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}_i = 16.5 \quad \checkmark \quad \text{(matches Part (a) Step 4)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 2**: > 1. **Sign errors in loss rows**: In Regret matrix construction, students often subtract in the wrong direction. Always remember: **Regret = Best Payoff in that State − Your Payoff in that State**. > 2. **Negative payoffs in max-finding**: When finding $\max_i V_{ij}$, do not skip negative numbers — they may still be the maximum if all values are negative (e.g., $-20 > -40$). Here, $-20$ vs $30$ → **30** is the max; regret for Machine A under Low Demand = $30 - (-20) = 50$. > 3. **Forgetting probability summation**: Always verify $\sum P_j = 1$ before starting. Here $0.55 + 0.45 = 1$ ✓. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Decision Trees, EMV & EOL** - ☐ A **Decision Tree** is built **left-to-right** but evaluated **right-to-left** (roll-back method). - ☐ **Decision Node (☐)** = Manager-controlled; **Chance Node (○)** = Probability-controlled. - ☐ **EMV** = Probability-weighted average of payoffs; choose **MAX EMV** (profit) or **MIN EMV** (cost). - ☐ **Regret** $R_{ij} = \max_i V_{ij} - V_{ij}$ — computed **column-wise**, then EOL = $\sum P_j \cdot R_{ij}$ (row-wise sum). - ☐ **EOL Decision Rule**: Choose the alternative with **MIN EOL**. - ☐ **EMV and EOL always give the same optimal decision** for single-stage problems. - ☐ **EVPI** = $\text{EV}_{\text{wPI}} - \max_i \text{EMV}_i = \min_i \text{EOL}_i$. - ☐ **EVPI interpretation**: The maximum amount a rational firm should pay for **perfect information** before deciding. - ☐ Always check **probability axiom**: $\sum P_j = 1$. - ☐ In **sequential trees**, evaluate inner chance nodes first, then propagate expected values outward. - ☐ Watch for **negative payoffs** in regret computation — sign errors cost 1–2 marks per KTU exam. - ☐ The **three components** of a decision tree (Decision, Chance, End branch) must each be clearly drawn and labelled for full marks. - ☐ Always **state the decision rule** explicitly in your answer (e.g., "Choose MAX EMV") to earn the reasoning marks in KTU valuation.

topics.title: MaxiMin Criterion, MiniMax Criterion, MiniMax Regret Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET504 - MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MaxiMin Criterion, MiniMax Criterion, MiniMax Regret Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Decision-Making Under Uncertainty

In managerial decision theory, when a decision-maker must choose among several alternatives (strategies) whose outcomes depend on **states of nature** (future events whose probabilities are unknown or cannot be assigned), the situation is called **decision-making under uncertainty**. Three classic non-probabilistic criteria taught under KTU Module 2 are the **MaxiMin**, **MiniMax**, and **MiniMax Regret** criteria. All three are **pessimistic (conservative) criteria** because they protect the decision-maker against the worst possible state of nature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> These criteria belong to the broader topic of "Decision Theory under Uncertainty" and assume a **purely payoff-based (or cost-based) matrix** with no probability distribution attached to the states of nature.

---

## 1.2 MaxiMin Criterion (Pessimistic Profit Criterion)

### Formal Definition
The **MaxiMin Criterion** is applied to a **payoff (profit) matrix**. For every alternative (row), identify the *worst* (minimum) possible payoff across all states of nature. Then, select the alternative that gives the **maximum of these row-minimums**.

$$a^{*} \;=\; \max_{i}\;\min_{j}\; a_{ij}$$

where $a_{ij}$ is the payoff from alternative $i$ under state of nature $j$, and $a^{*}$ is the chosen (optimal) alternative.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are a student choosing an elective subject where the grading scheme is unknown. You do not know whether the professor will grade generously or strictly. Being cautious, you look at each subject, find the *worst grade you could possibly get* in each, and then pick the subject where even your **worst grade** is the **highest** among all subjects. That is MaxiMin — playing safe, hedging against the worst.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Trait:** MaxiMin is also called the *pessimistic criterion* or *Wald's criterion* (named after mathematician Abraham Wald). It assumes nature is your **adversary** and prepares you for the worst.

---

## 1.3 MiniMax Criterion (Pessimistic Cost Criterion)

### Formal Definition
The **MiniMax Criterion** is the mirror image of MaxiMin, but applied to a **cost (loss) matrix**. For every alternative (row), identify the *worst* (maximum) possible cost across all states of nature. Then, select the alternative whose **maximum cost is the smallest**.

$$a^{*} \;=\; \min_{i}\;\max_{j}\; c_{ij}$$

where $c_{ij}$ is the cost incurred by alternative $i$ under state of nature $j$.

### Conceptual Analogy
You are buying raw materials from three suppliers. You do not know the future market price of the commodity. For each supplier, you compute the **highest possible total cost** you might end up paying. To be safe, you pick the supplier whose worst-case bill is the **lowest** of the three. That is MiniMax.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Trait:** MiniMax is the cost-side counterpart of MaxiMin. Mathematically, both criteria formalize the *worst-case philosophy*. In practice, use MaxiMin when the table lists profits/gains; use MiniMax when the table lists costs/losses.

---

## 1.4 MiniMax Regret Criterion (Savage's Criterion)

### Formal Definition
The **MiniMax Regret Criterion** (proposed by Leonard Jimmie Savage) shifts focus from raw payoffs to **opportunity loss (regret)**. A *regret table* is first constructed by subtracting each cell value from the **best (maximum) value in its column**. Then, for every alternative, find the **maximum regret** across all states. Finally, choose the alternative whose **maximum regret is the minimum**.

$$r_{ij} \;=\; \max_{k}\, a_{kj} \;-\; a_{ij}$$

$$a^{*} \;=\; \min_{i}\;\max_{j}\; r_{ij}$$

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you sold your old phone for ₹8,000, only to learn later that your friend sold the same model for ₹12,000. Your **regret** is ₹4,000 — the *lost opportunity*. The MiniMax Regret criterion says: look at every decision you could have made, compute how much you would regret each one in the worst-case state of nature, and choose the decision whose *biggest possible regret is the smallest*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use Regret?**
> Many managers do not maximize absolute profit; instead, they minimize *disappointment* relative to what could have been earned. The regret criterion captures this psychological reality better than MaxiMin.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Criterion | Matrix Type | Row Operation | Final Selection |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **MaxiMin** | Payoff (Profit) | Take **Row Min** | **Max of Row Mins** |
| **MiniMax** | Cost / Loss | Take **Row Max** | **Min of Row Maxes** |
| **MiniMax Regret** | Regret (built from Payoff) | Take **Row Max** of Regret | **Min of Row Max Regrets** |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU board-exam pitfall: students apply MaxiMin to a cost matrix or MiniMax to a profit matrix. Always confirm whether the table reports **profits** or **costs** before selecting the criterion.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Philosophy: Why Three Criteria?

All three criteria belong to the family of **non-probabilistic decision rules under ignorance**. They were formalized in the 1950s as part of *Statistical Decision Theory*. Their shared assumption is that the decision-maker has **no reliable probability distribution** over the states of nature, so they instead use the *guaranteed floor* of each alternative as the basis for choice.

The differences lie in:
1. **What is being optimized** — raw payoff, raw cost, or opportunity loss.
2. **The psychological stance** — Wald assumes the worst; Savage assumes the worst *missed opportunity*.

---

## 2.2 Logical Step-Wise Procedure

### A. MaxiMin Procedure
1. Construct the **payoff matrix** of size $m \times n$ (alternatives × states).
2. For each row $i$, compute $\mu_i = \min_j a_{ij}$.
3. Select $i^*$ such that $\mu_{i^*} = \max_i \mu_i$.

### B. MiniMax Procedure
1. Construct the **cost matrix** of size $m \times n$.
2. For each row $i$, compute $\kappa_i = \max_j c_{ij}$.
3. Select $i^*$ such that $\kappa_{i^*} = \min_i \kappa_i$.

### C. MiniMax Regret Procedure
1. Start from the **payoff matrix** $A = [a_{ij}]$.
2. For each column $j$, compute the column maximum $M_j = \max_i a_{ij}$.
3. Build the **regret matrix** $R$ where $r_{ij} = M_j - a_{ij}$.
4. For each row $i$, compute $\rho_i = \max_j r_{ij}$.
5. Select $i^*$ such that $\rho_{i^*} = \min_i \rho_i$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Used In |
|---|------------------|---------|---------|
| 1 | $a^{*} = \max_{i}\,\min_{j}\,a_{ij}$ | MaxiMin selection rule | MaxiMin |
| 2 | $\mu_i = \min_{j}\,a_{ij}$ | Worst payoff for alternative $i$ | MaxiMin |
| 3 | $a^{*} = \min_{i}\,\max_{j}\,c_{ij}$ | MiniMax selection rule | MiniMax |
| 4 | $\kappa_i = \max_{j}\,c_{ij}$ | Worst cost for alternative $i$ | MiniMax |
| 5 | $r_{ij} = \max_{k}\,a_{kj} - a_{ij}$ | Regret at cell $(i,j)$ | MiniMax Regret |
| 6 | $\rho_i = \max_{j}\,r_{ij}$ | Maximum regret for alternative $i$ | MiniMax Regret |
| 7 | $a^{*} = \min_{i}\,\rho_i$ | MiniMax Regret selection rule | MiniMax Regret |
| 8 | $m \times n$ | Matrix dimensions (alternatives × states) | All |
| 9 | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} r_{ij} = \text{(informational only)}$ | Sum of regrets in a column | Diagnostic |
| 10 | $r_{ij} \geq 0 \; \forall i,j$ | Non-negativity of regret | Validation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regret Matrix Property:** Each column of the regret matrix contains *at least one zero* — corresponding to the alternative that performed best in that state. Verifying this is a quick way to cross-check your arithmetic during exams.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Capital Budgeting** | Choosing a project when market demand is unknown |
| **Supply Chain Management** | Selecting a supplier when raw-material price volatility is unknown |
| **Software Project Bidding** | Picking a bidding strategy when competitor behavior is unknown |
| **Construction Engineering** | Choosing a foundation type when soil behavior is uncertain |
| **Energy Sector** | Selecting a power-plant design when fuel-price trends are unpredictable |
| **Inventory Management** | Choosing an ordering policy when demand pattern is unknown |

The MiniMax Regret criterion, in particular, is heavily used in **medical decision analysis**, **military strategy**, and **portfolio management** because it explicitly accounts for the *psychological cost of missed opportunities*.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Worked Example (Payoff-Based)

### Problem Setup
A Kerala-based startup is choosing among **three product-launch strategies** ($S_1, S_2, S_3$) under three possible market states ($N_1$ = High Demand, $N_2$ = Moderate Demand, $N_3$ = Low Demand). The expected profit (in **₹ Lakhs**) is given in the payoff matrix below.

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & N_1 & N_2 & N_3 \\
\hline
S_1 & 200 & 80 & -40 \\
S_2 & 150 & 100 & 30 \\
S_3 & 100 & 70 & 50 \\
\end{array}
$$

Apply all three criteria and recommend the best strategy.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution — MaxiMin Criterion

**Step 1:** Identify the row minimum (worst payoff) for each strategy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= \min(200,\; 80,\; -40) \;=\; -40 \\
\mu_2 &= \min(150,\; 100,\; 30) \;=\; 30 \\
\mu_3 &= \min(100,\; 70,\; 50) \;=\; 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the maximum of these minimums.

$$
\max(-40,\; 30,\; 50) \;=\; 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Choose } S_3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even in the worst-case scenario (low demand), Strategy $S_3$ guarantees ₹50 Lakhs — the highest guaranteed floor.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Solution — MiniMax Criterion (Cost Version)

### Cost Matrix (Same Startup — Operational Expenditure View)
The same management is now examining the **operational cost** (in ₹ Lakhs) of each strategy.

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & N_1 & N_2 & N_3 \\
\hline
C_1 & 250 & 150 & 80 \\
C_2 & 200 & 180 & 120 \\
C_3 & 150 & 130 & 100 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the row maximum (worst cost) for each strategy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\kappa_1 &= \max(250,\; 150,\; 80) \;=\; 250 \\
\kappa_2 &= \max(200,\; 180,\; 120) \;=\; 200 \\
\kappa_3 &= \max(150,\; 130,\; 100) \;=\; 150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the minimum of these maximums.

$$
\min(250,\; 200,\; 150) \;=\; 150 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Choose } C_3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even if conditions turn out to be the worst, $C_3$ never costs more than ₹150 Lakhs — the smallest ceiling on possible losses.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Solution — MiniMax Regret Criterion (Back to Payoff Matrix)

**Step 1:** Find the column maximum of the original payoff matrix.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= \max(200,\; 150,\; 100) \;=\; 200 \\
M_2 &= \max(80,\; 100,\; 70) \;=\; 100 \\
M_3 &= \max(-40,\; 30,\; 50) \;=\; 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Construct the **regret matrix** $r_{ij} = M_j - a_{ij}$.

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & N_1 & N_2 & N_3 \\
\hline
S_1 & 200-200=0 & 100-80=20 & 50-(-40)=90 \\
S_2 & 200-150=50 & 100-100=0 & 50-30=20 \\
S_3 & 200-100=100 & 100-70=30 & 50-50=0 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3:** Find the maximum regret for each strategy (row maximum of regret matrix).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_1 &= \max(0,\; 20,\; 90) \;=\; 90 \\
\rho_2 &= \max(50,\; 0,\; 20) \;=\; 50 \\
\rho_3 &= \max(100,\; 30,\; 0) \;=\; 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Take the minimum of these maximum regrets.

$$
\min(90,\; 50,\; 100) \;=\; 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Choose } S_2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The worst-case regret of choosing $S_2$ is only ₹50 Lakhs — the smallest opportunity loss a manager could face across all possible future states.

---

## 3.5 Consolidated Comparison Table

| Criterion | Recommended Strategy | Reason |
|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| **MaxiMin** | $S_3$ | Best worst-case payoff = ₹50 Lakhs |
| **MiniMax** | $C_3$ | Best worst-case cost = ₹150 Lakhs |
| **MiniMax Regret** | $S_2$ | Smallest worst-case opportunity loss = ₹50 Lakhs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the conflict:** MaxiMin selects $S_3$, while MiniMax Regret selects $S_2$. This is a *real and frequent* outcome in practice — different criteria can recommend different strategies. KTU exams often ask students to compare and comment on such conflicts.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision-Flow Schematic for All Three Criteria

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Decision Problem under Uncertainty"] --> B["Identify Nature of Matrix: Payoff OR Cost"]
    B --> C{"Is Matrix Payoff or Cost?"}
    C -- "Payoff (Profit)" --> D["Apply MaxiMin or MiniMax Regret"]
    C -- "Cost (Loss)" --> E["Apply MiniMax"]
    D --> F["MaxiMin: Find Row Min, then Max"]
    D --> G["MiniMax Regret: Build Regret Table, then Min of Row Max"]
    E --> H["MiniMax: Find Row Max, then Min"]
    F --> I["Select Alternative: a-star"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["End: Optimal Conservative Decision"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Regret Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Input: Original Payoff Matrix A_ij"] --> P2["Block 1: Column-Wise Maxima M_j"]
    P2 --> P3["Block 2: Regret Cell r_ij = M_j minus a_ij"]
    P3 --> P4["Block 3: Row-Wise Max Regret rho_i"]
    P4 --> P5["Block 4: Selection min over rho_i"]
    P5 --> P6["Output: Optimal Strategy a-star"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual State-of-Nature and Alternative Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StateN["States of Nature (Unknown Probability)"]
        N1["N1 : Favorable Market"]
        N2["N2 : Moderate Market"]
        N3["N3 : Unfavorable Market"]
    end
    subgraph AltSpace["Decision Alternatives"]
        S1["S1 : Aggressive Launch"]
        S2["S2 : Balanced Launch"]
        S3["S3 : Conservative Launch"]
    end
    StateN -- "Determines Payoff" --> AltSpace
    AltSpace -- "Feeds MaxiMin" --> DEC["MaxiMin Decision Block"]
    AltSpace -- "Feeds MiniMax Regret" --> DEC
    DEC --> OUT["Optimal Pessimistic Choice"]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the MaxiMin Criterion. State one practical situation where it is most appropriate.**

**Model Answer:**
The MaxiMin Criterion is a non-probabilistic decision rule used under conditions of uncertainty. For each alternative, the *minimum* possible payoff across all states of nature is first identified. The alternative corresponding to the *maximum* of these row-minimums is then selected. Mathematically,

$$a^{*} = \max_{i}\,\min_{j}\,a_{ij}.$$

It is most appropriate in **high-stakes, irreversible decisions** such as selecting a foundation type for a critical civil engineering structure, where the decision-maker must guarantee protection against the worst geological condition.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for example)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between MiniMax Criterion and MiniMax Regret Criterion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | MiniMax Criterion | MiniMax Regret Criterion |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Matrix used | **Cost matrix** | **Regret matrix** derived from payoff |
| Row operation | Row **maximum** | Row **maximum** of regrets |
| Final rule | **Min** of row maximums | **Min** of row maximum regrets |
| Focus | Minimize the *worst cost* | Minimize the *worst opportunity loss* |
| Originator | Wald | Savage |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each correct distinguishing line and 1 for the originator pair)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A manufacturing firm must select one of three machine-purchasing options: $M_1, M_2, M_3$. The profit (in ₹ Lakhs) under four demand states is given below. Apply the **MaxiMin Criterion** and the **MiniMax Regret Criterion** and recommend the best strategy. **(7 marks)**

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & D_4 \\
\hline
M_1 & 120 & 80 & 40 & -20 \\
M_2 & 90 & 70 & 60 & 50 \\
M_3 & 70 & 60 & 50 & 40 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**MaxiMin (3 marks):**

Row minimums:
$\mu_1 = \min(120, 80, 40, -20) = -20$
$\mu_2 = \min(90, 70, 60, 50) = 50$
$\mu_3 = \min(70, 60, 50, 40) = 40$

$\max(-20, 50, 40) = 50 \Rightarrow$ Choose $M_2$.

**MiniMax Regret (4 marks):**

Column maxima: $M_{D_1}=120$, $M_{D_2}=80$, $M_{D_3}=60$, $M_{D_4}=50$.

Regret matrix:
- $M_1$: $(120-120, 80-80, 60-40, 50-(-20)) = (0, 0, 20, 70)$
- $M_2$: $(120-90, 80-70, 60-60, 50-50) = (30, 10, 0, 0)$
- $M_3$: $(120-70, 80-60, 60-50, 50-40) = (50, 20, 10, 10)$

Row max regrets: $\rho_1 = 70$, $\rho_2 = 30$, $\rho_3 = 50$.

$\min(70, 30, 50) = 30 \Rightarrow$ Choose $M_2$.

**Recommendation:** Both criteria suggest $M_2$.

---

**(b)** Explain with a numerical example why MaxiMin and MiniMax Regret can lead to **different recommendations** for the same payoff matrix. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The criteria differ in their optimization targets: MaxiMin compares *raw payoffs*, while MiniMax Regret compares *opportunity losses*. They can disagree when one strategy has a high guaranteed floor but suffers large regret under another state.

**Example Matrix (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
 & S_1 & S_2 \\
\hline
A_1 & 100 & 0 \\
A_2 & 60 & 50 \\
\end{array}
$$

**MaxiMin (2 marks):** Row minimums — $A_1: 0$, $A_2: 50$. Max of mins = 50 $\Rightarrow$ Choose $A_2$.

**MiniMax Regret (2 marks):** Column maxima — $S_1: 100$, $S_2: 50$. Regret matrix — $A_1: (0, 50)$, $A_2: (40, 0)$. Row max regrets — $A_1: 50$, $A_2: 40$. Min of max regrets = 40 $\Rightarrow$ Choose $A_2$.

*(Here both agreed; but replace $A_1$ row with $(100, 30)$ and the criteria will disagree — MaxiMin still picks $A_1$, while MiniMax Regret may pick $A_2$.)*

Hence, MaxiMin and MiniMax Regret are *not equivalent*; the choice between them depends on whether the manager values the absolute worst payoff or the worst missed opportunity more.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A logistics company is choosing among three transport contracts. The total cost (in ₹ Lakhs) under three fuel-price scenarios is:

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & F_1 \text{ (Low)} & F_2 \text{ (Normal)} & F_3 \text{ (High)} \\
\hline
T_1 & 80 & 120 & 200 \\
T_2 & 100 & 110 & 150 \\
T_3 & 90 & 130 & 140 \\
\end{array}
$$

Apply the **MiniMax Criterion** and identify the optimal contract. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Since the matrix reports **costs**, MiniMax applies directly.

Row maximums (worst-case cost):
$\kappa_1 = \max(80, 120, 200) = 200$
$\kappa_2 = \max(100, 110, 150) = 150$
$\kappa_3 = \max(90, 130, 140) = 140$

Minimum of these: $\min(200, 150, 140) = 140 \Rightarrow$ Choose $T_3$.

**Optimal contract: $T_3$** with a worst-case cost ceiling of ₹140 Lakhs. *(7 marks: 2 marks for row max, 2 marks for the min, 1 mark for selection, 2 marks for interpretation)*

---

**(b)** Discuss the **assumptions and limitations** of the MaxiMin, MiniMax, and MiniMax Regret criteria. Which criterion is most suitable for a risk-averse manager? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Common Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. States of nature are **mutually exclusive and exhaustive**.
2. No probability distribution over states is known or used.
3. The decision-maker adopts a **pessimistic stance** (worst-case protection).

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Ignores probabilities entirely** — even when partial probability information is available.
2. **Excessively pessimistic** — for MaxiMin and MiniMax, only the worst state matters; all other information is discarded.
3. **Regret computation requires payoff conversion** — not applicable directly to cost matrices without transformation.
4. **May yield different recommendations** for the same problem, creating a decision paradox.
5. **Scale-dependent** — outcomes must be in comparable units.

**Best Criterion for a Risk-Averse Manager (2 marks):**
A risk-averse manager should prefer **MiniMax** (for costs) or **MaxiMin** (for profits) because these criteria explicitly hedge against the worst possible outcome. However, if the manager is more concerned with **relative under-performance** (i.e., how badly they will feel about missed opportunities), the **MiniMax Regret** criterion is psychologically and rationally superior. In practice, the MiniMax Regret criterion (Savage's) is often preferred because it balances conservatism with awareness of the best achievable alternative in each state.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing matrix type (2 marks lost):** Applying MaxiMin to a *cost* matrix or vice-versa. Always verify whether values represent profit or cost.
> 2. **Regret-table construction error (3 marks lost):** Subtracting *row* maxima instead of *column* maxima when computing regret. Remember: **column** maxima determine opportunity loss in each state.
> 3. **Skipping interpretation (1–2 marks lost):** KTU examiners reward a concluding managerial sentence such as *"Hence, $S_2$ is the recommended strategy because its worst-case regret is the lowest."* Do not stop at the numerical answer.
> 4. **Not labeling units:** Always mention ₹ Lakhs / Crores / Units explicitly.
> 5. **Wrong sign on regret:** Regret $r_{ij}$ is *always* non-negative. A negative value means an arithmetic mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ MaxiMin applies to **payoff matrices**; pick the **max of row mins**.
- ✅ MiniMax applies to **cost matrices**; pick the **min of row maxes**.
- ✅ MiniMax Regret applies to **payoff matrices** but converts them first into a **regret table** using **column maxima**.
- ✅ Each column of a regret matrix **must contain at least one zero** — use this as a quick validation step.
- ✅ All three criteria are **pessimistic** and **non-probabilistic**.
- ✅ Different criteria may yield **different recommendations** for the same data — this is normal and is itself a frequent KTU exam question.
- ✅ Always include a **managerial interpretation sentence** at the end of your solution.
- ✅ Memorize the formulas: $\max \min$, $\min \max$, $\min \max r_{ij}$ with $r_{ij} = M_j - a_{ij}$.
- ✅ Watch for **sign conventions** in matrices — a negative number indicates a *loss*, not necessarily a data error.
- ✅ Remember the originators: **Wald** (MaxiMin/MiniMax) and **Savage** (MiniMax Regret).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Construction, AOA Diagrams, Redundancy, CPM and PERT Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET504 - MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Construction, AOA Diagrams, Redundancy, CPM and PERT Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — NETWORK CONSTRUCTION, AOA DIAGRAMS, REDUNDANCY, CPM & PERT NETWORKS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — Network Construction**
> *Network Construction* in Project Management is the systematic, graphical representation of a project's activities (tasks) and their logical interdependencies using nodes (events) and arrows (activities), thereby enabling quantitative scheduling, monitoring, and control of engineering and industrial projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — AOA Diagram**
> An **Activity-On-Arrow (AOA)** network is a project schematic where each **arrow represents an activity** (a time-consuming task) and each **node (circle/rectangle) represents an event** (a milestone marking the start or completion of one or more activities).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — CPM**
> The **Critical Path Method (CPM)** is a deterministic, time-based project planning technique that identifies the longest path of dependent activities (the *critical path*) and calculates the minimum time required to complete a project, assuming activity durations are known with certainty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — PERT**
> The **Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)** is a probabilistic, event-oriented planning tool developed for projects involving uncertainty, where each activity's duration is estimated using **three values**: *Optimistic (a)*, *Most Likely (m)*, and *Pessimistic (b)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — Redundancy**
> *Redundancy* in a network refers to **redundant (dummy) activities** or **redundant paths** — logical connectors or sequences that do not represent actual work but are required to correctly preserve the *precedence relationships* between real activities in an AOA diagram.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kerala to Delhi by car** with several sightseeing stops (activities).

- Each **stop / task** (e.g., "Visit Taj Mahal", "Refuel car", "Lunch break") is an **Activity** → drawn as an **Arrow**.
- Each **point in time** where you finish one task and are ready for the next (e.g., "Boarding completed", "Refuel done") is an **Event** → drawn as a **Node** (a circle).
- Some tasks cannot start until others finish. For example, *"Cross the Ganges bridge"* depends on *"Reach the bridge"*. This **dependency** is shown by linking arrows.
- Sometimes, you might split the trip logically — "Travel by flight" and "Travel by train" might converge at Delhi, but neither depends on the other. To correctly model this, you insert a **dummy link** — a zero-time dependency arrow that just exists to keep the logic right.

The **longest route** through this trip (the one with no slack) is the *critical path* — if any activity on this route is delayed, the *entire* trip is delayed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students**
> Think of a project network like a *spider's web of dependent tasks*. The **Critical Path** is the *backbone thread*. Pull it, and the entire web collapses (project delay). All other paths have **slack** (float) — they can stretch without breaking the project schedule.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Standard Unit / Notation |
|------|---------|---------------------------|
| Activity | A time-consuming task | Arrow (→) |
| Event / Node | Start or finish milestone | Circle (●) |
| Dummy Activity | Logical dependency (zero time, zero cost) | Dashed Arrow (⇢) |
| EST | Earliest Start Time of an activity | Days / Weeks |
| EFT | Earliest Finish Time of an activity | Days / Weeks |
| LST | Latest Start Time of an activity | Days / Weeks |
| LFT | Latest Finish Time of an activity | Days / Weeks |
| Slack / Float | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying project | Days / Weeks |
| Critical Path | Longest path through the network (zero slack) | Sequence of activities |
| Optimistic time (a) | Best-case duration estimate | Time units |
| Most Likely time (m) | Most probable duration estimate | Time units |
| Pessimistic time (b) | Worst-case duration estimate | Time units |
| Expected time (te) | Weighted mean of (a, m, b) | Time units |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Rules for AOA Network Construction

The AOA method has a strict set of construction rules. Violating them produces a **logically invalid network**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rulebook for AOA Networks**

**Rule 1 — Single Activity per Arrow Pair**
Each *real* activity must be represented by *one and only one* arrow between any two given nodes. You cannot have two arrows connecting the same pair of nodes representing two different activities.

**Rule 2 — No Two Nodes Connected by More Than One Activity**
Between any two specific nodes (say, node $i$ and node $j$), there can only be one direct arrow. If two activities logically start and end at the same events, **dummy activities** must be introduced to differentiate them.

**Rule 3 — No Loops / Closed Cycles**
A network must always flow forward from the start event to the end event. **Cycles (loops)** violate the precedence logic.

**Rule 4 — No Dangling Nodes**
Every event (except the start and end) must have at least one **incoming** and one **outgoing** activity. Dangling nodes represent orphan tasks that are not logically connected.

**Rule 5 — Event Numbering Convention**
Events are numbered such that the arrow generally proceeds from a lower-numbered node to a higher-numbered node: $i < j$.

**Rule 6 — One Start and One End Event**
A project network must have a **single Start Node** (where all root activities originate) and a **single End Node** (where all terminal activities converge). If multiple end-points exist, they are merged using dummy activities.

---

## 2.2 Dummy Activities — The Heart of Redundancy in AOA

A **dummy activity** is a logical (zero-duration, zero-cost) connector used in AOA networks to:

1. Show a **logical dependency** that is not actual work (e.g., "Activity C depends on completion of Activity A, but Activity D depends only on Activity B").
2. **Eliminate redundancy** when two real activities have identical start and end events.
3. **Maintain correct network logic** when merging sub-projects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Insight**
> A dummy activity has **duration = 0** and **cost = 0**, but it is treated as a *real activity* during forward and backward pass calculations. This distinction is frequently tested in KTU Part A questions.

### 2.2.1 The Classic "Redundancy" Problem

Consider the following precedence table:

| Activity | Depends on |
|----------|------------|
| A | — |
| B | — |
| C | A |
| D | B |
| E | C, D |

If we naively draw AOA: A and B both start from node 1, but C and D converge at node 3 to feed E. The problem: **A and B share the same start node, but C depends only on A, not B, and D depends only on B, not A.**

Without a dummy, we cannot correctly express this in AOA. The dummy solves it by:

```
    A
 1 ─────► 3
    B
 2 ─────► 4
   ╲       ╱
    ╲ D  ╱
     ╲  ╱
      ▼
   Dummy (3→4)
```

Actually, the proper AOA construction uses a dummy from the end of A to the start of D (or vice versa) so that the logic flows correctly.

---

## 2.3 CPM — Critical Path Method: Deterministic Framework

The Critical Path Method (CPM) operates on **known, fixed activity durations**. It involves two passes:

### 2.3.1 Forward Pass (Calculating Earliest Times)

For each activity $(i, j)$ with duration $t_{ij}$:

**EFT of node $j$ = $\max$ over all predecessor nodes $i$ of { EFT of node $i$ + $t_{ij}$ }**

Or equivalently:

$$E_{j} = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} \left( E_{i} + t_{ij} \right)$$

For the **start node**, $E_{1} = 0$.

### 2.3.2 Backward Pass (Calculating Latest Times)

For each activity $(i, j)$:

**LST of node $i$ = $\min$ over all successor nodes $j$ of { LST of node $j$ − $t_{ij}$ }**

$$L_{i} = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} \left( L_{j} - t_{ij} \right)$$

For the **end node**, $L_{\text{end}} = E_{\text{end}}$.

### 2.3.3 Slack / Float Calculations

**Total Slack (TS)** for activity $(i, j)$:

$$TS_{ij} = L_{j} - E_{i} - t_{ij}$$

**Free Slack (FS)** for activity $(i, j)$:

$$FS_{ij} = E_{j} - E_{i} - t_{ij}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path Identification**
> Activities where **Total Slack = 0** form the **Critical Path**. These activities cannot be delayed by even one day without delaying the entire project.

---

## 2.4 PERT — Probabilistic Framework with Three-Time Estimates

PERT was developed by the **U.S. Navy (1958)** for the Polaris missile project to handle uncertainty in research and development activities.

### 2.4.1 Three-Time Estimate System

For each activity, three estimates are made:

- $a$ = **Optimistic time** (best-case scenario; everything goes perfectly)
- $m$ = **Most Likely time** (mode of the probability distribution)
- $b$ = **Pessimistic time** (worst-case scenario; delays, setbacks)

### 2.4.2 Beta Distribution Approximation

PERT models the activity duration as a **Beta distribution** with:

**Expected (Mean) Time:**

$$t_{e} = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6}$$

**Variance:**

$$\sigma^{2} = \left( \frac{b - a}{6} \right)^{2}$$

**Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma = \frac{b - a}{6}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "4m Weighting"**
> The factor 4 on the most likely time is because in the Beta distribution, the **mode is weighted twice as much as the endpoints**, while the endpoints share the remaining weight equally. This is a hallmark PERT formula frequently asked in KTU exams.

### 2.4.3 Project Variance and Probability of Completion

For the critical path:

$$\sigma_{\text{project}}^{2} = \sum_{i \in \text{CP}} \sigma_{i}^{2}$$

**Standard Deviation of Project:**

$$\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{project}}^{2}}$$

**Z-Value (Standard Normal Variate):**

$$Z = \frac{T_{s} - T_{e}}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$$

where $T_{s}$ = scheduled (target) completion time, $T_{e}$ = expected completion time.

The probability $P(Z \leq z)$ is obtained from **standard normal tables**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Table — It Covers ~70% of Numerical Questions in KTU 2024 ESE**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | When Used |
|---------|---------|-------------------|-----------|
| Earliest Finish Time (EFT) of node $j$ | $E_{j} = \max_{i \in \text{pred}} \left( E_{i} + t_{ij} \right)$ | $E_{j}$: days/weeks | Forward Pass |
| Latest Start Time (LST) of node $i$ | $L_{i} = \min_{j \in \text{succ}} \left( L_{j} - t_{ij} \right)$ | $L_{i}$: days/weeks | Backward Pass |
| Total Slack | $TS_{ij} = L_{j} - E_{i} - t_{ij}$ | TS: days/weeks | Slack Analysis |
| Free Slack | $FS_{ij} = E_{j} - E_{i} - t_{ij}$ | FS: days/weeks | Slack Analysis |
| Critical Path | Sequence where $TS = 0$ for all activities | Path (set of nodes) | Identification |
| PERT Expected Time | $t_{e} = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$ | $t_{e}$: days | PERT |
| PERT Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \left( \dfrac{b - a}{6} \right)^{2}$ | $\sigma^{2}$: days$^2$ | PERT |
| PERT Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \dfrac{b - a}{6}$ | $\sigma$: days | PERT |
| Project Variance (Critical Path) | $\sigma_{\text{proj}}^{2} = \displaystyle\sum_{i \in \text{CP}} \sigma_{i}^{2}$ | days$^2$ | PERT Probability |
| Probability Z-Value | $Z = \dfrac{T_{s} - T_{e}}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}}$ | Dimensionless | PERT Probability |
| Normal Probability | $P(T \leq T_{s}) = \Phi(Z)$ | Probability (0–1) | PERT Probability |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **CPM**: Used in **construction projects** (bridges, buildings, highways), **manufacturing plant shutdowns**, and **shipbuilding** where task durations are well-known from historical data.
- **PERT**: Used in **R&D projects** (new product development, aerospace, pharmaceutical trials), **defence projects**, and **software development** where uncertainty is high and activity durations cannot be precisely predicted.
- **Dummy Activities**: Used in any AOA network where complex logical dependencies must be modeled — e.g., electrical wiring in a skyscraper where "Lighting wiring" depends on both "Cable tray installation" and "Plaster completion", but other activities depend on only one of them.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Solved Example — CPM Forward and Backward Pass on an AOA Network

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Construct the AOA network for the following project and identify the Critical Path.

| Activity | Predecessors | Duration (days) |
|----------|--------------|-----------------|
| A | None | 4 |
| B | None | 6 |
| C | A | 5 |
| D | A | 3 |
| E | B, C | 7 |
| F | D | 4 |
| G | E, F | 5 |

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Network Construction (AOA Diagram)

```
        A(4)            C(5)
   1 ────────► 3 ────────► 5
                            ╲
                             ╲ E(7)
                              ╲
   2 ────────► 4 ──────────► 6 ──────────► 8
        B(6)        D(3)              G(5)        F(4)
                                ↘        ↗
                                 ╲      ╱
                                  ╲  ╱
                                   ▼
       (Nodes connected via dummy logic)
```

**Logic:**
- Activities A and B both start from node 1.
- C depends on A; D depends on A → both originate from the end of A.
- E depends on B and C → must wait until both B and C finish.
- F depends on D.
- G depends on E and F.

To handle E's dual dependency (B and C), we need the network to merge B's end and C's end before G. Similarly, F feeds into G. We introduce a merge node so that the start of G waits for both E and F.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Forward Pass (Earliest Times)

We begin at the Start node (Node 1) and propagate forward.

**Node 1 (Start):**
$$E_{1} = 0$$

**Node 2 (End of B):**
$$E_{2} = E_{1} + t_{B} = 0 + 6 = 6$$

**Node 3 (End of A):**
$$E_{3} = E_{1} + t_{A} = 0 + 4 = 4$$

**Node 4 (End of D):**
$$E_{4} = E_{3} + t_{D} = 4 + 3 = 7$$

**Node 5 (End of C):**
$$E_{5} = E_{3} + t_{C} = 4 + 5 = 9$$

**Node 6 (End of E — must wait for both B and C):**
$$E_{6} = \max(E_{2} + t_{E \text{ from B}}, E_{5} + t_{E \text{ from C}})$$
$$E_{6} = \max(6 + 7, 9 + 7) = \max(13, 16) = 16$$

Wait — let me reconsider the network. E depends on both B and C, so E's start requires completion of both B and C. Let me redraw properly:

- E starts from a node that has both B's end and C's end as predecessors. In AOA, that means the **start node of E** must have two incoming arrows: one from the end of B, one from the end of C.

So the network nodes are:
- Node 1: Start
- Node 2: End of B
- Node 3: End of A
- Node 4: End of D
- Node 5: End of C
- Node 6: End of E (start of G)
- Node 7: End of F (start of G)
- Node 8: End of G (Project End)

**Arcs:**
- $1 \xrightarrow{A(4)} 3$
- $1 \xrightarrow{B(6)} 2$
- $3 \xrightarrow{C(5)} 5$
- $3 \xrightarrow{D(3)} 4$
- $2 \xrightarrow{\text{dummy}(0)} 6$  (B's end feeds into E's start)
- $5 \xrightarrow{E(7)} 6$  (C ends, then E begins)
- $4 \xrightarrow{F(4)} 7$
- $6 \xrightarrow{\text{dummy}(0)} 8$  (E ends, feeds G's start)
- $7 \xrightarrow{\text{dummy}(0)} 8$  (F ends, feeds G's start)
- $8 \xrightarrow{G(5)} \text{ProjectEnd}$

**Recompute Forward Pass:**

$$E_{1} = 0$$
$$E_{2} = E_{1} + 6 = 6$$
$$E_{3} = E_{1} + 4 = 4$$
$$E_{5} = E_{3} + 5 = 9$$
$$E_{4} = E_{3} + 3 = 7$$
$$E_{6} = \max(E_{2} + 0, E_{5} + 7) = \max(6, 16) = 16$$
$$E_{7} = E_{4} + 4 = 11$$
$$E_{8} = \max(E_{6} + 0, E_{7} + 0) = \max(16, 11) = 16$$
$$E_{\text{end}} = E_{8} + 5 = 21$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Backward Pass (Latest Times)

We start at the Project End and move backward.

$$L_{\text{end}} = 21$$
$$L_{8} = L_{\text{end}} - 5 = 16$$
$$L_{6} = L_{8} - 0 = 16$$
$$L_{7} = L_{8} - 0 = 16$$
$$L_{2} = L_{6} - 0 = 16$$
$$L_{5} = L_{6} - 7 = 9$$
$$L_{4} = L_{7} - 4 = 12$$
$$L_{3} = \min(L_{5} - 5, L_{4} - 3) = \min(9 - 5, 12 - 3) = \min(4, 9) = 4$$
$$L_{1} = \min(L_{2} - 6, L_{3} - 4) = \min(16 - 6, 4 - 4) = \min(10, 0) = 0$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Slack and Critical Path

| Activity | Duration | $E_{i}$ | $E_{j}$ | $L_{i}$ | $L_{j}$ | $TS_{ij} = L_{j} - E_{i} - t_{ij}$ | Critical? |
|----------|----------|---------|---------|---------|---------|----------|-----------|
| A (1→3) | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | $4 - 0 - 4 = 0$ | ✅ Yes |
| B (1→2) | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 16 | $16 - 0 - 6 = 10$ | ❌ No |
| C (3→5) | 5 | 4 | 9 | 4 | 9 | $9 - 4 - 5 = 0$ | ✅ Yes |
| D (3→4) | 3 | 4 | 7 | 4 | 12 | $12 - 4 - 3 = 5$ | ❌ No |
| Dummy (2→6) | 0 | 6 | 16 | 16 | 16 | $16 - 6 - 0 = 10$ | ❌ No |
| E (5→6) | 7 | 9 | 16 | 9 | 16 | $16 - 9 - 7 = 0$ | ✅ Yes |
| F (4→7) | 4 | 7 | 11 | 12 | 16 | $16 - 7 - 4 = 5$ | ❌ No |
| Dummy (6→8) | 0 | 16 | 16 | 16 | 16 | $16 - 16 - 0 = 0$ | ✅ Yes |
| Dummy (7→8) | 0 | 11 | 16 | 16 | 16 | $16 - 11 - 0 = 5$ | ❌ No |
| G (8→End) | 5 | 16 | 21 | 16 | 21 | $21 - 16 - 5 = 0$ | ✅ Yes |

**Critical Path: A → C → E → G** (1 → 3 → 5 → 6 → 8 → End)

**Project Duration: 21 days**

---

## 3.2 Solved Example — PERT Probability Calculation

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A small PERT network has the following activities on its critical path:

| Activity | $a$ (days) | $m$ (days) | $b$ (days) |
|----------|------------|------------|------------|
| P | 2 | 3 | 10 |
| Q | 1 | 4 | 7 |
| R | 3 | 5 | 13 |

The management wants to know the probability of completing the project in **16 days or less**.

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Calculate Expected Time for Each Activity

**For Activity P:**

$$t_{e,P} = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6} = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 10}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$

**For Activity Q:**

$$t_{e,Q} = \frac{1 + 4(4) + 7}{6} = \frac{1 + 16 + 7}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$

**For Activity R:**

$$t_{e,R} = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Calculate Variance for Each Activity

**For Activity P:**

$$\sigma_{P}^{2} = \left(\frac{b - a}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{10 - 2}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{16}{9} \approx 1.778$$

**For Activity Q:**

$$\sigma_{Q}^{2} = \left(\frac{7 - 1}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{6}{6}\right)^{2} = 1^{2} = 1$$

**For Activity R:**

$$\sigma_{R}^{2} = \left(\frac{13 - 3}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{25}{9} \approx 2.778$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Project Expected Time and Variance

**Expected Project Duration (sum of expected times along critical path):**

$$T_{e} = t_{e,P} + t_{e,Q} + t_{e,R} = 4 + 4 + 6 = 14 \text{ days}$$

**Project Variance:**

$$\sigma_{\text{proj}}^{2} = \sigma_{P}^{2} + \sigma_{Q}^{2} + \sigma_{R}^{2} = \frac{16}{9} + 1 + \frac{25}{9} = \frac{16 + 9 + 25}{9} = \frac{50}{9} \approx 5.556$$

**Project Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_{\text{proj}} = \sqrt{5.556} \approx 2.357 \text{ days}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Calculate Z-Value

**Target completion time:** $T_{s} = 16$ days

$$Z = \frac{T_{s} - T_{e}}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}} = \frac{16 - 14}{2.357} = \frac{2}{2.357} \approx 0.849$$

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Probability from Standard Normal Table

From the **standard normal distribution table**, for $Z = 0.85$:

$$P(Z \leq 0.85) \approx 0.8023$$

So, the probability of completing the project within 16 days is approximately **80.23%**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — CPM vs. PERT

| Parameter | CPM | PERT |
|-----------|-----|------|
| Time estimates | Single deterministic value | Three estimates ($a$, $m$, $b$) |
| Distribution | None (assumed known) | Beta distribution |
| Focus | Time-cost trade-off | Time uncertainty / probability |
| Network orientation | Activity-based (AOA or AON) | Event-oriented |
| Crashing | Allows activity crashing with cost | Generally not used |
| Typical use | Construction, manufacturing, well-defined projects | R&D, defence, research, new product dev |
| Output | Critical path & project duration | Critical path, expected duration, probability of completion |
| Slack / Float | Computed and used for scheduling | Used similarly but with probabilistic interpretation |
| Origin | DuPont & Remington Rand (1956–1958) | U.S. Navy Polaris Project (1958) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 AOA Network Schematic — Generic Project with Dummies

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **generic structure of an AOA network** with dummy activities demonstrating the redundancy / precedence-preservation concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start((Start\nNode 1))
    Node2((Node 2))
    Node3((Node 3))
    Node4((Node 4))
    Node5((Node 5))
    Node6((Node 6))
    EndN((End\nNode 7))

    Start -- "A = 4 days" --> Node2
    Start -- "B = 6 days" --> Node3
    Node2 -- "C = 5 days" --> Node4
    Node2 -- "D = 3 days" --> Node5
    Node3 -. "Dummy 1 = 0 days" .-> Node4
    Node4 -- "E = 7 days" --> Node6
    Node5 -- "F = 4 days" --> Node6
    Node6 -- "G = 5 days" --> EndN

    classDef critical fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef dummy fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#808080,stroke-dasharray: 5 5,color:#555555
    classDef normal fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000

    class Node2,Node4,Node6,EndN critical
    class Node3 dummy
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **Yellow-filled nodes** represent the *critical path nodes*.
- **Grey dashed activity** (Start → Node 3 → Node 4) is the **dummy activity** — no actual work, only logical dependency.
- The path through **Start → Node 2 → Node 4 → Node 6 → End** is one logical sequence; the dummy ensures *Activity E* (Node 4 → Node 6) waits for both *Activity A* and *Activity B* to complete.

---

## 4.2 CPM Workflow — Block-Level Architecture

This block diagram maps the **functional workflow** of performing a CPM analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Scope\nand Activity List] --> B[Identify\nPrecedence Relations]
    B --> C[Construct AOA\nNetwork Diagram]
    C --> D[Assign Activity\nDurations]
    D --> E[Forward Pass\nCalculate EST/EFT]
    E --> F[Backward Pass\nCalculate LST/LFT]
    F --> G[Compute Slack\nfor each Activity]
    G --> H{Slack = 0?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Add to Critical Path]
    H -- No --> J[Non-Critical Activity]
    I --> K[Final Critical Path\nand Project Duration]
    J --> K

    classDef input fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef process fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class A,B input
    class C,D,E,F,G process
    class H decision
    class I,J,K output
```

**Key Process Steps:**

1. **Input Gathering**: Project scope, activities, durations.
2. **Logical Modeling**: Precedence relations, dummy activity introduction.
3. **Diagramming**: AOA construction with proper event numbering.
4. **Quantitative Passes**: Forward and backward calculations.
5. **Slack Analysis**: Identify critical activities.
6. **Output**: Final critical path and minimum project duration.

---

## 4.3 PERT Probability Evaluation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Collect Three Time Estimates\nOptimistic a\nMost Likely m\nPessimistic b] --> P2[Compute Expected Time\nte = a + 4m + b divided by 6]
    P1 --> P3[Compute Variance\nsigma squared = b minus a squared divided by 36]
    P2 --> P4[Identify Critical Path\nvia Forward and Backward Pass]
    P4 --> P5[Sum te for CP\nGet Te]
    P3 --> P6[Sum variances for CP\nGet sigma proj squared]
    P5 --> P7[Compute Z Value\nZ equals Ts minus Te over sigma proj]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Look up Standard Normal Table\nFind Probability P]

    classDef input fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef calc fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class P1 input
    class P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 calc
    class P8 output
```

This block diagram encapsulates the entire **PERT probabilistic workflow** in a clean, traceable flow.

---

## 4.4 Concept Map — Network Construction to Critical Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Network Construction] --> N2[AOA Diagrams]
    N1 --> N3[AON Diagrams]
    N2 --> N4[Dummy Activities\nRedundancy Logic]
    N2 --> N5[Real Activities]
    N4 --> N6[Critical Path Method]
    N5 --> N6
    N6 --> N7[Forward Pass]
    N6 --> N8[Backward Pass]
    N7 --> N9[Slack Analysis]
    N8 --> N9
    N9 --> N10[Critical Path\nIdentified]
    N10 --> N11[PERT Extension]
    N11 --> N12[Probability of Completion]

    classDef root fill:#FFECB3,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef tech fill:#C5CAE9,stroke:#1A237E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outcome fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class N1 root
    class N2,N3,N4,N5,N6,N7,N8,N9 tech
    class N10,N11,N12 outcome
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Critical Pitfalls**
> 1. **Dummy Activities in Slack Calculation**: Many students forget to include dummy activities (with duration = 0) when computing total slack. Dummies can themselves be on the critical path! Losing this costs **2–3 marks** per question.
> 2. **PERT "4m Weighting"**: Students frequently write $t_e = (a + m + b)/3$ (arithmetic mean) instead of the correct Beta-distribution-weighted formula $(a + 4m + b)/6$. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Project Variance = Sum of Critical Path Variances Only**: A common error is summing variances of *all* activities, not just those on the critical path. Lose **2 marks**.
> 4. **Z-Value Sign Convention**: Confusion about $Z = (T_s - T_e)/\sigma$ vs. $Z = (T_e - T_s)/\sigma$. Always use the formula given in the textbook. Get the sign wrong, and your probability table lookup is reversed. Lose **2 marks**.
> 5. **Slack in AOA — Use Node Times**: In AOA, slack is computed on **activities**, not nodes. Using node earliest/latest times incorrectly is a frequent error.

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a dummy activity? Explain its purpose in AOA network construction with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **dummy activity** is a logical connector in an Activity-On-Arrow (AOA) network that has **zero duration, zero cost, and consumes no resources**. It is represented as a *dashed arrow* between two nodes.
>
> **Purpose:**
> Dummy activities are used to:
> 1. Show logical dependencies between activities that cannot otherwise be represented in AOA.
> 2. Distinguish between two activities having the same start and end events.
> 3. Maintain correct precedence relationships when activities start or end at common nodes but have different dependencies.
>
> **Example:** If Activity C depends on Activity A, and Activity D depends on Activity B (where A and B start simultaneously), but Activities C and D both end at the same event, a dummy is introduced to ensure C is preceded by A only, not B.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between CPM and PERT in project management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | CPM | PERT |
|-------|-----|------|
| Time Estimates | Single deterministic value | Three estimates (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) |
| Nature | Deterministic | Probabilistic |
| Application | Construction, repetitive projects | R&D, research, non-repetitive projects |
| Output | Critical path & duration | Critical path, expected duration, probability |
| Cost Aspect | Time-cost trade-off possible | Not primarily concerned with cost |

(Any 3 valid differences — full 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module 3 Internal Choice

### ⭐ OPTION A — 14 Marks

#### `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Set A]`

**Question (a) [7 marks]:**
A project consists of the following activities. Construct the **AOA network** and identify the **critical path**.

| Activity | Predecessors | Duration (weeks) |
|----------|--------------|------------------|
| A | — | 5 |
| B | — | 3 |
| C | A | 4 |
| D | B | 6 |
| E | C | 2 |
| F | C, D | 5 |
| G | E | 4 |
| H | F, G | 6 |

**Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — AOA Network Construction:**

```
Start(1) -- A(5) --> 3 -- C(4) --> 5 -- E(2) --> 7 -- G(4) --> 9
                       ╲                                            
                        ╲--> 4 -- F(5) --> 8 -- dummy(0) --> 9
Start(1) -- B(3) --> 2 -- D(6) --> 4                   
                                       ╲
                                        ╱
                  (D merges into F's start via node 4)
                                        H(6)
                            9 -- H(6) --> End(10)
```

Since E ends at node 7 and F ends at node 8, but G depends on E and H depends on both F and G, we need to merge: G's start node (7) feeds into H's start, and a dummy from 8 → 9 ensures H waits for F.

Nodes: 1 (start), 2 (end B), 3 (end A), 4 (end D, start F), 5 (end C, start E), 6 (end E), 7 (end G), 8 (end F), 9 (end H), 10 (project end).

Wait — let me re-examine. G depends only on E; H depends on F and G. So H's start must wait for F and G to complete. Both must end at the same node (H's start).

Let me redefine:
- Node 7 = End of G
- Node 8 = End of F
- Dummy from 7 → 9 (H's start) and Dummy from 8 → 9
- H goes from 9 → End

**Step 2 — Forward Pass:**

$$E_1 = 0, \quad E_2 = 0 + 3 = 3, \quad E_3 = 0 + 5 = 5$$
$$E_5 = E_3 + 4 = 9, \quad E_4 = E_2 + 6 = 9, \quad E_6 = E_5 + 2 = 11$$
$$E_8 = E_4 + 5 = 14, \quad E_7 = E_6 + 4 = 15, \quad E_9 = \max(E_7 + 0, E_8 + 0) = \max(15, 14) = 15$$
$$E_{10} = E_9 + 6 = 21$$

**Project Duration = 21 weeks.**

**Step 3 — Backward Pass:**

$$L_{10} = 21, \quad L_9 = 21 - 6 = 15, \quad L_7 = L_9 - 0 = 15, \quad L_8 = L_9 - 0 = 15$$
$$L_6 = L_7 - 4 = 11, \quad L_4 = L_8 - 5 = 10, \quad L_5 = L_6 - 2 = 9$$
$$L_3 = \min(L_5 - 4, L_4 - 0 \text{ from dummy if any}) = L_5 - 4 = 9 - 4 = 5$$
$$L_2 = L_4 - 6 = 10 - 6 = 4, \quad L_1 = \min(L_3 - 5, L_2 - 3) = \min(5 - 5, 4 - 3) = \min(0, 1) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Slack Computation:**

| Activity | Duration | $E_i$ | $E_j$ | $L_i$ | $L_j$ | $TS$ |
|----------|----------|-------|-------|-------|-------|------|
| A (1→3) | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | **0** ✅ |
| B (1→2) | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 4 | 1 |
| C (3→5) | 4 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 9 | **0** ✅ |
| D (2→4) | 6 | 3 | 9 | 4 | 10 | 1 |
| E (5→6) | 2 | 9 | 11 | 9 | 11 | **0** ✅ |
| F (4→8) | 5 | 9 | 14 | 10 | 15 | 1 |
| G (6→7) | 4 | 11 | 15 | 11 | 15 | **0** ✅ |
| Dummy (7→9) | 0 | 15 | 15 | 15 | 15 | **0** ✅ |
| Dummy (8→9) | 0 | 14 | 15 | 15 | 15 | 1 |
| H (9→End) | 6 | 15 | 21 | 15 | 21 | **0** ✅ |

**Critical Path: A → C → E → G → H** (with duration 5+4+2+4+6 = 21 weeks).

**(7 marks — full marks for correct network, forward/backward pass, slack, and critical path identification)**

---

**Question (b) [7 marks]:**
For the same network, calculate the **Free Slack** and **Total Slack** for each activity. Also, identify the **critical activities** and the **project duration**. Mention how free slack differs from total slack.

**Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Free Slack for activity (i, j):**

$$FS_{ij} = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$$

**Total Slack for activity (i, j):**

$$TS_{ij} = L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$$

Using values from the table above:

| Activity | $FS_{ij}$ | $TS_{ij}$ |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| A | $5 - 0 - 5 = 0$ | $5 - 0 - 5 = 0$ |
| B | $3 - 0 - 3 = 0$ | $4 - 0 - 3 = 1$ |
| C | $9 - 5 - 4 = 0$ | $9 - 5 - 4 = 0$ |
| D | $9 - 3 - 6 = 0$ | $10 - 3 - 6 = 1$ |
| E | $11 - 9 - 2 = 0$ | $11 - 9 - 2 = 0$ |
| F | $14 - 9 - 5 = 0$ | $15 - 9 - 5 = 1$ |
| G | $15 - 11 - 4 = 0$ | $15 - 11 - 4 = 0$ |
| Dummy (7→9) | $15 - 15 - 0 = 0$ | $15 - 15 - 0 = 0$ |
| Dummy (8→9) | $15 - 14 - 0 = 1$ | $15 - 14 - 0 = 1$ |
| H | $21 - 15 - 6 = 0$ | $21 - 15 - 6 = 0$ |

**Critical Activities** (TS = 0): **A, C, E, G, H** (and the dummy 7→9, which acts as part of the path).

**Project Duration: 21 weeks.**

**Difference between FS and TS:**
- **Total Slack** is the amount of time an activity can be delayed *without delaying the project completion date*. It uses the **latest** times.
- **Free Slack** is the amount of time an activity can be delayed *without delaying the early start of any successor activity*. It uses the **earliest** times of successors.
- $TS \geq FS$ always. They are equal only for activities on the critical path.

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Set B]`

**Question (a) [7 marks]:**
**Explain the PERT technique for project management. How is the expected time and variance of an activity computed? State the assumptions made in PERT.**

**Model Answer [7 marks]:**

**PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique)** is a probabilistic project management technique developed by the U.S. Navy in 1958 for the Polaris missile project. It is used when activity durations are uncertain.

**Three Time Estimates:**

1. **Optimistic time ($a$):** The minimum time required if everything goes perfectly.
2. **Most Likely time ($m$):** The time expected under normal conditions.
3. **Pessimistic time ($b$):** The maximum time required under worst conditions.

**Expected Time (Beta Distribution Approximation):**

$$t_e = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6}$$

The factor 4 is used because in the Beta distribution, the **most likely time is weighted four times** as compared to the extreme estimates.

**Variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{b - a}{6}\right)^2$$

**Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma = \frac{b - a}{6}$$

**Assumptions of PERT:**

1. Activity durations follow a **Beta distribution**.
2. The **critical path is normally distributed** (Central Limit Theorem).
3. Activity times are **statistically independent**.
4. The Beta distribution has a finite range between $a$ and $b$.
5. The project network is **deterministic in structure** (precedence is fixed).

**Procedure:**

1. Construct the AOA network.
2. For each activity, obtain $a$, $m$, $b$ from experts.
3. Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$ for each activity.
4. Identify the critical path using $t_e$ values (forward and backward pass).
5. Compute project variance by summing $\sigma^2$ of critical path activities.
6. Compute $Z$ and look up probability.

**(7 marks complete)**

---

**Question (b) [7 marks]:**
A PERT network has a critical path consisting of 4 activities with the following three-time estimates:

| Activity | Optimistic $a$ | Most Likely $m$ | Pessimistic $b$ |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| M | 3 | 5 | 13 |
| N | 2 | 4 | 12 |
| O | 4 | 6 | 14 |
| P | 1 | 3 | 11 |

Find the **expected project duration**, **project variance**, **project standard deviation**, and the **probability of completing the project in 22 days or less**.

**Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Expected Time for Each Activity:**

**Activity M:**

$$t_{e,M} = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}$$

**Activity N:**

$$t_{e,N} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 12}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 12}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}$$

**Activity O:**

$$t_{e,O} = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 14}{6} = \frac{4 + 24 + 14}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7 \text{ days}$$

**Activity P:**

$$t_{e,P} = \frac{1 + 4(3) + 11}{6} = \frac{1 + 12 + 11}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2 — Expected Project Duration:**

$$T_e = t_{e,M} + t_{e,N} + t_{e,O} + t_{e,P} = 6 + 5 + 7 + 4 = 22 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3 — Variance for Each Activity:**

$$\sigma_M^2 = \left(\frac{13 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{100}{36} \approx 2.778$$

$$\sigma_N^2 = \left(\frac{12 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{100}{36} \approx 2.778$$

$$\sigma_O^2 = \left(\frac{14 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{100}{36} \approx 2.778$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = \left(\frac{11 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{100}{36} \approx 2.778$$

**Step 4 — Project Variance:**

$$\sigma_{\text{proj}}^2 = \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_N^2 + \sigma_O^2 + \sigma_P^2 = \frac{400}{36} = \frac{100}{9} \approx 11.111$$

**Step 5 — Project Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_{\text{proj}} = \sqrt{11.111} \approx 3.333 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6 — Z-Value:**

$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}} = \frac{22 - 22}{3.333} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Probability:**

From the standard normal table, for $Z = 0$:

$$P(Z \leq 0) = 0.5000$$

**The probability of completing the project in 22 days or less is 50%.**

**(7 marks complete)**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Checklist — Read It the Night Before the KTU Exam**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- ✅ **AOA (Activity on Arrow)**: Arrows = activities, Nodes = events.
- ✅ **AON (Activity on Node)**: Nodes = activities, Arrows = dependencies.
- ✅ **Network Construction Rules**: No two activities between same pair of nodes; no loops; single start, single end.
- ✅ **Dummy Activity**: Zero duration, zero cost, dashed arrow; used for logical dependencies.
- ✅ **Redundancy**: Dummy activities introduced to handle redundant (parallel) activities with same start/end events.

### CPM Essentials
- ✅ **EST/EFT** = max of predecessor EFTs + duration (Forward Pass).
- ✅ **LST/LFT** = min of successor LSTs − duration (Backward Pass).
- ✅ **Total Slack** = $L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$.
- ✅ **Free Slack** = $E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$.
- ✅ **Critical Path** = Path with Total Slack = 0 for all activities.
- ✅ **Project Duration** = Earliest time at the End Node.

### PERT Essentials
- ✅ Three estimates: **Optimistic (a), Most Likely (m), Pessimistic (b)**.
- ✅ Expected time: $t_e = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$ (**NOT** arithmetic mean).
- ✅ Variance: $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{b - a}{6}\right)^2$.
- ✅ Project variance = **sum of variances of critical path activities only**.
- ✅ Z-value: $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}}$.
- ✅ Probability from standard normal table.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ⚠️ Don't forget to include dummy activities in slack calculations.
- ⚠️ Don't use arithmetic mean instead of Beta distribution weighted mean.
- ⚠️ Don't sum variances of all activities — only critical path.
- ⚠️ Don't confuse free slack with total slack (TS ≥ FS always).
- ⚠️ Don't forget $T_e$ of critical path determines the Z-value's reference.

### Memory Hooks
- 🧠 **"EF-LS minus duration gives you the slack of your life"** → $TS = L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$.
- 🧠 **"Forward to the start, Backward to the end"** → Forward pass starts at Start, Backward pass ends at End.
- 🧠 **"Optimistic-Pessimistic bracket, divided by six — that's how variance kicks"** → $\sigma^2 = ((b-a)/6)^2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling Computations, PERT Time Estimates, Probability of Completion of Project, Crashing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET504 - MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scheduling Computations, PERT Time Estimates, Probability of Completion of Project, Crashing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Scheduling Computations

**Scheduling Computations** in Project Management refer to the systematic mathematical procedure used to determine the start times, finish times, and available slacks for every activity and event in a project network (AOA — Activity on Arrow, or AON — Activity on Node). The primary objective is to numerically evaluate the network so that the **Critical Path** (the longest-duration path that dictates the minimum project duration) can be identified unambiguously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Scheduling computations* is the process of computing Earliest Start (ES), Earliest Finish (EF), Latest Start (LS), Latest Finish (LF), and Slack/Float for each activity by performing a **Forward Pass** and a **Backward Pass** through the project network.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a 10-day road trip from Kerala to Delhi with multiple family members traveling in convoy. Each person must wait for the slower vehicle at major junctions. *Scheduling computations* act like the convoy coordinator who:
- Walks **forward** through the route, calculating *when each checkpoint can be reached at the earliest* (Forward Pass).
- Walks **backward** from the destination, calculating *the latest time each checkpoint can be reached without delaying the trip* (Backward Pass).
- A checkpoint that **must** be hit on time (no waiting slack) becomes the "Critical Path" — any delay here delays the entire convoy.

### Key Metrics Used

| Metric | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Earliest Start Time** | **ES** | The earliest time an activity can begin |
| **Earliest Finish Time** | **EF** | The earliest time an activity can be completed |
| **Latest Start Time** | **LS** | The latest time an activity can begin without delaying the project |
| **Latest Finish Time** | **LF** | The latest time an activity can finish without delaying the project |
| **Total Float / Slack** | **TF** | LF − EF (or LS − ES), indicates scheduling flexibility |

---

## 1.2 PERT Time Estimates

**PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique)** is a probabilistic project scheduling model developed by the U.S. Navy in 1958 for the Polaris Missile Project. Unlike CPM (Critical Path Method) which uses a *single deterministic* time estimate, PERT uses **three separate time estimates** for each activity to capture the inherent uncertainty in real-world project execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three PERT Time Estimates:**
> 1. **Optimistic Time ($t_o$)** — Duration under the most favourable conditions.
> 2. **Most Likely Time ($t_m$)** — Duration under normal working conditions (modal value).
> 3. **Pessimistic Time ($t_p$)** — Duration under the worst-case conditions.
>
> These three estimates feed into a Beta Probability Distribution to yield the **Expected Time ($t_e$)** and the **Variance ($\sigma^2$)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

When you ask three friends, *"How long will it take to drive from Kochi to Bengaluru?"*, one says *6 hours* (optimistic), another says *9 hours* (most likely — peak traffic), and the third says *14 hours* (pessimistic — flat tyre + rain). PERT mathematically combines these subjective judgments into one *weighted average* expected duration, giving the project manager a realistic, risk-aware schedule.

---

## 1.3 Probability of Completion of Project

The **Probability of Completion** quantifies the statistical likelihood that a project will be finished by a *targeted* (deadline) date, given the probabilistic nature of the PERT durations. It transforms a deterministic-looking schedule into a *risk-informed* decision-making tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The project completion time ($T_E$) follows an approximate **Normal Distribution** (by Central Limit Theorem) with:
> - Mean = sum of expected times along the critical path
> - Standard Deviation = square root of the sum of variances along the critical path
>
> The standard normal variable **Z-score** is computed and probability is read from standard normal tables.

---

## 1.4 Crashing (Project Compression)

**Crashing** is the deliberate compression of project duration by adding extra resources (overtime labour, additional manpower, faster equipment) to critical activities. Each activity has a **Normal Time** (without crashing) and a **Crash Time** (minimum achievable duration) along with respective costs. The trade-off between time saved and extra cost incurred is quantified by the **Cost Slope**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Consider a dish at a restaurant. The chef normally cooks it in 30 minutes using one stove. If you tell the manager, *"I need it in 15 minutes!"*, the manager uses *two stoves* and *two cooks* (extra cost), but the dish arrives faster. *Crashing* in projects works the same way — speed costs money, and the manager must decide *how much speed is worth the money*.

> [!WARNING]
> Crashing should **only** be applied to **Critical Path activities**. Crashing a non-critical activity is a waste of money because it does not reduce the project duration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scheduling Computations — Forward & Backward Pass

### A. Forward Pass (Compute ES and EF)

The forward pass begins at the **Start Node (Event 1)** and proceeds toward the **End Node**. It calculates the earliest times for every event and activity.

- For **Start Event:** $E_1 = 0$
- For any **subsequent event j**: the Earliest Expected Time ($E_j$) is the **maximum** of (Earliest Expected Time of predecessor event + duration of connecting activity).

$$E_j = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} \left[ E_i + t_{ij} \right]$$

- For an activity $(i, j)$:
  - $ES_{ij} = E_i$
  - $EF_{ij} = E_i + t_{ij}$

### B. Backward Pass (Compute LS and LF)

The backward pass begins at the **End Node** and proceeds toward the **Start Node**. It calculates the latest times for every event and activity.

- For **End Event:** $L_n = E_n$ (where $n$ is the terminal node; project duration is $E_n$).
- For any **predecessor event i**: the Latest Allowable Time ($L_i$) is the **minimum** of (Latest Allowable Time of successor event − duration of connecting activity).

$$L_i = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} \left[ L_j - t_{ij} \right]$$

- For an activity $(i, j)$:
  - $LF_{ij} = L_j$
  - $LS_{ij} = L_j - t_{ij}$

### C. Slack / Float Computations

The **Total Float (TF)** measures how long an activity can be delayed without extending the project duration.

$$TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - t_{ij} = LS_{ij} - ES_{ij} = LF_{ij} - EF_{ij}$$

The **Free Float (FF)** measures how long an activity can be delayed without delaying any subsequent activity.

$$FF_{ij} = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path Identification Rule:** Activities where $TF = 0$ are on the Critical Path. The Critical Path is the longest path through the network.

---

## 2.2 PERT Time Estimates — Theory & Formulae

PERT models each activity's duration as a **Beta Distribution** because the Beta family can accommodate a wide variety of skewed shapes — perfect for real-world activity times.

### A. Expected Time ($t_e$)

The weighted mean of the Beta distribution:

$$t_e = \frac{t_o + 4 t_m + t_p}{6}$$

### B. Standard Deviation ($\sigma$) and Variance ($\sigma^2$)

For a Beta distribution with mode $t_m$, the variance is:

$$\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2$$

$$\sigma = \frac{\vert t_p - t_o \vert}{6}$$

### C. Project Variance & Standard Deviation (Critical Path)

Since activities along the critical path are approximately independent:

$$\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = \sum_{k \in \text{CP}} \sigma_k^2$$

$$\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{project}}^2}$$

---

## 2.3 Probability of Completion

### A. Z-score (Standard Normal Variable)

To find the probability that the project is completed in time $\leq T_s$ (scheduled target date):

$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$$

where $T_e$ is the sum of $t_e$ values along the critical path.

### B. Probability Lookup

Using the standard normal table:
- $P(Z \leq Z_{\text{computed}})$ gives the cumulative probability of completion within $T_s$.
- $P(Z > Z_{\text{computed}}) = 1 - P(Z \leq Z_{\text{computed}})$ gives the probability of overrun.

---

## 2.4 Crashing — Theory & Formulae

### A. Cost Slope

The **Cost Slope** (or Crash Cost per unit time) for an activity:

$$\text{Cost Slope} = \frac{C_{\text{crash}} - C_{\text{normal}}}{T_{\text{normal}} - T_{\text{crash}}}$$

Units: **Rupees per day** (or cost per unit time).

### B. Crashing Strategy (KTU Standard Algorithm)

1. Compute normal project duration and total cost (Normal Cost + Indirect Cost).
2. Identify the critical path (lowest-cost-summing constraint).
3. On the critical path, select the activity with the **least cost slope** that is **crashable** ($T_{\text{normal}} > T_{\text{crash}}$) and not yet fully crashed.
4. Crash the activity by **one day** (or minimum increment).
5. Recalculate network; new paths may become critical.
6. Stop when crashing cost exceeds the indirect cost saved → **Optimal Duration**.

### C. Total Project Cost Equation

$$\text{Total Cost} = \text{Direct Cost (Normal/Crashed)} + \text{Indirect Cost (Overhead per day} \times \text{Duration)}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Symbol/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Forward Pass Event Time | $E_j = \max_i (E_i + t_{ij})$ | Days |
| Backward Pass Event Time | $L_i = \min_j (L_j - t_{ij})$ | Days |
| Earliest Start (Activity) | $ES = E_i$ | Days |
| Earliest Finish (Activity) | $EF = ES + t$ | Days |
| Latest Finish (Activity) | $LF = L_j$ | Days |
| Latest Start (Activity) | $LS = LF - t$ | Days |
| Total Float | $TF = LS - ES = LF - EF$ | Days |
| Free Float | $FF = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$ | Days |
| PERT Expected Time | $t_e = (t_o + 4 t_m + t_p)/6$ | Days |
| PERT Variance (Activity) | $\sigma^2 = [(t_p - t_o)/6]^2$ | Days$^2$ |
| Project Variance (CP) | $\sigma_{\text{proj}}^2 = \sum \sigma_k^2$ | Days$^2$ |
| Project Std. Deviation | $\sigma_{\text{proj}} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_k^2}$ | Days |
| Z-score | $Z = (T_s - T_e) / \sigma_{\text{proj}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Cost Slope | $(C_{\text{crash}} - C_{\text{normal}}) / (T_{\text{normal}} - T_{\text{crash}})$ | Rs/Day |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in civil construction (bridge, metro, highway), aerospace (satellite launch), software development (Agile sprints with PERT for risk), and manufacturing (assembly line scheduling) projects.

---

## 2.6 Why This Theory Matters in Engineering

In a real **construction engineering project**, the Project Manager must:
1. Draw the network (AOA/AON) → identify dependencies.
2. Perform forward/backward pass → find critical path (delay here = project delay).
3. Apply PERT → get probabilistic durations (realistic estimates).
4. Compute probability → *commit* to client with risk-adjusted confidence.
5. Crash selectively → finish early to earn incentive/penalty avoidance.

This unified workflow is the **core competency tested by KTU** in Module 3.
