<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define motivation. State its three core elements. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Motivation is the psychological process that arouses, directs, and sustains human behavior toward achieving goals.
The three core elements are:
1. **Arousal** — triggering the energy to act.
2. **Direction** — channeling the behavior toward a specific goal.
3. **Persistence** — continuing the effort over time despite obstacles.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Use the words "arouse, direct, sustain" — they are the exact KTU keywords and the examiner's checkpoint markers.

### Question 2: Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation. Give one workplace example each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Dimension | Intrinsic Motivation | Extrinsic Motivation |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Source | Internal (within person) | External (outside person) |
| Driver | Curiosity, mastery, passion | Salary, bonus, promotion, awards |
| Sustainability | Long-lasting | Often short-term |
| Example | Engineer codes a new algorithm for the joy of solving it | Engineer codes overtime to earn overtime pay |

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Explain Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs theory. Discuss its relevance in motivating employees in an IT company. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Maslow's theory (1943) proposes a five-tier pyramid of human needs. The lower-level needs must be reasonably satisfied before higher-level needs become dominant motivators.

**The Five Levels:**
1. **Physiological Needs** — food, water, salary, rest.
2. **Safety Needs** — job security, safe workplace, insurance, predictable future.
3. **Social Needs** — friendship, team belonging, group affiliation.
4. **Esteem Needs** — recognition, status, achievement, self-respect.
5. **Self-Actualization Needs** — creativity, personal growth, reaching one's full potential.

**Logical explanation of prepotency:** A hungry employee cannot focus on self-actualization. Once the salary is adequate (physiological and safety met), social belonging and recognition (esteem) become active motivators.

**Relevance in an IT Company (Application):**
- IT firms (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) provide competitive salaries, free meals, and transport to satisfy physiological and safety needs.
- Team outings, welcome kits, and buddy programs address social needs.
- "Star Performer" awards, employee-of-the-month certificates, and appraisal ratings satisfy esteem needs.
- Hackathons, conference sponsorships, and R&D projects enable self-actualization.

**Merits:** Simple to understand, intuitive, helps managers design layered reward systems.
**Limitations:** Needs may not always follow a strict hierarchy (an artist may starve for their art); cultural variations exist; the theory is hard to test empirically.

**(b) Critically evaluate Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory. How is it used in job enrichment? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (1959) emerged from research on engineers and accountants. He found that:
- **Hygiene factors** (extrinsic) such as salary, working conditions, and company policy prevent dissatisfaction but do not produce satisfaction.
- **Motivators** (intrinsic) such as achievement, recognition, responsibility, and the work itself create true satisfaction.

**Critical Evaluation:**

**Merits:**
- Introduced the revolutionary idea that satisfaction and dissatisfaction are *not* opposite ends of a single scale but two independent dimensions.
- Provides a clear, actionable framework for managers — first fix hygiene, then add motivators.
- Inspired the *job enrichment* movement.

**Limitations:**
- The methodology (critical incident technique) is criticized for reliability issues.
- The sample was limited to engineers, accountants, and professionals — generalization is limited.
- It assumes universal application of motivators, ignoring individual differences.

**Application in Job Enrichment:**
Job enrichment (vertical job loading) means deliberately adding motivators to jobs. Example: A data-entry clerk is given:
- Responsibility for a complete client file (responsibility motivator).
- Direct client interaction (achievement motivator).
- Authority to recommend solutions (recognition motivator).
- Skill variety and growth opportunities (growth motivator).

**Industrial Example:** Tata Consultancy Services uses job rotation and stretch assignments to enrich engineering roles, while ensuring hygiene factors (salary, infrastructure) are baseline.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain Vroom's Expectancy Theory of motivation with a suitable example. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Vroom's Expectancy Theory (1964) is a cognitive process theory arguing that motivation is the product of three beliefs:

$$
M = E \times I \times V
$$

Where:
- **E (Expectancy):** Belief that effort will lead to performance ($0 \leq E \leq 1$).
- **I (Instrumentality):** Belief that performance will lead to a reward ($0 \leq I \leq 1$).
- **V (Valence):** Value the person places on the reward ($-1 \leq V \leq +1$).

**Logical Explanation:**
A person calculates — "Will my effort actually translate into good performance? Will good performance actually get me the reward? And do I really want that reward?" If any one factor is zero, motivation is zero.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A junior engineer is deciding whether to work overtime on a project.

- **E = 0.8** (she believes working extra hours will let her complete the project on time).
- **I = 0.7** (she believes the project manager actually rewards on-time delivery with a good rating).
- **V = 0.9** (she highly values the good rating as it leads to a promotion).

$$
M = 0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 = 0.504
$$

This represents a moderate-to-high motivation level. If instrumentality drops to 0.3 (because the manager is known to reward favorites, not performance), then:

$$
M = 0.8 \times 0.3 \times 0.9 = 0.216
$$

Motivation collapses by more than half. **This demonstrates the leverage of instrumentality.**

**Key Takeaway:** Managers must ensure *clear* effort-performance-reward linkages, *transparent* performance appraisal, and *valued* rewards to maximize motivation.

**(b) Discuss Adams' Equity Theory. What are the remedies for perceived inequity? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Adams' Equity Theory (1965) is based on social comparison. An individual compares his input-output ratio with that of a referent (peer, colleague, industry benchmark).

**The Core Equation:**

$$
\frac{O_{\text{self}}}{I_{\text{self}}} \;\overset{?}{=}\; \frac{O_{\text{referent}}}{I_{\text{referent}}}
$$

**Three Possible States:**
1. **Equity** — ratios are equal. Motivation is stable.
2. **Underpayment Inequity** — self-ratio is less. Employee feels angry and demotivated.
3. **Overpayment Inequity** — self-ratio is greater. Employee may feel guilty and temporarily increase effort.

**Workplace Example:**
Two mechanical engineers, Ajith and Binu, have the same qualification and experience. Ajith gets promoted to team lead; Binu doesn't. Binu experiences underpayment inequity, reduces his extra contributions, becomes absent frequently, and eventually leaves.

**Remedies for Perceived Inequity:**
1. **Change Inputs** — Reduce effort, attendance, or extra hours.
2. **Change Outcomes** — Negotiate for higher pay, better role, or perks.
3. **Change Perception of Comparison** — Cognitively reframe the situation.
4. **Change the Referent** — Compare with a different peer group.
5. **Leave the Situation** — Resign or transfer (most extreme but common).
6. **Act on the Referent** — Try to change the comparison person's outcomes (rare and unethical).

**Managerial Implication:** Organizations must maintain transparent, fair, and consistent compensation systems. Equity audits, clear job evaluations, and objective performance metrics are essential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Negative Reinforcement with Punishment** — Always distinguish: NR removes something unpleasant; Punishment delivers an aversive consequence. This costs 2 marks in a 7-mark question.
> 2. **Listing Maslow's needs without the prepotency logic** — Mere list = 2 marks; explaining *why* lower needs must be met first = 7 marks.
> 3. **Writing Vroom's formula without a numerical example** — Examiners in 2024 actively look for applied calculation. Always include a worked numerical.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply theories to engineering scenarios** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards context-specific application, especially in manufacturing/IT scenarios.
> 5. **Mixing up Maslow and Herzberg** — Maslow is a hierarchy of *needs*; Herzberg is a distinction between *satisfaction and dissatisfaction*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Motivation = Arousal + Direction + Persistence** — the *core 3 elements*.
- [ ] The motivation cycle is **Need → Tension → Drive → Behavior → Goal → Satisfaction → New Need**.
- [ ] **Maslow**: 5 levels, prepotency principle, lower needs first.
- [ ] **Herzberg**: Hygiene (extrinsic) + Motivators (intrinsic) — two independent dimensions, not opposites.
- [ ] **Vroom**: $M = E \times I \times V$ — if any factor is zero, motivation is zero.
- [ ] **Adams**: Compare input-output ratio with referent. Three states — equity, underpayment, overpayment.
- [ ] **McGregor**: Theory X (negative) vs. Theory Y (positive) — two managerial mindsets. Add Theory Z (Ouchi) for bonus marks.
- [ ] **Skinner**: Four reinforcement strategies. Negative Reinforcement ≠ Punishment.
- [ ] Always **apply theories to engineering/IT/manufacturing scenarios** in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] For 14-mark questions, structure as: **Definition → Explanation → Diagram/Equation → Engineering Application → Merits → Limitations**.
- [ ] Remember the **Mnemonic "HET-MAE"**: **H**erzberg, **E**xpectancy (Vroom), **T**wo-Factor → **M**aslow, **A**dams (Equity), **E**xtrinsic/Intrinsic.
- [ ] Modern KTU 2024 answers should mention **intrinsic motivation for knowledge workers** for higher order thinking marks.
- [ ] Draw **at least one diagram** (Maslow pyramid or Vroom model) in every 14-mark answer to score full graphics marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations Management: Introduction, Concept of Productivity and Its Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET504 - MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operations Management: Introduction, Concept of Productivity and Its Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Operations Management – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Operations Management (OM)** is the branch of management that deals with designing, planning, directing, and controlling the activities concerned with the transformation of inputs (materials, labour, capital, information) into outputs (goods and services) to achieve the organization's goals efficiently and effectively.

In KTU parlance, **Operations** refers to the *value-creating activities* of any organization — whether a manufacturing plant producing bolts, an IT firm writing code, or a hospital treating patients. **Management** of these operations is what ensures the transformation process is *productive*, *cost-effective*, and *quality-driven*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Kitchen Restaurant"

> [!NOTE]
> 🍳 **The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy**
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen as an **operating system** of a business:
> - **Inputs** = Raw vegetables, spices, chefs (labour), gas/electricity (energy), kitchen equipment (capital), recipes (information).
> - **Transformation Process** = Cooking, plating, garnishing.
> - **Outputs** = Served dishes to customers.
> - **Operations Manager** = The Head Chef, who plans the menu, schedules the chefs, ensures quality of taste, controls waste, and delivers food *on time*.
>
> If the Head Chef orders too much, **waste increases** (low productivity). If the kitchen is too slow, **customers leave** (low service efficiency). The art of balancing this *Input → Process → Output* system is exactly what **Operations Management** does at an industrial scale.

### 1.3 Scope of Operations Management (KTU Board Favourite)

The scope, as per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, encompasses the following functional areas:

| S.No | Functional Area | Core Activity |
|------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Product Design & Development** | Deciding what to produce and its features |
| 2 | **Process Selection & Design** | Choosing the right technology & workflow |
| 3 | **Facility Location & Layout** | Where to set up and how to arrange |
| 4 | **Production Planning & Control (PPC)** | What, when, and how much to produce |
| 5 | **Inventory Management** | Balancing stock levels (raw, WIP, finished) |
| 6 | **Quality Management (TQM, Six Sigma)** | Ensuring conformance to standards |
| 7 | **Maintenance Management** | Uptime of machines & equipment |
| 8 | **Supply Chain & Logistics** | Movement of materials |
| 9 | **Operations Strategy** | Long-term competitive alignment |

### 1.4 Key Characteristics of Operations

- **Output is tangible (goods) or intangible (services)**
- **High capital investment** is involved
- **Time utility & place utility** are created
- **Subject to variability** (demand, supply, machine breakdowns)
- **Measurable** — outputs can be quantified
- **Systematic** — follows the **Input → Process → Output** model

### 1.5 The Productivity Concept – The Heart of This Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of Productivity (KTU Standard):**
> **Productivity** is the quantitative relationship between what is produced (output) and what is required to produce it (input). It is a measure of **how efficiently** an organization converts its inputs into outputs.
>
> In symbolic form, the most basic productivity definition is:
> $$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

#### 1.5.1 Intuitive Analogy – "The Study Hours vs. Marks Graph"

> [!NOTE]
> 📚 **The Student Analogy for Productivity**
> Consider a B.Tech student preparing for the KTU exam:
> - **Input** = Number of study hours, number of books read, money spent on coaching.
> - **Output** = Marks scored, concepts understood.
> - If a student studies **100 hours** and scores **90 marks**, productivity = 90/100 = **0.9 marks/hour**.
> - Another student studies **100 hours** and scores **70 marks**, productivity = 70/100 = **0.7 marks/hour**.
>
> The first student is **more productive**. Productivity isn't about working *harder* (more input), it's about working *smarter* (more output per input). This is the same principle a manager uses to judge workers, machines, departments, and entire companies.

### 1.6 Why Productivity Matters in KTU Context

- **India's manufacturing sector** productivity directly affects GDP.
- **Service industries** (BPO, IT, banking) measure productivity in *transactions per agent per hour*.
- **Engineers as future managers** must understand productivity because they will be responsible for process improvement, lean manufacturing, and Six Sigma projects.

### 1.7 Types of Productivity – KTU Classification

| S.No | Type of Productivity | Formula / Description |
|------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Partial Productivity** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Single Input}}$ (e.g., output per labour hour) |
| 2 | **Total (Multi-Factor) Productivity** | $\text{TFP} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{All Inputs Combined}}$ |
| 3 | **Labour Productivity** | Output per worker or per labour-hour |
| 4 | **Capital Productivity** | Output per rupee of capital invested |
| 5 | **Material Productivity** | Output per kg/tonne of raw material |
| 6 | **Machine Productivity** | Output per machine-hour |
| 7 | **Energy Productivity** | Output per unit of energy consumed |
| 8 | **Land Productivity** | Output per hectare (in agriculture) |
| 9 | **Total Factor Productivity (TFP)** | Output / (Labour + Capital + Materials + Energy) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Operations System – The Transformation Model

Every operations system can be broken into a **closed-loop transformation model**. The KTU textbook (PBMET504 prescribed content) defines it as:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Operations System Equation:**
> $$\text{Inputs} \xrightarrow{\text{Transformation Process}} \text{Outputs}$$

But this is incomplete. The *real* operations system has **feedback** built in:

```
Inputs → Transformation Process → Outputs → Feedback
   ↑                                            ↓
   └──────────── Corrective Action ←───────────┘
```

**Components explained:**

1. **Inputs** – The "5M's" plus Information
   - **Man** (Labour / Human Resources)
   - **Machine** (Equipment, Tools)
   - **Material** (Raw materials, components)
   - **Money** (Capital, Finance)
   - **Method** (Process, Technology)
   - **Measurement** (Information, Data for control)

2. **Transformation Process** – The actual conversion
   - Physical (manufacturing)
   - Chemical (refining, pharma)
   - Transport (logistics)
   - Service (consulting, banking)

3. **Outputs** – The finished goods or services that have *value* to the customer.

4. **Feedback** – Information on quality, defect rates, customer satisfaction used to *correct* the system.

## 2.2 Productivity – The Multi-Layered Concept

Productivity is **not a single number**. It is a multi-dimensional concept. The KTU syllabus demands students distinguish between:

### 2.2.1 Partial Productivity (Single-Factor)

The most commonly used measure in industry. It is **easy to compute** and **easy to understand**, but **may be misleading** because it ignores other inputs.

$$\text{Partial Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{One specific Input}}$$

**Common partial productivities:**

$$\text{Labour Productivity} = \frac{\text{Total Output (units)}}{\text{Total Labour Input (man-hours)}}$$

$$\text{Machine Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output (units)}}{\text{Machine Hours Used}}$$

$$\text{Material Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output (units)}}{\text{Material Consumed (kg)}}$$

$$\text{Capital Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output (Rs.)}}{\text{Capital Employed (Rs.)}}$$

### 2.2.2 Total Productivity (Multi-Factor)

This is a **holistic measure**. It accounts for *all* major inputs.

$$\text{Total Productivity (TP)} = \frac{\text{Total Output}}{\text{Labour + Capital + Material + Energy + Other Inputs}}$$

In practice, since adding different units (man-hours + rupees + kg) is meaningless, **monetary value** is used to express both output and input:

$$\text{TP} = \frac{\text{Total Monetary Value of Output}}{\text{Total Monetary Value of All Inputs}}$$

### 2.2.3 Total Factor Productivity (TFP)

A refinement of TP where only the **primary factors of production** (labour and capital) are considered. This is widely used in *macroeconomics* and is the basis of national productivity indices.

$$\text{TFP} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Combined Input of Labour and Capital}}$$

TFP growth = Real Output Growth − (Labour share × Labour input growth) − (Capital share × Capital input growth)

## 2.3 Productivity Index – The KTU Favourite Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Definition (Board-Standard):**
> **Productivity Index** is a ratio that compares the productivity of the *current period* with the productivity of the *base period* (or standard). It indicates the percentage change in productivity.

$$\boxed{\text{Productivity Index} = \frac{\text{Productivity in Current Period}}{\text{Productivity in Base Period}} \times 100}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If **PI > 100** → Productivity has **improved** (good for management).
- If **PI = 100** → Productivity **unchanged**.
- If **PI < 100** → Productivity has **declined** (warning sign).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|--------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Basic Productivity | $\text{P} = \dfrac{\text{O}}{\text{I}}$ | Output units per Input unit | Single output, single input |
| 2 | Labour Productivity | $\text{LP} = \dfrac{\text{O}}{\text{Labour hours}}$ | Units / Man-hr | Factory floor, service ops |
| 3 | Machine Productivity | $\text{MP} = \dfrac{\text{O}}{\text{Machine hr}}$ | Units / Machine-hr | CNC, batch production |
| 4 | Material Productivity | $\text{MatP} = \dfrac{\text{O}}{\text{Material (kg)}}$ | Units / kg | Process industries |
| 5 | Capital Productivity | $\text{CapP} = \dfrac{\text{Output (Rs)}}{\text{Capital (Rs)}}$ | Ratio | Investment decisions |
| 6 | Total Productivity | $\text{TP} = \dfrac{\text{Total Output Value}}{\text{Total Input Value}}$ | Ratio | Whole-organisation measure |
| 7 | Productivity Index | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{\text{P}_{\text{current}}}{\text{P}_{\text{base}}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Year-on-year comparison |
| 8 | Productivity Improvement % | $\Delta\text{P\%} = \dfrac{\text{P}_{\text{new}} - \text{P}_{\text{old}}}{\text{P}_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Improvement projects |
| 9 | Multi-Factor Productivity | $\text{MFP} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}_1 + \text{Input}_2 + \ldots}$ | Ratio (monetary) | Comparing departments |
| 10 | Value-Added Productivity | $\text{VAP} = \dfrac{\text{Value Added}}{\text{Total Input}}$ | Ratio | Lean manufacturing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Matter in KTU Answers:** If output is in *units* and labour is in *man-hours*, the productivity unit is *units/man-hour*. Examiners *do* check units. Always write the unit, e.g., "tonnes / man-hour" or "₹ / ₹".

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications of Productivity

1. **Manufacturing Plants** – A Toyota plant measures *vehicles per worker per day*.
2. **Software Companies (Infosys, TCS)** – Measures *lines of validated code per developer-day* or *story points per sprint*.
3. **Power Plants (NTPC, KSEB)** – Measures *kWh generated per tonne of coal* (energy productivity).
4. **Construction (L&T, Gammon India)** – Measures *cubic metres of concrete poured per crew-day*.
5. **Agriculture** – *Tonnes of rice per hectare* (land productivity).
6. **Hospitals (Apollo, KIMS)** – *Patients treated per doctor per day*.

## 2.6 Factors Affecting Productivity (KTU Board Expected Answer)

**A. Internal Factors (within the firm's control):**

1. **Technology & Automation** – CNC machines, robotics, AI.
2. **Workforce Skill & Training** – Skilled workers are 2-3× more productive.
3. **Work Methods & Process Design** – Lean, Six Sigma, 5S.
4. **Motivation & Morale** – Higher morale = higher output.
5. **Product Design** – Designs that are *Design for Manufacturing (DFM)* reduce assembly time.
6. **Quality of Inputs** – Better raw materials → less rework.
7. **Plant Layout & Workflow** – Good layout reduces motion waste.
8. **Management Style** – Participative vs. autocratic.
9. **Capital Investment** – Modern machines increase output per worker.
10. **Inventory & Material Handling** – JIT systems reduce waiting.

**B. External Factors (outside the firm's control):**

1. **Government Policies & Regulations** – Labour laws, environmental norms.
2. **Infrastructure** – Power supply, roads, port connectivity.
3. **Economic Conditions** – Recessions reduce demand → idle capacity.
4. **Trade Unions & Industrial Relations** – Strikes & lockouts.
5. **Natural Factors** – Monsoon, earthquakes, pandemics.
6. **Global Competition** – Forces firms to innovate or perish.

## 2.7 Ways to Improve Productivity (Engineering-Oriented)

1. **Work Simplification** – Eliminate unnecessary steps in a process.
2. **Method Study & Motion Study** – As taught in Industrial Engineering.
3. **Training & Skill Development** – Human capital investment.
4. **Investment in Technology** – CAD/CAM, FMS, CIM.
5. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** – Keep machines running.
6. **Total Quality Management (TQM)** – Defects = rework = wasted input.
7. **Incentive Wage Systems** – Piece-rate, Halsey, Rowan plans.
8. **Better Working Conditions** – Ergonomics, lighting, ventilation.
9. **Just-in-Time (JIT)** – Eliminate inventory waste.
10. **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)** – The Japanese philosophy of small, daily improvements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Productivity Index Relationship

The **Productivity Index** is derived from the basic productivity concept, applied across two time periods. Let us derive it step by step.

### Step 1: Define Productivity in Each Period

Let $P_b$ be the productivity in the base period (e.g., Year 0).

$$P_b = \frac{O_b}{I_b}$$

Where:
- $O_b$ = Output in base period
- $I_b$ = Input in base period

### Step 2: Define Productivity in the Current Period

Let $P_c$ be the productivity in the current period (e.g., Year 1).

$$P_c = \frac{O_c}{I_c}$$

### Step 3: Define the Productivity Index

The **Productivity Index (PI)** is defined as the ratio of current productivity to base productivity, expressed as a percentage.

$$PI = \frac{P_c}{P_b} \times 100$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Definitions

Substituting $P_c$ and $P_b$ from Steps 1 and 2:

$$PI = \frac{\frac{O_c}{I_c}}{\frac{O_b}{I_b}} \times 100$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Compound Fraction

$$PI = \frac{O_c}{I_c} \times \frac{I_b}{O_b} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{PI = \frac{O_c \times I_b}{I_c \times O_b} \times 100}$$

### Step 6: Derive the Percentage Change Formula

The percentage change in productivity, $\Delta P\%$, is given by:

$$\Delta P\% = PI - 100$$

$$\Delta P\% = \left(\frac{P_c - P_b}{P_b}\right) \times 100$$

### Step 3.2 — Worked Example 1 (Partial Productivity)

> **Problem:** A manufacturing unit produces **5,000 units** in a month. The total labour input is **2,000 man-hours**. The same unit, in the previous year, produced **4,000 units** with **2,500 man-hours**. Calculate:
> (a) Labour productivity in both periods
> (b) Productivity Index
> (c) Percentage change in productivity
> (d) Comment on the result.

#### Solution:

**Part (a): Labour Productivity**

$$P_{\text{current}} = \frac{O_c}{I_c} = \frac{5000 \text{ units}}{2000 \text{ man-hr}} = 2.5 \text{ units / man-hr}$$

$$P_{\text{base}} = \frac{O_b}{I_b} = \frac{4000 \text{ units}}{2500 \text{ man-hr}} = 1.6 \text{ units / man-hr}$$

**Part (b): Productivity Index**

$$PI = \frac{P_c}{P_b} \times 100 = \frac{2.5}{1.6} \times 100 = 156.25$$

**Part (c): Percentage Change**

$$\Delta P\% = \frac{P_c - P_b}{P_b} \times 100 = \frac{2.5 - 1.6}{1.6} \times 100 = 56.25\%$$

**Part (d): Comment**

> The labour productivity has **improved by 56.25%**. The same workforce now produces 56.25% more units per man-hour. This indicates successful implementation of training, better methods, or improved technology.

### Step 3.3 — Worked Example 2 (Total Productivity with Monetary Values)

> **Problem:** An engineering firm produced output worth **₹ 50,00,000** in 2023 (base year) and **₹ 75,00,000** in 2024. The total input costs were:
> - 2023: Labour ₹ 20,00,000; Materials ₹ 10,00,000; Capital ₹ 15,00,000
> - 2024: Labour ₹ 22,00,000; Materials ₹ 12,00,000; Capital ₹ 16,00,000
>
> Calculate Total Productivity for both years and the Productivity Index.

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Total Input 2023**

$$I_{2023} = 20{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 = 45{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Total Input 2024**

$$I_{2024} = 22{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: Total Productivity 2023**

$$TP_{2023} = \frac{O_{2023}}{I_{2023}} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{45{,}00{,}000} = 1.111$$

**Step 4: Total Productivity 2024**

$$TP_{2024} = \frac{O_{2024}}{I_{2024}} = \frac{75{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} = 1.500$$

**Step 5: Productivity Index**

$$PI = \frac{TP_{2024}}{TP_{2023}} \times 100 = \frac{1.500}{1.111} \times 100 = 135.01$$

**Interpretation:** Total productivity has **risen by ~35%** despite input costs going up by 11%. The firm has become significantly more efficient.

### Step 3.4 — Worked Example 3 (Multi-Factor Productivity)

> **Problem:** A factory uses 3 inputs: Labour = 500 hrs @ ₹ 50/hr, Materials = ₹ 30,000, Capital = ₹ 50,000. Output = 1,000 units worth ₹ 150 each. Find Total Factor Productivity (TFP).

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Total Output (Monetary Value)**

$$O = 1000 \times 150 = 1{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 2: Total Input (Monetary Value)**

$$I = (500 \times 50) + 30{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 25{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 1{,}05{,}000$$

**Step 3: TFP**

$$TFP = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}05{,}000} = 1.4286$$

**Interpretation:** Every ₹ 1 of input produces ₹ 1.43 of output — a healthy productivity ratio.

### 3.5 The Productivity Improvement Equation (Engineering Form)

In many KTU numerical problems, the question is: *"By how much should input reduce (or output increase) to achieve a target productivity index?"*

Let target productivity index = $PI_{\text{target}}$. We know:

$$PI_{\text{target}} = \frac{P_{\text{new}}}{P_{\text{old}}} \times 100$$

Rearranging for $P_{\text{new}}$:

$$P_{\text{new}} = \frac{PI_{\text{target}}}{100} \times P_{\text{old}}$$

If output is fixed at $O$ and we need to find new input $I_{\text{new}}$:

$$I_{\text{new}} = \frac{O}{P_{\text{new}}} = \frac{O}{\frac{PI_{\text{target}}}{100} \times P_{\text{old}}}$$

If input is fixed at $I$ and we need to find new output $O_{\text{new}}$:

$$O_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{new}} \times I = \frac{PI_{\text{target}}}{100} \times P_{\text{old}} \times I$$

### 3.6 KTU Exam-Style Numerical Template (Master Template)

For any productivity problem, follow this **5-step analytical matrix**:

| Step | Action | What You Compute |
|------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Identify** | What is output? What is input? What time period? |
| 2 | **Choose** | Which productivity? Partial or Total? |
| 3 | **Compute** | $P = O / I$ for each period |
| 4 | **Compare** | $PI = (P_c / P_b) \times 100$ |
| 5 | **Conclude** | Interpretation in one line + recommendation |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Q: Define Operations Management. List any four of its key functions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Operations Management is the discipline that plans, designs, directs, and controls the resources and activities required to transform inputs into goods and services that provide value to customers.

**Four Key Functions:**

1. **Product Design & Development** – Specifying the goods/services to be produced.
2. **Process Planning** – Selecting the right technology, equipment, and workflow.
3. **Production Planning & Control (PPC)** – Determining quantity, timing, and routing of production.
4. **Quality Management** – Ensuring products meet defined quality standards (TQM, Six Sigma).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**Q: Differentiate between Partial Productivity and Total Productivity with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Partial Productivity | Total Productivity |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Definition** | Output per *one* specific input | Output per *all* combined inputs |
| **Inputs used** | Single (e.g., labour) | Multiple (labour + material + capital + energy) |
| **Units** | Output units per input unit | Ratio of monetary values |
| **Ease of measurement** | Easy | Difficult (requires monetary conversion) |
| **Insight** | Narrow — focuses on one resource | Holistic — captures overall efficiency |
| **Example** | Units produced per man-hour | Total revenue / (Labour cost + Material cost + Capital cost) |
| **Drawback** | May mislead (e.g., high labour productivity but high material wastage) | Hides individual input inefficiencies |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> Common mistakes students make that **cost 2-3 marks each**:
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Productivity without units is incomplete. Always write "units / man-hour" or "₹ / ₹".
> 2. **Confusing Productivity Index with Output Increase %** — PI is *productivity* change, not *output* change.
> 3. **Mixing base and current periods** — Always use $P_{\text{base}}$ as the denominator.
> 4. **Ignoring interpretation** — Every numerical answer *must* end with a one-line interpretation in context.
> 5. **Not writing the formula first** — Always write $P = O/I$ and $PI = (P_c/P_b) \times 100$ before substituting.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A. (a)** Define Productivity. Explain the various types of productivity measures used in an industrial organization. **(7 Marks)**

**A. (b)** A company produced 12,000 units in 2023 using 4,000 man-hours and ₹ 8,00,000 worth of materials. In 2024, it produced 15,000 units using 4,500 man-hours and ₹ 9,00,000 worth of materials. Calculate:
   (i) Labour productivity for both years
   (ii) Material productivity for both years
   (iii) Productivity Index based on labour productivity
   (iv) Comment on the productivity trend. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**A. (a) — Definition & Types (7 Marks)**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Productivity is the ratio of output produced to the input consumed in the process of production. It measures the efficiency with which inputs are converted into outputs.

$$P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

**Types of Productivity Measures (5 Marks):**

1. **Partial Productivity** — Output per unit of a *single* input.
   - Examples: Labour productivity, Material productivity, Machine productivity, Capital productivity, Energy productivity.

2. **Total Productivity (Multi-Factor)** — Output per *combined* input (typically expressed in monetary value).
   - Example: Total monetary output / Total monetary input.

3. **Total Factor Productivity (TFP)** — Output per combined input of *primary factors* (labour and capital). Used in macro-economic analysis.

4. **Value-Added Productivity** — Value added (Output − Input material cost) / Total input.

5. **Service Productivity** — In service industries, measured as *transactions per employee per hour*.

---

**A. (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | 2023 (Base) | 2024 (Current) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Output (units) | 12,000 | 15,000 |
| Labour (man-hours) | 4,000 | 4,500 |
| Materials (₹) | 8,00,000 | 9,00,000 |

**(i) Labour Productivity (2 Marks):**

$$LP_{2023} = \frac{12{,}000}{4{,}000} = 3.0 \text{ units / man-hour}$$

$$LP_{2024} = \frac{15{,}000}{4{,}500} = 3.33 \text{ units / man-hour}$$

**(ii) Material Productivity (2 Marks):**

$$MatP_{2023} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ units}}{8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}} = 0.015 \text{ units per ₹}$$

$$MatP_{2024} = \frac{15{,}000 \text{ units}}{9{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}} = 0.01667 \text{ units per ₹}$$

**(iii) Productivity Index based on Labour Productivity (2 Marks):**

$$PI_{\text{Labour}} = \frac{LP_{2024}}{LP_{2023}} \times 100 = \frac{3.33}{3.0} \times 100 = 111.11$$

**(iv) Comment (1 Mark):**

> The labour productivity has improved by 11.11% and material productivity has improved by ~11.11% as well. The company is showing **healthy productivity growth**, possibly due to better training, technology adoption, or process improvements. Management should continue investing in such initiatives.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**B. (a)** Explain the concept of Productivity. Discuss in detail the internal and external factors affecting productivity in an industrial organization. **(7 Marks)**

**B. (b)** An automobile plant produced 8,000 cars in 2023 with the following inputs: Labour = 1,60,000 man-hours @ ₹ 100/hr, Materials = ₹ 50 crores, Capital = ₹ 30 crores. In 2024, output increased to 9,500 cars and inputs were: Labour = 1,80,000 man-hours @ ₹ 110/hr, Materials = ₹ 55 crores, Capital = ₹ 32 crores. Calculate:
   (i) Total Productivity for both years
   (ii) Productivity Index
   (iii) Percentage change in productivity. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**B. (a) — Concept & Factors (7 Marks)**

**Concept of Productivity (2 Marks):**
Productivity is a measure of the efficiency of production — how effectively an organization converts inputs into outputs. It is a *relative* measure, always expressed as a ratio. Productivity improvement is the cornerstone of organizational growth, profitability, and competitive advantage.

**Internal Factors (3 Marks):**

1. **Technology & Automation** — Use of CNC, robotics, AI.
2. **Workforce Skill & Training** — Skilled workers produce more with same resources.
3. **Work Methods & Process Design** — Lean, Six Sigma, 5S reduce waste.
4. **Motivation & Incentives** — Higher morale increases output.
5. **Product Design** — DFM/DFA reduce complexity.
6. **Plant Layout** — Reduces motion and transport waste.
7. **Quality Systems** — TQM reduces rework and wastage.
8. **Capital Investment** — Modern machines increase throughput.

**External Factors (2 Marks):**

1. **Government Policy** — Labour laws, taxes, subsidies.
2. **Infrastructure** — Power, transport, water supply.
3. **Economic Conditions** — Demand cycles, recession.
4. **Trade Unions** — Industrial relations, strikes.
5. **Natural Factors** — Monsoon, disasters, pandemics.
6. **Global Competition** — Market dynamics.

---

**B. (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | 2023 (Base) | 2024 (Current) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Output (cars) | 8,000 | 9,500 |
| Labour (man-hrs) | 1,60,000 | 1,80,000 |
| Labour rate (₹/hr) | 100 | 110 |
| Materials (₹) | 50,00,00,000 | 55,00,00,000 |
| Capital (₹) | 30,00,00,000 | 32,00,00,000 |

**(i) Total Productivity (3 Marks):**

**Total Input 2023:**

$$I_{2023} = (1{,}60{,}000 \times 100) + 50{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$I_{2023} = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 81{,}60{,}00{,}000$$

**Total Input 2024:**

$$I_{2024} = (1{,}80{,}000 \times 110) + 55{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 32{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$I_{2024} = 1{,}98{,}00{,}000 + 55{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 32{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 88{,}98{,}00{,}000$$

**Total Productivity:**

$$TP_{2023} = \frac{8{,}000}{81{,}60{,}00{,}000} = 0.000000980 \text{ cars per ₹} = 9.80 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cars/₹}$$

$$TP_{2024} = \frac{9{,}500}{88{,}98{,}00{,}000} = 1.068 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cars/₹}$$

In ratio terms (with output valued at ₹ 10 lakh per car for illustration):
$$TP_{2023} = \frac{8000 \times 10{,}00{,}000}{81{,}60{,}00{,}000} = \frac{8000 \text{ crores}}{816 \text{ crores}} = 9.80$$

$$TP_{2024} = \frac{9500 \times 10{,}00{,}000}{88{,}98{,}00{,}000} = \frac{9500 \text{ crores}}{889.8 \text{ crores}} = 10.68$$

**(ii) Productivity Index (2 Marks):**

$$PI = \frac{TP_{2024}}{TP_{2023}} \times 100 = \frac{10.68}{9.80} \times 100 = 108.98$$

**(iii) Percentage Change (2 Marks):**

$$\Delta P\% = \frac{TP_{2024} - TP_{2023}}{TP_{2023}} \times 100 = \frac{10.68 - 9.80}{9.80} \times 100 = 8.98\%$$

**Interpretation (in step 4):**

> The automobile plant's total productivity has increased by **8.98%** — a healthy sign. Despite an 18.75% rise in input costs, output grew by 18.75%, but the productivity gain shows that input cost growth was more than offset by output growth. The firm should continue investing in process improvement.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 🚀 Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points (Print This!)

**1. Core Definitions**
- ✅ **Operations Management** = Planning, organizing, controlling of transformation of inputs → outputs.
- ✅ **Productivity** = Output / Input (efficiency measure).
- ✅ **Productivity Index** = $(P_{\text{current}} / P_{\text{base}}) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Value-Added** = Output Value − Input Material Cost.

**2. The 5M + 1 Inputs**
- ✅ Man, Machine, Material, Money, Method, Measurement/Information.

**3. Types of Productivity (must remember all 9)**
- ✅ Partial (Labour, Machine, Material, Capital, Energy, Land).
- ✅ Total Productivity (Monetary value basis).
- ✅ Total Factor Productivity (Labour + Capital only).
- ✅ Service Productivity (per transaction).

**4. PI Interpretation**
- ✅ PI > 100 → Improvement. PI = 100 → No change. PI < 100 → Decline.

**5. Formula Mastery**
- ✅ $P = O/I$
- ✅ $PI = (P_c / P_b) \times 100$
- ✅ $\Delta P\% = (P_{\text{new}} - P_{\text{old}})/P_{\text{old}} \times 100$
- ✅ $TFP = O / (\text{Labour} + \text{Capital})$

**6. Factors (3 Internal + 3 External)**
- ✅ Internal: Technology, Skill, Method, Layout, Quality, Motivation.
- ✅ External: Government, Infrastructure, Economy, Unions, Nature, Global Competition.

**7. Improvement Techniques**
- ✅ Lean, Six Sigma, 5S, Kaizen, TQM, TPM, JIT, Work Study, Incentives, Ergonomics.

**8. Common KTU Pitfalls**
- ❌ Forgetting units → Lose 0.5–1 mark.
- ❌ Confusing PI with output growth % → Lose 2 marks.
- ❌ Mixing base and current periods → Lose 2 marks.
- ❌ Not writing interpretation → Lose 1 mark.
- ❌ No formula written before substitution → Lose 1 mark.

**9. Real-World Connect (Examiner's Favourite Question)**
- ✅ Be ready with **one** example each from manufacturing, service, agriculture, IT, and healthcare.

**10. Key Engineering Connect**
- ✅ Engineers must understand productivity because they design processes — the *transformation* step. Better process design = higher productivity = better engineering.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET504 - MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — FORECASTING: MOVING AVERAGE & WEIGHTED MOVING AVERAGE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Forecasting?

> [!NOTE]
> **Forecasting** is the systematic process of estimating future values of a business variable (such as demand, sales, revenue, inventory consumption, or production output) based on **historical data, trend analysis, and statistical models.** It forms the backbone of planning, scheduling, capacity decisions, and supply chain coordination in any engineering or business enterprise.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, forecasting belongs to the family of **Quantitative Time-Series Forecasting Techniques**, where past observations of a variable are used to project its future behavior under the assumption that **pattern in the past will continue into the future** (a concept known as *persistence* of pattern).

### 1.2 Classification of Forecasting Methods

| Category | Sub-Type | Examples |
|----------|----------|----------|
| **Qualitative Methods** | Judgmental | Delphi Technique, Market Research, Executive Opinion |
| **Quantitative Methods** | Time-Series | Moving Average, Weighted Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, Trend Analysis |
| **Quantitative Methods** | Associative / Causal | Regression, Econometric Models, Leading Indicators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 4 of PBMET504 explicitly focuses on **Time-Series Quantitative Forecasting** — specifically the **Simple Moving Average (SMA)** and **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** methods. These are classified under the **Naïve / Averaging family** of forecasting models.

### 1.3 What is a Moving Average?

> [!NOTE]
> **Moving Average (MA)** is a time-series forecasting technique in which the forecast for the next period is computed as the **arithmetic mean of the actual demand (or sales) of the most recent `n` periods.** As new data becomes available, the oldest observation is *dropped* and the newest one is *added* — hence the term "moving."

**Mathematical Form:**

$$F_{t+1} \;=\; \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2} + \cdots + D_{t-n+1}}{n} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i}$$

where:
- $F_{t+1}$ = Forecast for the next period (period $t+1$)
- $D_t$ = Actual demand in the most recent period
- $n$ = Number of periods included in the average (called the *order* or *span* of the moving average)
- $D_{t-n+1}$ = Oldest period included in the average

### 1.4 What is a Weighted Moving Average?

> [!NOTE]
> **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** is a refined version of the simple moving average in which **different weights are assigned to past observations** based on their relative importance. Typically, **more recent observations receive higher weights** because they are more representative of current conditions.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$F_{t+1} \;=\; w_1 D_t + w_2 D_{t-1} + w_3 D_{t-2} + \cdots + w_n D_{t-n+1}$$

subject to the **weight constraint:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \;=\; w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + \cdots + w_n \;=\; 1$$

and $w_1 \geq w_2 \geq w_3 \geq \cdots \geq w_n \geq 0$ (typically).

### 1.5 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Kerala Monsoon Memory" Model:**
> Imagine you are a farmer in Thrissur trying to predict next year's rainfall. You don't blindly take the rainfall of the *last 30 years combined and divide*. Instead, you intuitively feel that the **rainfall of last year matters more** than the rainfall from 15 years ago. So you mentally do this:
> - Simple Moving Average = "Average the rainfall of the last, say, **5 years** equally."
> - Weighted Moving Average = "Average the last 5 years, but give **double importance to the most recent year**, 1.5× to the year before, and so on."
> 
> That mental calculation is precisely what a Moving Average / Weighted Moving Average does — **it treats the past as data and weights recent memory more heavily (in WMA).**

### 1.6 Why Are These Methods Important in Engineering Management?

- **Production Planning:** Deciding how many units to manufacture next quarter.
- **Inventory & Supply Chain:** Setting safety stock and reorder points.
- **Workforce Planning:** Hiring decisions based on demand projections.
- **Capacity Expansion:** Whether to invest in a new plant or machinery.
- **Cost Reduction:** Avoiding overproduction and stockouts through accurate forecasting.
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme PBMET504 module stresses that **engineers are decision-makers**, and forecasting provides the *quantitative backbone* for those decisions. Both **Moving Average** and **Weighted Moving Average** are explicitly listed as high-weightage topics for Part B (14-mark) questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind Moving Averages

The fundamental idea is rooted in **smoothing the time-series**. Real demand data contains:
1. **Random fluctuations (Noise)** — short-term, unpredictable variations.
2. **Trend** — long-term upward or downward movement.
3. **Cyclical patterns** — medium-term oscillations.
4. **Seasonality** — fixed, periodic patterns (e.g., Onam sales spike every year).

A **moving average filters out the noise** and reveals the underlying pattern. The logic operates as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Logic of Moving Average**
> - **Step 1:** Identify the value of `n` (the number of periods to average). Common choices: $n = 3, 4, 5$ for quarterly/monthly data.
> - **Step 2:** Add the actual demand of the most recent `n` periods.
> - **Step 3:** Divide the sum by `n` to obtain the forecast for the *next* period.
> - **Step 4:** When a new period's actual demand becomes available, **slide the window forward by one period**, dropping the oldest and adding the newest.
> - **Step 5:** Repeat the procedure to generate rolling forecasts.

### 2.2 Why Use a Weighted Moving Average Instead?

The simple moving average **treats the most recent period and the oldest period in the window as equally important**. This is rarely true in reality. In most business scenarios, **the most recent observation carries the highest information value**.

**WMA corrects this by:**
- Assigning a **larger weight ($w_1$)** to the most recent demand.
- Assigning **progressively smaller weights** to older demands.
- Ensuring the sum of weights equals **1 (i.e., 100 %)**.

This makes the WMA **more responsive to recent changes** in demand patterns.

### 2.3 The Choice of `n` (Number of Periods)

| Value of `n` | Effect on Forecast |
|--------------|--------------------|
| **Small n (e.g., 2 or 3)** | Forecast is **highly responsive** to recent changes; less smooth; reacts quickly to trend reversals. |
| **Large n (e.g., 12)** | Forecast is **heavily smoothed**; stable but slow to react to recent changes. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students select `n` arbitrarily. The KTU valuation key penalizes answers that do not justify the choice of `n`. In your exam answer, **always state the trade-off** between smoothness and responsiveness when choosing `n`.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE (Print & Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Condition |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Simple Moving Average (SMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}D_{t-i}$ | $F_{t+1}$ = forecast, $D$ = actual demand, $n$ = number of periods |
| 2 | **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i D_{t-i+1}$ | Weights satisfy $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$ |
| 3 | **Weight Constraint (WMA)** | $w_1 + w_2 + \cdots + w_n = 1$ | $w_1 \geq w_2 \geq \cdots \geq w_n$ typically |
| 4 | **Forecast Error (eₜ)** | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $D_t$ = actual, $F_t$ = forecast |
| 5 | **Absolute Deviation** | $\vert e_t \vert = \vert D_t - F_t \vert$ | Always non-negative |
| 6 | **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** | $\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\vert e_t \vert$ | Lower MAD = better forecast |
| 7 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(e_t)^2$ | Penalizes large errors more |
| 8 | **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\frac{\vert e_t \vert}{D_t} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| 9 | **Cumulative Sum of Errors (CSE / Bias)** | $\text{CSE} = \sum e_t$ | Should ideally be near zero |
| 10 | **Tracking Signal** | $\text{TS} = \frac{\text{CSE}}{\text{MAD}}$ | Used to monitor forecast bias |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** A factory in Ernakulam producing ball-bearings uses 12-month MA to forecast monthly demand and plan raw-material procurement.
- **Power Sector:** KSEB uses weighted moving average of past 5 years' electricity consumption (with higher weight on recent years) to project summer peak load.
- **Retail & E-commerce:** Flipkart/Amazon-style platforms use weighted moving averages for daily sales forecasting per SKU.
- **Inventory Management:** EOQ (Economic Order Quantity) calculations require an accurate demand forecast — MA & WMA provide the simplest baseline.
- **Project Management:** Predicting the duration of repetitive engineering tasks (e.g., welding, casting) using historical time data.

### 2.6 Advantages & Limitations

> [!TIP]
> **Advantages of MA & WMA:**
> - Extremely **simple to compute** (no advanced software required).
> - **Easy to understand** by non-technical managers and workers.
> - **Smooths out random fluctuations** in demand.
> - Works well for **stable demand patterns** without strong trend.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitations of MA & WMA:**
> - **Requires historical data** for at least `n` periods.
> - **Cannot capture trend** (it averages trend out, in fact).
> - **Lags behind** actual turning points (always one period behind).
> - All periods (in SMA) or weighted (in WMA) are treated as the only input — ignores external factors (e.g., promotions, economic shifts).
> - WMA requires **subjective selection of weights** — no objective method exists.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Numerical Example — Simple Moving Average (SMA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]:**
> The following data represents the **quarterly sales (in lakhs of ₹) of a manufacturing company** in Kerala for the last 8 quarters. Using a **3-quarter Simple Moving Average**, forecast the sales for **Quarter 9**, and compute the **MAD, MSE, and MAPE** for the available forecast periods.

| Quarter | Actual Sales (Dₜ) in ₹ Lakhs |
|:-------:|:----------------------------:|
| Q1 | 100 |
| Q2 | 120 |
| Q3 | 130 |
| Q4 | 150 |
| Q5 | 170 |
| Q6 | 160 |
| Q7 | 180 |
| Q8 | 200 |
| Q9 | **?** (To be forecasted) |

### Step-by-Step Solution (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Identify n**

We are given that $n = 3$ (3-quarter moving average).

**Step 2 — Compute forecasts for Q4, Q5, Q6, Q7, Q8**

The first forecast we can make is for **Q4**, using actuals of Q1, Q2, Q3.

**Forecast for Q4:**

$$F_4 = \frac{D_1 + D_2 + D_3}{3} = \frac{100 + 120 + 130}{3} = \frac{350}{3} = 116.67 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

**Forecast for Q5:**

$$F_5 = \frac{D_2 + D_3 + D_4}{3} = \frac{120 + 130 + 150}{3} = \frac{400}{3} = 133.33 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

**Forecast for Q6:**

$$F_6 = \frac{D_3 + D_4 + D_5}{3} = \frac{130 + 150 + 170}{3} = \frac{450}{3} = 150.00 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

**Forecast for Q7:**

$$F_7 = \frac{D_4 + D_5 + D_6}{3} = \frac{150 + 170 + 160}{3} = \frac{480}{3} = 160.00 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

**Forecast for Q8:**

$$F_8 = \frac{D_5 + D_6 + D_7}{3} = \frac{170 + 160 + 180}{3} = \frac{510}{3} = 170.00 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the forecast for Q9 (the final required answer)**

$$F_9 = \frac{D_6 + D_7 + D_8}{3} = \frac{160 + 180 + 200}{3} = \frac{540}{3} = 180.00 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecast for Quarter 9 = ₹ 180 Lakhs**

**Step 4 — Build the complete analysis table**

| Quarter (t) | Actual $D_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Error $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ | $\frac{\vert e_t \vert}{D_t} \times 100$ |
|:-----------:|:------------:|:--------------:|:----------------------:|:-----------------:|:-------:|:--------------------------------------:|
| Q1 | 100 | — | — | — | — | — |
| Q2 | 120 | — | — | — | — | — |
| Q3 | 130 | — | — | — | — | — |
| Q4 | 150 | 116.67 | 33.33 | 33.33 | 1110.89 | 22.22 |
| Q5 | 170 | 133.33 | 36.67 | 36.67 | 1344.69 | 21.57 |
| Q6 | 160 | 150.00 | 10.00 | 10.00 | 100.00 | 6.25 |
| Q7 | 180 | 160.00 | 20.00 | 20.00 | 400.00 | 11.11 |
| Q8 | 200 | 170.00 | 30.00 | 30.00 | 900.00 | 15.00 |
| Q9 | — | **180.00** | — | — | — | — |
| **Sum** | | | **130.00** | **130.00** | **3855.58** | **76.15** |

**Step 5 — Compute MAD, MSE, MAPE**

Number of available forecast periods = 5 (Q4, Q5, Q6, Q7, Q8).

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{130.00}{5} = 26.00 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{\sum e_t^2}{n} = \frac{3855.58}{5} = 771.12 \;\text{(₹ Lakhs)}^2$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{D_t} \times 100 = \frac{76.15}{5} = 15.23 \;\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The forecast is, on average, off by about 15.23 % from the actual sales — a moderately accurate baseline. The MSE is high because the early forecast errors (Q4, Q5) were large.

---

## 3.2 Comprehensive Numerical Example — Weighted Moving Average (WMA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Using the same sales data as above, compute the **forecast for Q9** using a **3-quarter Weighted Moving Average** with weights:
> - $w_1 = 0.5$ (most recent — Q8)
> - $w_2 = 0.3$ (second most recent — Q7)
> - $w_3 = 0.2$ (oldest in window — Q6)
> 
> Verify the weight constraint and compute the **MAD**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify weight constraint**

$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute forecasts for Q4, Q5, Q6, Q7, Q8, and finally Q9**

**General formula:**

$$F_{t+1} = w_1 \cdot D_t + w_2 \cdot D_{t-1} + w_3 \cdot D_{t-2}$$

**Forecast for Q4** (using Q1, Q2, Q3 where Q3 is most recent):

$$F_4 = (0.5)(130) + (0.3)(120) + (0.2)(100) = 65 + 36 + 20 = 121.00$$

**Forecast for Q5** (using Q2, Q3, Q4):

$$F_5 = (0.5)(150) + (0.3)(130) + (0.2)(120) = 75 + 39 + 24 = 138.00$$

**Forecast for Q6** (using Q3, Q4, Q5):

$$F_6 = (0.5)(170) + (0.3)(150) + (0.2)(130) = 85 + 45 + 26 = 156.00$$

**Forecast for Q7** (using Q4, Q5, Q6):

$$F_7 = (0.5)(160) + (0.3)(170) + (0.2)(150) = 80 + 51 + 30 = 161.00$$

**Forecast for Q8** (using Q5, Q6, Q7):

$$F_8 = (0.5)(180) + (0.3)(160) + (0.2)(170) = 90 + 48 + 34 = 172.00$$

**Forecast for Q9** (using Q6, Q7, Q8):

$$F_9 = (0.5)(200) + (0.3)(180) + (0.2)(160) = 100 + 54 + 32 = \mathbf{186.00}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecast for Quarter 9 (WMA) = ₹ 186 Lakhs**

**Step 3 — Compute the analysis table for WMA**

| Quarter | Actual $D_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Error $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ |
|:-------:|:------------:|:--------------:|:-----------:|:-----------------:|:-------:|
| Q4 | 150 | 121.00 | 29.00 | 29.00 | 841.00 |
| Q5 | 170 | 138.00 | 32.00 | 32.00 | 1024.00 |
| Q6 | 160 | 156.00 | 4.00 | 4.00 | 16.00 |
| Q7 | 180 | 161.00 | 19.00 | 19.00 | 361.00 |
| Q8 | 200 | 172.00 | 28.00 | 28.00 | 784.00 |
| **Sum** | | | **112.00** | **112.00** | **3026.00** |

**Step 4 — Compute MAD for WMA**

$$\text{MAD}_{\text{WMA}} = \frac{112.00}{5} = 22.40 \;\text{₹ Lakhs}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison: SMA vs. WMA**

| Metric | SMA (n=3) | WMA (n=3) | Better Method |
|--------|:---------:|:---------:|:-------------:|
| Forecast for Q9 | 180.00 | 186.00 | WMA (closer to trend) |
| MAD | 26.00 | 22.40 | **WMA** ✓ |
| MSE | 771.12 | 605.20 | **WMA** ✓ |
| MAPE | 15.23 % | — | WMA ✓ |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** WMA outperforms SMA in this case because the data has a clear **upward trend**, and WMA's higher weight on the most recent observation tracks the trend better.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For KTU 14-Mark Answers)

| Dimension | Simple Moving Average (SMA) | Weighted Moving Average (WMA) |
|-----------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Weight Assignment** | All periods weighted equally ($1/n$ each) | Differentiated weights, sum to 1 |
| **Responsiveness** | Lower — older data gets same importance | Higher — recent data is prioritized |
| **Complexity** | Very simple | Slightly more complex |
| **Objectivity** | Fully objective (only `n` is chosen) | Weights are subjective |
| **Trend Capture** | Poor — lags by one period | Better — but still lagging |
| **Best Suited For** | Stable demand, no strong trend | Demand with mild-to-moderate trend |
| **Forecast Equation** | $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}D_{t-i}$ | $F_{t+1} = \sum w_i D_{t-i+1}$ |
| **Error Sensitivity** | Equal across periods | Sensitive to weight selection |
| **KTU Mark Weightage** | 7 marks typical | 7 marks typical |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Forecasting Process with Moving Averages

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end logical flow** of a moving-average based forecasting system, from data collection to forecast validation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HISTORICAL DEMAND DATA COLLECTION"] --> B["DATA CLEANING & ARRANGEMENT IN TIME ORDER"]
    B --> C{"SELECT FORECASTING METHOD"}
    C --> D["SIMPLE MOVING AVERAGE (SMA)"]
    C --> E["WEIGHTED MOVING AVERAGE (WMA)"]
    D --> F["CHOOSE VALUE OF n"]
    E --> G["ASSIGN WEIGHTS w1 to wn SUMMING TO 1"]
    F --> H["COMPUTE F(t+1) = SUM D(t-i) / n"]
    G --> I["COMPUTE F(t+1) = SUM wi * D(t-i+1)"]
    H --> J["RECORD FORECAST F(t+1)"]
    I --> J["RECORD FORECAST F(t+1)"]
    J --> K["ACTUAL DEMAND D(t+1) BECOMES AVAILABLE"]
    K --> L["COMPUTE ERROR e(t+1) = D(t+1) - F(t+1)"]
    L --> M["COMPUTE MAD, MSE, MAPE"]
    M --> N{"IS ACCURACY ACCEPTABLE?"}
    N -- "NO" --> O["ADJUST n OR WEIGHTS AND RECOMPUTE"]
    O --> C
    N -- "YES" --> P["DEPLOY FORECAST FOR PLANNING"]
    P --> Q["INVENTORY / PRODUCTION / WORKFORCE DECISIONS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at node A. Data is cleaned, the method is chosen (SMA or WMA), the forecast is computed, the actual demand is later observed, the error is calculated, and the model's accuracy is checked. If accuracy is poor, the loop restarts with adjusted parameters. This is the **continuous improvement cycle** of any forecasting system.

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram: SMA vs. WMA — Internal Block Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SMA["SIMPLE MOVING AVERAGE BLOCK"]
        D1["D(t)"] --> SUM1["SUMMATION BLOCK"]
        D2["D(t-1)"] --> SUM1
        D3["D(t-2)"] --> SUM1
        SUM1 --> DIV["DIVIDE BY n = 3"]
        DIV --> OUT1["FORECAST F(t+1) = 116.67"]
    end
    subgraph WMA["WEIGHTED MOVING AVERAGE BLOCK"]
        W1["D(t) x 0.5"] --> ADDSUM["WEIGHTED SUMMATION"]
        W2["D(t-1) x 0.3"] --> ADDSUM
        W3["D(t-2) x 0.2"] --> ADDSUM
        ADDSUM --> OUT2["FORECAST F(t+1) = 121.00"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow: How a New Data Point Updates the Moving Average

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["PERIOD t-2, t-1, t HAVE BEEN OBSERVED"] --> WIN["WINDOW CONTAINS: D(t-2), D(t-1), D(t)"]
    WIN --> FORECAST["FORECAST FOR PERIOD t+1 IS COMPUTED"]
    FORECAST --> NEW["NEW ACTUAL DATA D(t+1) ARRIVES"]
    NEW --> SLIDE["SLIDE WINDOW: REMOVE D(t-2), ADD D(t+1)"]
    SLIDE --> RECOMPUTE["RECOMPUTE FORECAST FOR PERIOD t+2"]
    RECOMPUTE --> LOOP["REPEAT INDEFINITELY"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note on Mermaid compilation:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`-style logic), no reserved keywords are used as labels, and no markdown formatting tags appear inside the quoted labels — ensuring the diagram renders cleanly in any Mermaid-compatible viewer.
