| **Free Samples** | Consumers | Short | Low (builds trial) | High | Launching a new product |
| **Coupons** | Consumers | Short–Medium | Medium (price dilution) | Medium | Stimulating repeat purchase |
| **Cash Refund** | Consumers | Short | Low | Medium | Switching from competitor |
| **Price-Off** | Consumers | Short | High (devalues brand) | Low | Stock clearance / off-season |
| **Premium (Gifts)** | Consumers | Short | Low | High | Loyalty reinforcement |
| **Contests / Sweepstakes** | Consumers | Medium | Low | High | Mass brand engagement |
| **Trade Discounts** | Wholesalers/Retailers | Short | Low | Medium | Push inventory through channel |
| **Free Goods (Buy-X-Get-Y)** | Retailers + Consumers | Short | Low | High | Boost trade loading |
| **Sales Force Contests** | Internal Sales Team | Short | None | Medium | Revive sluggish sales periods |
| **Co-op Advertising** | Retailers | Short | Low | Shared | Joint brand promotion |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Channel of Distribution — Three Structural Levels (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["MANUFACTURER"] --> Q1{"Level of Distribution?"}
    Q1 -->|Zero Level Direct| C1["CONSUMER"]
    Q1 -->|One Level| R1["RETAILER"] --> C2["CONSUMER"]
    Q1 -->|Two Level| W1["WHOLESALER"] --> R2["RETAILER"] --> C3["CONSUMER"]
    Q1 -->|Three Level| A1["AGENT OR C AND F"] --> W2["WHOLESALER"] --> R3["RETAILER"] --> C4["CONSUMER"]
    Q1 -->|Modern E Commerce| E1["E COMMERCE PLATFORM"] --> E2["LOGISTICS PARTNER"] --> C5["CONSUMER"]

    style M1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style W1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style W2 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style A1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style R1 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style R2 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style R3 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style C1 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style C2 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style C3 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style C4 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style C5 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style E1 fill:#bf9000,color:#000000
    style E2 fill:#bf9000,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Sales Promotion Methods — Hierarchical Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP["SALES PROMOTION TOOLS"] --> CO["CONSUMER ORIENTED PULL"]
    SP --> TO["TRADE ORIENTED PUSH"]
    SP --> SFO["SALES FORCE ORIENTED"]

    CO --> CO1["SAMPLES"]
    CO --> CO2["COUPONS"]
    CO --> CO3["CASH REFUNDS"]
    CO --> CO4["PRICE OFFS"]
    CO --> CO5["PREMIUMS GIFTS"]
    CO --> CO6["FREE TRIALS"]
    CO --> CO7["PRIZES SWEEPSTAKES"]
    CO --> CO8["LOYALTY PROGRAMS"]

    TO --> TO1["TRADE DISCOUNTS"]
    TO --> TO2["ALLOWANCES OFF INVOICE"]
    TO --> TO3["FREE GOODS BUY X GET Y"]
    TO --> TO4["PUSH MONEY DEALER LOADER"]
    TO --> TO5["SALES CONTESTS"]
    TO --> TO6["CO OP ADVERTISING"]
    TO --> TO7["TRAINING PROGRAMS"]

    SFO --> SFO1["SALES BONUSES"]
    SFO --> SFO2["SPIFFS"]
    SFO --> SFO3["CONTEST RALLIES"]
    SFO --> SFO4["RECOGNITION AWARDS"]

    style SP fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style CO fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style TO fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style SFO fill:#bf9000,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Distribution Channel Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRO["PRODUCER MANUFACTURER"] -->|1 Order Placement| WH["WHOLESALER DISTRIBUTOR"]
    WH -->|2 Bulk Breaking| RE["RETAILER OUTLET"]
    RE -->|3 Final Sale| CS["CONSUMER END USER"]

    PRO -.->|A Market Info Feedback| CS
    CS -.->|B Demand Signal| PRO
    WH -.->|C Inventory Carry| WH
    RE -.->|D Point of Sale Data| PRO
    WH -.->|E Credit Financing| RE
    RE -.->|F After Sales Service| CS

    style PRO fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style WH fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style RE fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style CS fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Solid arrows (1, 2, 3)** represent the *forward flow* of goods. **Dotted arrows (A–F)** represent the *backward flow* of information, finance, and service support — both are essential for a healthy channel.

## 4.4 Push vs. Pull Strategy — Visual Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DECISION{"What is the Strategic Objective?"}
    DECISION -->|"Create Trade Loading"| PUSH["PUSH STRATEGY"]
    DECISION -->|"Stimulate End User Demand"| PULL["PULL STRATEGY"]

    PUSH --> P1["Trade Discounts"]
    PUSH --> P2["Free Goods to Retailers"]
    PUSH --> P3["Dealer Contests"]
    PUSH --> P4["Sales Force Bonuses"]

    PULL --> Q1["Consumer Coupons"]
    PULL --> Q2["Free Samples"]
    PULL --> Q3["Mass Media Advertising"]
    PULL --> Q4["Loyalty Rewards"]

    PUSH --> RESULT1["Retailer Pushes to Consumer"]
    PULL --> RESULT2["Consumer Asks Retailer for Product"]

    style DECISION fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000
    style PUSH fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style PULL fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style RESULT1 fill:#fce4d6,color:#000000
    style RESULT2 fill:#e2efda,color:#000000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Push and Pull strategies in sales promotion with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Push Strategy** is directed at channel intermediaries (wholesalers, retailers) to motivate them to stock, display, and actively sell the product to end consumers. The manufacturer "pushes" the product down the channel. *Example:* A Hindustan Unilever offer of "Buy 10 cases, get 2 free" to a distributor during a festival season.

The **Pull Strategy** is directed at the end consumer, creating demand that "pulls" the product through the channel. The consumer asks the retailer, who in turn orders from the wholesaler, who in turn orders from the manufacturer. *Example:* Maggi's "₹10 cashback coupon" advertisement in TV commercials.

| Parameter | Push Strategy | Pull Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Channel members | End consumer |
| Cost burden | Trade margin | Advertising + redemption |
| Speed | Slower (requires trade education) | Faster (impulse + awareness) |
| Risk | Channel conflict | High promo cost |

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2. What is a Zero-Level Channel of Distribution? When is it most suitable?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Zero-Level Channel** (also called the **Direct Marketing Channel**) is the simplest distribution structure in which the manufacturer sells directly to the end consumer without any intermediary. The product moves in a single hop: Manufacturer → Consumer.

**Suitability Conditions:**
1. **High-value, complex products** (industrial machinery, software, customized solutions) requiring direct technical interface.
2. **Perishable or fragile products** (cakes, fresh seafood, flowers) where intermediate handling causes damage.
3. **Strong manufacturer financial capability** to bear logistics and warehousing costs.
4. **Geographically concentrated or niche markets** (e.g., premium bakeries, luxury brands).
5. **Tight service and feedback loops** (e.g., L&T, Tata Steel's direct industrial sales).

*Real-World Examples:* Amul (direct-to-home dairy app), Tata CLiQ Luxury, Apple Stores, ITC's e-Choupal for agri-inputs, direct-to-consumer (D2C) brands like Mamaearth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Choose ONE of the following two questions:**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a)** *"Explain the major methods of Consumer-Oriented Sales Promotion with suitable examples."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consumer-oriented sales promotion tools are aimed at the **end user** to stimulate trial, accelerate purchase, and encourage brand switching or repeat buying. There are **eight major methods**:

**1. Samples (Free Trial Packs):** A small quantity of the product is given free to consumers. This eliminates the financial risk of trying a new product. *Example:* Parle-G distributing free sachets at railway stations; L'Oréal giving free sachets of shampoo in newspaper inserts.

**2. Coupons:** Vouchers that entitle the holder to a price reduction at the point of purchase. They can be distributed via print, digital, mobile apps, or packaging. *Example:* Amazon Pay coupons, Nykaa ₹200 off coupons, FMCG newspapers coupons.

**3. Cash Refunds (Rebates):** The customer receives a cash refund after proof of purchase. Useful for converting brand switchers from competitors. *Example:* "Send the MRP flap + bill and get ₹50 back" campaigns for electronics.

**4. Price-Offs (Direct Price Reduction):** A reduced price is offered on the product itself (e.g., "₹50 off MRP"). Effective for stimulating immediate sales but risks **brand dilution** if used too frequently. *Example:* Surf Excel's "Buy 1 Get 1 ₹10 off" festive packs.

**5. Premiums / Gifts:** Free or low-cost items offered as a bonus with purchase. *Example:* "5-star chocolate wrapper = free notebook"; Kinder Joy toy inside.

**6. Free Trials:** Used primarily for services and software — full-feature use for a limited time. *Example:* Spotify Premium 30-day free trial, Netflix 1-month free trial.

**7. Prizes, Contests, Sweepstakes:** Consumers get a chance to win a prize. Creates excitement and engagement. *Example:* Pepsi "Number One" bottle-cap contests; Paytm Scratch cards.

**8. Loyalty Programs / Frequent Buyer Incentives:** Points or rewards for repeat purchases. *Example:* Marriott Bonvoy, Amazon Prime membership, CRED coins.

**Conclusion:** Each method should be chosen based on the **promotion objective, target audience, budget, and brand positioning**. Heavy price-off tools drive volume but can erode premium brand perception; premiums and contests build engagement without devaluing the brand.

**(b)** *"Discuss the factors affecting the choice of a Channel of Distribution. Illustrate with an example."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The choice of a distribution channel is a **strategic decision** that influences a company's revenue, cost structure, control, and market reach. KTU 2024 emphasizes **five factors** that determine this choice:

**1. Market Factors:**
- **Buyer Density:** Concentrated urban markets favor direct selling; dispersed rural markets require intermediaries. *Example:* Samsung Galaxy phones — direct company-owned showrooms in metros; multi-brand retailers in Tier 2/3 cities.
- **Order Size:** Industrial customers place large orders, justifying direct sales; consumers place small orders, requiring retailers.
- **Buying Patterns:** Impulse purchases (cold drinks, snacks) demand intensive distribution; planned purchases (cars) use selective distribution.

**2. Product Factors:**
- **Perishability:** Fresh milk (Aavin) needs short, fast channels.
- **Bulk/Weight:** Cement and steel use direct delivery.
- **Unit Value:** Luxury watches (Rolex) — exclusive; biscuits (Parle-G) — intensive.
- **Technical Complexity:** MRI machines require direct sales with trained engineers.

**3. Company (Producer) Factors:**
- **Financial Strength:** A well-funded firm (Apple) can run its own stores; a small firm must use intermediaries.
- **Desire for Control:** Direct channels give maximum control; intermediaries dilute it.
- **Marketing Expertise:** Firms with strong in-house marketing teams prefer direct channels.

**4. Competitive Factors:**
- Channels used by competitors must be analyzed. A firm may choose the **same channel** (e.g., Coca-Cola and Pepsi both use intensive) or **differentiate** (Tesla bypasses dealers and sells directly).

**5. Environmental Factors:**
- **Economic Conditions:** Recession → reduce channel length.
- **Legal Regulations:** Liquor and pharma are legally restricted to licensed channels.
- **Technology:** E-commerce platforms have **flattened traditional hierarchies** — small manufacturers can now reach consumers directly through Amazon/Flipkart.

**Illustration — Titan Watches:**
- *Market:* Premium, low-frequency purchase → selective distribution.
- *Product:* High unit value, requires aftersales servicing → company-managed service.
- *Company:* Strong brand and financial backing → Titan runs its own exclusive showrooms ("World of Titan") + selective multi-brand outlets.
- *Competitive:* Targets the same channels as Sonata, Fastrack but uses **exclusivity** in premium segment for differentiation.
- *Environmental:* Online sales (Titan's website) supplement offline exclusivity.

**Conclusion:** The optimal channel is one that **maximizes market coverage and customer satisfaction** while **minimizing distribution cost and channel conflict**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]*

**(a)** *"Explain Trade-Oriented Sales Promotion tools. How do they differ from Consumer-Oriented tools in terms of objectives and reach?"* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Trade-Oriented Sales Promotion** refers to marketing activities directed at **wholesalers, distributors, C&F agents, and retailers** to motivate them to stock, display, and aggressively sell the manufacturer's product.

**Key Trade Promotion Tools:**

**1. Trade Discounts:** A direct reduction in the invoice price offered to channel members, conditional on purchase volume or timely payment. *Example:* A FMCG company offers 5% trade discount on a minimum purchase of 100 cases.

**2. Off-Invoice Allowances:** A flat rebate paid to retailers per case purchased, often to reward past performance. *Example:* "₹50 per case off-invoice allowance for November sales."

**3. Free Goods (Bonus Packs):** Extra units of the product (or a complementary product) given free with a minimum order. *Example:* "Buy 10, get 2 free" deals for distributors.

**4. Push Money / Dealer Loader:** Direct cash incentive to the salesperson of the channel partner for promoting a specific SKU. *Example:* A car dealer paying ₹2000 to its salesperson for every test-drive conversion.

**5. Sales Contests:** A competition among retailers/distributors with attractive prizes (TV, gold, foreign trip) for the highest sales or display. *Example:* Asian Paints "Saal ka Sabse Bada Contest" for dealers.

**6. Co-operative Advertising:** The manufacturer shares the cost of local advertising with retailers. *Example:* Hindustan Unilever paying 50% of a retailer's local newspaper ad featuring Surf Excel.

**7. Training and Display Materials:** Free training for retail staff, POP (point-of-purchase) displays, shelves, danglers.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Consumer-Oriented | Trade-Oriented |
|---|---|---|
| **Target Audience** | End consumer | Channel intermediaries |
| **Primary Objective** | Stimulate demand | Stimulate supply / push inventory |
| **Typical Cost Bearer** | Manufacturer + retailer | Manufacturer |
| **Speed of Effect** | Direct & immediate | Indirect (channel lag) |
| **Risk** | Brand price erosion | Channel conflict, fake bill claims |
| **Measurability** | Coupon redemption, lift in sales | Stocking, shelf space, secondary sales |
| **Examples** | Coupons, samples, premiums | Trade discounts, free goods, contests |

**Conclusion:** While consumer-oriented tools *pull* demand, trade-oriented tools *push* the product through the channel. A balanced marketing mix uses **both** to ensure the product flows smoothly and is actively promoted at every channel node.

**(b)** *"Define Channels of Distribution. Explain the different levels of distribution channels with suitable diagrams."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
A **Channel of Distribution** is the path through which goods and services travel from the producer to the consumer or industrial user. It is a set of interdependent organizations (intermediaries) that make a product available for use or consumption.

**Levels of Distribution Channels:**

**1. Zero-Level / Direct Channel (Manufacturer → Consumer):**
The manufacturer sells directly to the consumer without any intermediary. This is the shortest and simplest channel. *Examples:* Amul dairy subscription, Apple Stores, Tesla direct sales, D2C brands (BoAt, Mamaearth), ITC e-Choupal, LIC insurance agents (technically direct, with agents being employees).

**2. One-Level Channel (Manufacturer → Retailer → Consumer):**
The manufacturer sells to a retailer (small shop, departmental store, or chain) who in turn sells to the consumer. Common for branded consumer durables, garments, and footwear. *Examples:* Bata selling its own brand through its own stores, Samsung selling through Croma.

**3. Two-Level Channel (Manufacturer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer):**
The most common channel for FMCG, grocery, and pharma. The manufacturer sells in bulk to a wholesaler/distributor, who breaks bulk and supplies to retailers, who finally reach the consumer. *Examples:* Hindustan Unilever products, Dabur, Coca-Cola distribution in India.

**4. Three-Level Channel (Manufacturer → Agent/C&F → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer):**
Used when the manufacturer uses a **C&F (Carrying & Forwarding) agent** or super-stockist, especially in geographically distant regions. The agent does not take title; they merely facilitate. *Examples:* Pharmaceutical companies using C&F agents in each state.

**5. Modern Distribution Channels (Digital):**
Manufacturer → E-commerce platform → Logistics partner → Consumer. *Examples:* Flipkart, Amazon, Swiggy, Zomato, Myntra, Nykaa, JioMart.

**Other Specialized Forms:**
- **Reverse Logistics Channel:** Consumer → Retailer → Distributor → Manufacturer (for returns, recycling, e-waste).
- **Dual Distribution:** Manufacturer uses **both direct and indirect** channels simultaneously (e.g., LG sells through exclusive brand stores + multi-brand retailers + online).
- **Franchising:** Manufacturer (franchisor) → Franchisee → Consumer. *Example:* McDonald's, Pizza Hut, Bata.

**Diagrammatic Representation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Zero-Level:} \quad & M \rightarrow C \\
\text{One-Level:} \quad & M \rightarrow R \rightarrow C \\
\text{Two-Level:} \quad & M \rightarrow W \rightarrow R \rightarrow C \\
\text{Three-Level:} \quad & M \rightarrow A \rightarrow W \rightarrow R \rightarrow C \\
\text{Modern:} \quad & M \rightarrow E \rightarrow L \rightarrow C
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $M$ = Manufacturer, $C$ = Consumer, $R$ = Retailer, $W$ = Wholesaler, $A$ = Agent/C&F, $E$ = E-commerce, $L$ = Logistics.

**Conclusion:** As the channel **lengthens**, the manufacturer's **reach increases** but **control and margin decrease**. The optimal channel length balances market coverage, cost efficiency, and brand control. Modern technology (e-commerce, direct shipping) is **disintermediating** traditional channels, but the functions of intermediaries remain essential — they have simply been **repackaged** in digital form.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Sales Promotion with Advertising:** Advertising builds long-term brand image; sales promotion drives short-term action with **tangible incentives**. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for conflating the two.
> 2. **Defining Push/Pull incorrectly:** Pull targets the *consumer*; Push targets the *channel member*. Reversing these is a common error.
> 3. **Mixing up channel levels:** Students often write "Three-level = Manufacturer → Wholesaler → Consumer" — forgetting the *Retailer* in the middle. Memorize: *every level adds one intermediary*.
> 4. **Omitting examples:** KTU's 2024 marking scheme **specifically rewards real-world examples** (Indian context preferred). Generic global examples without India references lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams:** For a 7-mark question on distribution, a labeled diagram earns **at least 2 free marks** even if the textual answer is average.
> 6. **Mathematical units:** Always include the ₹/INR unit in numerical answers. A "10" without "₹" is treated as incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Sales Promotion = Short-term, incentive-based, tangible reward** to accelerate demand or supply.
- ✅ **Three tiers of Sales Promotion:** Consumer-oriented (Pull), Trade-oriented (Push), Sales Force-oriented.
- ✅ **Consumer tools:** Samples, Coupons, Cash Refund, Price-Off, Premium, Free Trial, Prizes/Contests, Loyalty Programs.
- ✅ **Trade tools:** Trade Discount, Off-Invoice, Free Goods, Push Money, Sales Contests, Co-op Advertising, Training, Display Material.
- ✅ **Channel of Distribution = Path from producer to consumer** through intermediaries.
- ✅ **Channel Levels (memorize the sequence):** Zero (M→C) → One (M→R→C) → Two (M→W→R→C) → Three (M→A→W→R→C) → Modern (M→E→L→C).
- ✅ **Distribution Intensities:** Intensive (FMCG), Selective (durables), Exclusive (luxury).
- ✅ **Functions of Channel Members** cannot be *eliminated* — only *transferred* to the manufacturer.
- ✅ **Channel Conflict Types:** Vertical (mfr–retailer), Horizontal (retailer–retailer), Multi-channel.
- ✅ **Factors affecting channel choice:** Market, Product, Company, Competitive, Environmental (M-P-C-C-E).
- ✅ **Push vs. Pull:** Push = trade incentives; Pull = consumer demand creation.
- ✅ **Critical formulas:** Channel Margin %, Markup %, Promotion ROI, Break-even units, Redemption Rate, Channel Coverage %.
- ✅ **Key insight for Engineers:** Sales promotion reduces *perceived risk*, not just price; engineering products use free installation/warranty/EMI as "sales promotion."
- ✅ **Modern trend:** D2C brands + E-commerce = disintermediation, but logistics intermediaries (Amazon FBA, Delhivery) replace traditional ones.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favorite questions:** "Differentiate Push and Pull," "Explain channel levels with examples," "Discuss trade-oriented vs. consumer-oriented sales promotion."

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corporate Social Responsibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET504 - MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Corporate Social Responsibility # Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] **Core Definition** > **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** is a self-regulating business model that enables a company to be socially accountable to itself, its stakeholders, and the public. By practicing corporate social responsibility, companies can be conscious of the impact they are having on all aspects of society, including economic, social, and environmental. > [!NOTE] **KTU Board Definition (Most Accepted)** > *"CSR is the continuing commitment by business to behave ethically and contribute to economic development, while improving the quality of life of the workforce and their families as well as the local community and society at large."* > — **World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD)**, also known as the **Holme & Watts Definition** — most frequently quoted in KTU answer scripts. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a large banyan tree growing in a village. The tree gives shade to travelers, fruit to birds and villagers, oxygen to the air, and shelter to countless creatures — but it also draws water and nutrients from the soil. The villagers do **not** cut it down when it is useful; instead, they **protect it, water it, and even worship it** because the tree **gives back** more than it takes. A corporation is exactly like that banyan tree. It draws **natural resources, human labor, capital, and social goodwill** from society to produce goods and services. CSR is the company's promise to **give back to society** — not as charity, but as a **strategic, ethical, and legal obligation**. The company that only *takes* eventually wilts; the company that *gives back* grows stronger roots. ### 1.3 Key Terms in CSR (Must Memorize for KTU) - **Stakeholders** — Any group or individual affected by or affecting the company's operations (employees, customers, suppliers, community, government, environment). - **Triple Bottom Line (TBL / 3BL)** — Performance measured on three dimensions: **People, Planet, Profit**. - **Sustainability** — Meeting present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs (**Brundtland Report, 1987**). - **Philanthropy** — Voluntary giving of money, time, or resources (the *top* of the CSR pyramid). - **Ethical Behavior** — Conduct that conforms to accepted principles of right and wrong. - **CSR Mandate** — Legal compulsion (e.g., **Section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013** in India) that certain companies must spend on CSR. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Evolution of CSR (Timeline — KTU Frequently Asked) | **Period** | **Dominant View of Business** | **CSR Focus** | |---|---|---| | Pre-1950s | Profit maximization (Friedman) | Charity & donations | | 1950s–1970s | Stakeholder awareness begins | Bowen, Davis, Carroll definitions | | 1980s–1990s | Ethical & environmental concerns | Sustainable development, Brundtland | | 2000s onwards | Globalization & accountability | Triple Bottom Line, GRI, UN Global Compact | | 2013 onwards (India) | Legal mandate | **Section 135**, Companies Act 2013 | | 2015 onwards | **UN SDGs (17 Goals)** | Integrated reporting, ESG | ## 2.2 Carroll's CSR Pyramid (1981) — *Most Important KTU Question* Archie B. Carroll proposed that CSR consists of **four layers**, often drawn as a pyramid. The total CSR responsibility of a firm is the sum of all four layers. $$ \text{Total CSR} = \text{Economic} + \text{Legal} + \text{Ethical} + \text{Philanthropic} $$ ### Layer-by-Layer Breakdown **1. Economic Responsibilities (Base of Pyramid)** - Produce goods/services society needs at a profit. - Maximize shareholder value. - Be cost-competitive. - *The fundamental purpose* — without profit, the firm cannot survive to fulfill other duties. **2. Legal Responsibilities** - Obey the law — local, state, federal, and international. - Conduct business ethically within the legal framework. - Pay taxes, comply with labor laws, environmental regulations. - *"It is wrong to violate the law; it is not necessarily unethical if the law permits a certain action."* **3. Ethical Responsibilities** - Go *beyond* what is legally required. - Do what is *right, just, and fair* even when not compelled by law. - Respect stakeholders' moral rights. - Examples: honesty in advertising, fair wages beyond minimum wage, transparent reporting. **4. Philanthropic Responsibilities (Top of Pyramid)** - Voluntary contributions to community welfare. - Includes donations, scholarships, disaster relief, infrastructure development. - *Not mandated by law; expected by society.* - Examples: TATA Trust, Infosys Foundation, Reliance Foundation. > [!NOTE] **Memory Trick for KTU Exam** > **"E-L-E-P"** = **E**conomic, **L**egal, **E**thical, **P**hilanthropic. > Or imagine an elephant: **E**conomical feet (base) → **L**awful legs → **E**thical body → **P**hilanthropic trunk (top). ## 2.3 Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — John Elkington (1994) $$ \text{Sustainable Performance} = \text{People (Social)} + \text{Planet (Environmental)} + \text{Profit (Economic)} $$ | **Dimension** | **Focus** | **Examples** | |---|---|---| | **People (Social Equity)** | Human capital, community welfare, labor rights | Fair wages, education, healthcare, gender equality | | **Planet (Environmental)** | Ecological impact, resource use, emissions | Carbon footprint reduction, renewable energy, waste management | | **Profit (Economic)** | Economic value creation, shareholder return | Market share, profitability, sustainable growth | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Concept / Framework** | **Key Formula / Expression** | **Originator** | **Application** | |---|---|---|---| | Carroll's CSR | $\text{CSR} = E + L + E + P$ | A.B. Carroll (1981) | Hierarchical responsibility model | | Triple Bottom Line | $\text{Performance} = P_{social} + P_{env} + P_{econ}$ | John Elkington (1994) | Sustainability reporting | | CSR Spend (India) | $\text{CSR Spend} = \max(2\% \text{ of Avg. Net Profit},\, 0)$ | Companies Act 2013, **Sec 135** | Mandatory Indian compliance | | Net Profit for CSR | $\text{NP} = \text{Profit Before Tax (PBT)} - \text{Exceptional Items}$ | Sec 198 of Companies Act 2013 | Base for CSR calculation | | Stakeholder Salience | $S = f(\text{Power},\ \text{Legitimacy},\ \text{Urgency})$ | Mitchell, Agle & Wood (1997) | Identifying priority stakeholders | | Sustainability Index | $SI = \dfrac{\text{Output}_{sustainable}}{\text{Output}_{total}}$ | Custom metric | Performance benchmarking | | Carbon Footprint | $CF = \sum_{i} (E_i \times EF_i)$ | GHG Protocol | Environmental accounting | | Gini-like CSR Equity | $E = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i R_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | Weighted responsibility | Distribution analysis | > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Note** > In India, under **Section 135(5)** of the Companies Act 2013, companies meeting ANY of these three criteria in the preceding financial year **must** spend at least **2% of average net profits** of the preceding **3 financial years** on CSR activities: > 1. Net worth ≥ **₹500 crore**, OR > 2. Turnover ≥ **₹1,000 crore**, OR > 3. Net profit ≥ **₹5 crore**. ## 2.5 Stakeholder Theory (Edward Freeman, 1984) A company is responsible to **all stakeholders**, not just shareholders. The **primary stakeholders** are essential for company survival, while **secondary stakeholders** influence or are influenced by the company. - **Primary Stakeholders:** Employees, Customers, Suppliers, Shareholders, Government. - **Secondary Stakeholders:** Community, NGOs, Media, Trade Unions, Society at large. > [!NOTE] **Why CSR Matters — Real World Utility** > - **Reputation & Brand:** 87% of consumers purchase from companies supporting social/environmental issues (Cone Communications Survey). > - **Risk Management:** Prevents legal liabilities, strikes, and boycotts. > - **Talent Attraction:** 76% of millennials consider a company's social commitment when choosing an employer. > - **Investor Confidence:** ESG-focused firms attract long-term institutional capital. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: CSR Mandatory Spending Calculation (Indian Law) ### Step 1: Identify Eligibility A company is eligible for **mandatory CSR** under **Section 135(1)** of the Companies Act 2013 if it satisfies ANY ONE of the following thresholds in the **immediately preceding financial year**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Threshold Set} &= \big\{ \text{Net Worth} \geq \text{₹ 500 Cr} \big\} \\ &\cup \big\{ \text{Turnover} \geq \text{₹ 1,000 Cr} \big\} \\ &\cup \big\{ \text{Net Profit} \geq \text{₹ 5 Cr} \big\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Compute Average Net Profit (ANP) The base for CSR calculation is the **average of net profits of the last three financial years**, computed per **Section 198** of the Companies Act 2013. $$ \begin{aligned} ANP &= \frac{NP_1 + NP_2 + NP_3}{3} \end{aligned} $$ Where $NP_i$ = Net Profit in year $i$, after deducting **income tax** and **exceptional items** but **before** deducting **CSR expenditure** itself. ### Step 3: Compute Mandatory CSR Expenditure $$ \begin{aligned} CSR_{mandatory} &= \max\left(\, 0.02 \times ANP,\ 0 \,\right) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Determine Treatment if Not Spent If a company fails to spend the required amount, it must **disclose reasons in its Annual Report** (Board Responsibility Statement). However, **unspent CSR amount must be transferred to a separate Unspent CSR Account** within **6 months** of year-end and must be spent within **3 years**, failing which it must be transferred to the **PM CARES Fund** (Schedule VII). --- ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** XYZ Ltd. reports the following: | **Year** | **Net Profit (₹ Crore)** | |---|---| | FY 2021-22 | 200 | | FY 2022-23 | 250 | | FY 2023-24 | 300 | Net worth of FY 2023-24: ₹ 600 Crore. Turnover FY 2023-24: ₹ 1,200 Crore. **Determine:** 1. Is XYZ Ltd. eligible for mandatory CSR? 2. Calculate average net profit. 3. Compute mandatory CSR expenditure for FY 2024-25. **Solution:** **Step 1: Eligibility Check** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Worth} &= \text{₹ 600 Cr} \ \geq \ \text{₹ 500 Cr} \quad \checkmark \\ \text{Turnover} &= \text{₹ 1,200 Cr} \ \geq \ \text{₹ 1,000 Cr} \quad \checkmark \\ \text{Net Profit (FY24)} &= \text{₹ 300 Cr} \ \geq \ \text{₹ 5 Cr} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, XYZ Ltd. **IS ELIGIBLE** for mandatory CSR. **Step 2: Average Net Profit** $$ \begin{aligned} ANP &= \frac{NP_1 + NP_2 + NP_3}{3} = \frac{200 + 250 + 300}{3} = \frac{750}{3} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} ANP &= \text{₹ 250 Crore} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Mandatory CSR Expenditure** $$ \begin{aligned} CSR_{mandatory} &= 0.02 \times ANP = 0.02 \times 250 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} CSR_{mandatory} &= \text{₹ 5 Crore} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** XYZ Ltd. must spend **at least ₹ 5 Crore** on CSR activities in FY 2024-25. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Arguments For and Against CSR | **Aspect** | **Arguments FOR CSR** | **Arguments AGAINST CSR** | |---|---|---| | **Economic** | Long-term profitability, better ROI, operational efficiency, innovation | Short-term costs reduce profit; shareholder loss | | **Legal** | Prevents lawsuits; pre-empts stricter regulation | Increases regulatory burden; green-washing risk | | **Ethical** | Upholds justice, fairness, human dignity | Subjective — what is "ethical" varies by culture | | **Social** | Better community relations, social license to operate | Community may become dependent; inefficient NGOs | | **Environmental** | Resource conservation, climate compliance | Environmental costs raise product prices | | **Managerial** | Employee motivation, retention, brand value | Distracts managers from core operations | | **Strategic** | Competitive differentiation, market expansion | Smaller firms cannot afford structured CSR | > [!NOTE] **Kantian View (Immanuel Kant) — for CSR:** > *"Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law."* Companies must treat stakeholders as *ends*, not *means* — supports CSR. > [!NOTE] **Milton Friedman's View — against CSR:** > *"The social responsibility of business is to increase its profits."* Friedman argued CSR is a misuse of shareholder money. ## 3.3 CSR vs. Charity (Analytical Differentiation) | **Parameter** | **CSR** | **Charity** | |---|---|---| | **Nature** | Strategic, integrated into business | One-time, voluntary donation | | **Legal Status** | Mandatory under Sec 135 (in India for eligible cos.) | Always voluntary | | **Scope** | Continuous, long-term | Episodic, short-term | | **Stakeholders** | Multi-stakeholder approach | Often single beneficiary | | **Reporting** | Mandatory disclosure in Annual Report | Not required | | **Strategic Intent** | Aligned with business goals | No strategic alignment | | **Tax Treatment** | Treated as expenditure (not donation) | 80G tax benefit on donations | ## 3.4 Steps in Designing a CSR Program (Analytical Process) 1. **Identify CSR Vision & Mission** aligned with business values. 2. **Conduct Stakeholder Engagement & Materiality Assessment.** 3. **Select Focus Areas** from **Schedule VII** of Companies Act (education, health, environment, gender equality, rural development, etc.). 4. **Baseline Study & Needs Assessment** of the community. 5. **Set SMART Objectives** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound). 6. **Choose Implementation Mode:** Direct / NGO Partnership / Foundation / Public-Private Partnership. 7. **Budget Allocation:** Min. 2% of ANP, with impact-tracking. 8. **Monitoring & Impact Evaluation** using **Logical Framework Approach (LFA)**. 9. **Reporting** through **Annual CSR Report** and **Business Responsibility & Sustainability Report (BRSR)**. 10. **Continuous Improvement** based on feedback loops. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Impact Index (II)} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times O_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i} \end{aligned} $$ Where $W_i$ = weight of indicator $i$, $O_i$ = outcome score of indicator $i$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Carroll's CSR Pyramid (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB baseA[Economic Responsibilities]:::econ --> baseB baseB[Legal Responsibilities]:::legal --> baseC baseC[Ethical Responsibilities]:::eth --> baseD baseD[Philanthropic Responsibilities]:::phil classDef econ fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef legal fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef eth fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef phil fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Triple Bottom Line (3BL) — Sustainability Framework ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[People: Social Equity
Fair Wages, Education, Health]:::people P2[Planet: Environmental
Carbon Reduction, Green Energy]:::planet P3[Profit: Economic
Shareholder Value, Growth]:::profit P1 --- P2 P2 --- P3 P3 --- P1 P1 --> CORE[Corporate Sustainability]:::core P2 --> CORE P3 --> CORE classDef people fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef planet fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef profit fill:#bf9000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef core fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 CSR Process Flow (Section 135 Compliance Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart TD s1[Step 1: Check Eligibility
Sec 135 of Companies Act 2013]:::step --> s2 s2[Step 2: Constitute CSR Committee
Min 3 Directors]:::step --> s3 s3[Step 3: Compute Average Net Profit
Last 3 Years]:::step --> s4 s4[Step 4: Allocate 2% of ANP
for CSR Activities]:::step --> s5 s5[Step 5: Select Focus Area
Schedule VII Activities]:::step --> s6 s6[Step 6: Implement Project
via Trust, NGO, or Directly]:::step --> s7 s7[Step 7: Monitor and Evaluate
Impact Assessment]:::step --> s8 s8[Step 8: Disclose in Annual Report
and BRSR]:::step --> s1 classDef step fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Stakeholder Map (Primary vs Secondary) ```mermaid flowchart TB CO[Company
Center of CSR Activity]:::co P[Primary Stakeholders]:::prim --> CO S[Secondary Stakeholders]:::sec --> CO CO --> P CO --> S subgraph PRIMARY [Primary Stakeholders] P1[Employees] P2[Customers] P3[Shareholders] P4[Suppliers] P5[Government] end subgraph SECONDARY [Secondary Stakeholders] S1[Local Community] S2[NGOs] S3[Media] S4[Trade Unions] S5[Society at Large] end classDef co fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef prim fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef sec fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.5 CSR vs. Charity — Comparative Functional Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CSR]:::csr --> A1[Strategic] A --> A2[Mandatory if eligible Sec 135] A --> A3[Integrated into business] A --> A4[Multi-stakeholder] A --> A5[Reported in BRSR] B[Charity]:::char --> B1[Reactive] B --> B2[Always voluntary] B --> B3[One-time event] B --> B4[Single beneficiary] B --> B5[Not mandatorily reported] classDef csr fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef char fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define Corporate Social Responsibility. Mention any two arguments in favor of CSR.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) is the continuing commitment by business to behave ethically and contribute to economic development, while improving the quality of life of the workforce and their families, the local community, and society at large. *(WBCSD definition — Holme & Watts, 1999)* **Two Arguments in favor (1 mark):** 1. **Long-term Profitability:** Companies practicing CSR build a strong reputation, attract loyal customers, and achieve sustainable profitability. 2. **Risk Management & Social License:** Proactive CSR prevents legal disputes, strikes, and community conflicts, ensuring smooth operations. ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **What is Triple Bottom Line? Explain its three dimensions briefly.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The Triple Bottom Line (TBL or 3BL), proposed by **John Elkington in 1994**, expands the traditional reporting framework to include social and environmental performance in addition to financial performance. **Three Dimensions (2 marks):** 1. **People (Social):** Focus on human capital — fair wages, employee welfare, community development, diversity & inclusion. 2. **Planet (Environmental):** Focus on ecological responsibility — reducing emissions, conserving resources, sustainable sourcing. 3. **Profit (Economic):** Focus on economic value creation — sustainable growth, shareholder returns, market competitiveness. --- ## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain Carroll's Pyramid of CSR with a neat diagram. (7 marks)** **(b) Discuss the major arguments for and against CSR. (7 marks)** --- **Solution:** ### Part (a) — Carroll's Pyramid of CSR (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** Archie B. Carroll, in 1979 and refined in 1991, proposed a four-part definition of CSR, popularly visualized as a **Pyramid of CSR**. According to him, the total CSR of a firm is the sum of four types of responsibilities, ordered from most fundamental (base) to most aspirational (top). **The Four Layers (5 marks):** | **Layer** | **Responsibility** | **Description** | **Example** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 (Base) | **Economic** | Produce goods/services society needs at a fair price; be profitable. The foundation of business existence. | Tata Steel producing affordable steel profitably. | | 2 | **Legal** | Obey all laws — tax, labor, environmental, consumer protection. | Filing GST returns, complying with Factories Act. | | 3 | **Ethical** | Do what is right, just, and fair — beyond legal compliance. | Paying above-minimum wages voluntarily; honest advertising. | | 4 (Top) | **Philanthropic** | Voluntary contributions to community welfare and quality of life. | Infosys Foundation's mid-day meal program; TATA Trust education initiatives. | **Total CSR Formula (1 mark):** $$ \text{CSR}_{total} = \text{Economic} + \text{Legal} + \text{Ethical} + \text{Philanthropic} $$ **Conclusion:** The pyramid shows that all four levels must be fulfilled for a company to be a *truly socially responsible* enterprise. A firm focusing only on philanthropy without economic sustainability is destined to fail. --- ### Part (b) — Arguments For and Against CSR (7 marks) **Arguments FOR CSR (3.5 marks):** 1. **Ethical-Moral Argument:** Businesses use society's resources and hence have a moral duty to give back. *Kant's categorical imperative* applies. 2. **Long-term Profitability:** CSR improves brand image, customer loyalty, and operational efficiency, leading to sustained profits (supported by *Porter & Kramer, 2011, HBR*). 3. **Risk Mitigation:** Proactive CSR reduces chances of lawsuits, strikes, environmental disasters, and government penalties. 4. **Talent Attraction:** 76% of millennials prefer employers with strong CSR (Deloitte Survey 2021). 5. **Investor Confidence:** ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) investing reached **$35 trillion** globally in 2020 — firms with strong CSR attract capital. **Arguments AGAINST CSR (3.5 marks):** 1. **Friedman's Argument:** Milton Friedman (1970, *NYT*) argued that the *only* social responsibility of business is to increase profits within legal/ethical rules. CSR diverts resources from shareholders. 2. **Conflict with Profit Motive:** CSR spending reduces short-term earnings and may be resisted by shareholders. 3. **Managerial Incompetence:** Managers trained in business — not social work — may misallocate resources. *"Businessmen cannot be social workers."* 4. **Accountability Issues:** Difficult to measure social impact; potential for *green-washing* (falsely claiming CSR). 5. **Cost Burden on Small Firms:** SMEs cannot afford elaborate CSR programs; mandatory CSR laws hurt them disproportionately. **Balanced Conclusion:** Modern view (e.g., *Crane & Matten*) suggests CSR is not a substitute for profit but a *partner* to sustainable profit, creating shared value (*CSV — Creating Shared Value*, Porter & Kramer). --- ### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Explain the concept of CSR under the Indian Companies Act 2013. List the qualifying criteria and the prescribed CSR activities. (7 marks)** **(b) Differentiate between CSR and Charity with a comparative table. (7 marks)** --- **Solution:** ### Part (a) — CSR under Indian Companies Act 2013 (7 marks) **Legislative Background (1 mark):** Section 135 of the **Companies Act, 2013** made CSR spending **mandatory** for the first time in India. It came into effect on **1st April 2014**, with the Companies (Corporate Social Responsibility Policy) Rules, 2014 framing the operational rules. **Eligibility Criteria (Sec 135(1)) (2 marks):** A company must constitute a **CSR Committee** and comply if in the **preceding financial year** it satisfies ANY of: - **Net Worth** ≥ ₹ 500 Crore, OR - **Turnover** ≥ ₹ 1,000 Crore, OR - **Net Profit** ≥ ₹ 5 Crore. **Mandatory CSR Spending (2 marks):** Every eligible company must spend **at least 2% of its average net profits (computed under Sec 198)** of the **preceding three financial years** on CSR activities. $$ \begin{aligned} CSR_{spend} &= 0.02 \times \left(\frac{NP_1 + NP_2 + NP_3}{3}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Prescribed Activities (Schedule VII) (2 marks):** Activities may include (list any four): 1. Eradicating hunger, poverty, and malnutrition. 2. Promoting education and vocational skills. 3. Promoting gender equality and empowering women. 4. Ensuring environmental sustainability and animal welfare. 5. Protection of national heritage, art, and culture. 6. Rural development, slum area development. 7. Disaster management (incl. Covid-19 relief). **Discharge of CSR Obligation:** A company may undertake CSR through: - Direct programs, OR - Through a registered **Section 8 company** (non-profit), OR - Through a registered **public charitable trust**, OR - Through a **registered society**. **Disclosure:** Companies must disclose CSR policy and activities in the **Board's Report** and on the **MCA21 portal** (Ministry of Corporate Affairs). --- ### Part (b) — Difference between CSR and Charity (7 marks) | **Parameter** | **Corporate Social Responsibility** | **Charity** | |---|---|---| | **Meaning** | A company's strategic commitment to operate ethically and contribute to societal development. | Voluntary, one-time act of giving to the needy. | | **Nature** | Strategic, planned, continuous. | Episodic, ad-hoc, short-term. | | **Scope** | Wide — covers economic, legal, ethical, philanthropic dimensions. | Narrow — usually focused on a single cause. | | **Legality** | Mandatory under Sec 135 for eligible Indian companies. | Always voluntary. | | **Stakeholders** | Considers multiple stakeholders (employees, customers, community, environment). | Often benefits a single beneficiary group. | | **Business Linkage** | Integrated with business model and core strategy. | No strategic linkage to business. | | **Reporting** | Mandatory disclosure in Annual Report & BRSR. | No mandatory reporting. | | **Tax Treatment** | CSR spend is treated as expenditure; **not** eligible for tax deduction (per Sec 135). | Eligible for tax deduction under **Sec 80G** of Income Tax Act. | | **Motivation** | Long-term sustainability, brand, legal compliance. | Empathy, religious or moral sentiment. | | **Examples** | Infosys Foundation's 10,000+ scholarships; ITC's e-Choupal; Tata Water Mission. | Religious donations; one-time flood relief. | | **Sustainability** | Designed for long-term impact. | Often short-lived. | | **Risk of Failure** | Low — institutional frameworks involved. | High — poor monitoring, misallocation. | **Conclusion:** While charity is an act of goodwill, CSR is a **systematic, accountable, and strategic commitment** to society. Charity is the *top* of Carroll's pyramid; CSR is the **entire pyramid**. In the modern business environment, structured CSR is more impactful, measurable, and sustainable than traditional charity. --- > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing only **Carroll's Pyramid** without mentioning the **equation form** $\text{CSR} = E + L + E + P$. Examiners specifically reward the formula presentation. *— 1 mark lost.* > - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **Triple Bottom Line (Elkington, 1994)** with **Creating Shared Value (Porter & Kramer, 2011)** — they are different frameworks. > - **Common Mistake 3:** In CSR-Section 135 numericals, students often compute 2% of **Net Profit of current year** instead of **average net profit of last 3 years**. This is the most penalized error. *— 2 marks lost.* > - **Common Mistake 4:** Listing only Schedule VII activities partially. Examiners expect **at least 4-5 items** with correct categorization. > - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing **Carroll's Pyramid** (1979/1991) with **Davis's Iron Law of Responsibility** (1960). Remember: *who has power has responsibility*. > - **Common Mistake 6:** Writing *"CSR means charity"* — this is factually incorrect and reveals weak conceptual understanding. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **CSR Definition (most cited):** *"Continuing commitment by business to behave ethically and contribute to economic development, while improving the quality of life of the workforce, their families, local community, and society at large."* — **WBCSD (Holme & Watts, 1999)**. - **Carroll's Pyramid:** **E-L-E-P** — Economic, Legal, Ethical, Philanthropic. Sum them up: $\text{CSR} = E + L + E + P$. - **Triple Bottom Line (3BL):** **People, Planet, Profit** — proposed by **John Elkington (1994)**. - **Stakeholder Theory:** **Edward Freeman (1984)** — companies are responsible to all stakeholders, not just shareholders. - **Friedman (1970) vs. Carroll (1991):** Friedman = profit-maximization only. Carroll = four-tier responsibility. - **Section 135 of Companies Act, 2013** — made India the **first country to legally mandate CSR**. - **Threshold for Mandatory CSR:** Net worth ≥ ₹500 Cr **OR** Turnover ≥ ₹1,000 Cr **OR** Net Profit ≥ ₹5 Cr. - **CSR Spend Formula:** $\text{CSR Spend} = 2\% \times \text{Average Net Profit (last 3 years)}$. - **Average Net Profit** is computed as per **Section 198** of the Companies Act 2013 (after tax, excluding exceptional items). - **Schedule VII** lists 14 categories of permitted CSR activities (education, health, environment, gender equality, rural development, etc.). - **Disclosure Required** in **Board's Report** and **Business Responsibility & Sustainability Report (BRSR)**. - **Unspent CSR amount** must be transferred to a separate account within 6 months and spent within 3 financial years; otherwise → **PM CARES Fund**. - **Creating Shared Value (CSV)** — Porter & Kramer (2011) — links profit with social progress (often confused with CSR). - **Davis's Iron Law of Responsibility (1960):** *"Those who do not accept their responsibility will lose it."* - **Sustainable Development** — **Brundtland Commission Report (1987)**: *"Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."* - **UN SDGs (2015):** 17 Sustainable Development Goals that modern CSR frameworks align with. - **Indian CSR Examples:** Tata Water Mission, Infosys Foundation, Reliance Foundation, ITC e-Choupal, Mahindra Pride Schools, Adani Foundation. - **Modern Frameworks:** ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance), BRSR, GRI Standards, UN Global Compact, ISO 26000. - **Numerical Tip:** Always show the **Average Net Profit formula** and the **2% computation** step-by-step in the answer — this is what secures full marks. - **Diagram Tip:** Always draw **Carroll's Pyramid** as a **pyramid/triangle** with **4 levels labeled clearly**. Examiners reward visual presentation. - **KD Tip (Key Difference):** CSR is **strategic + mandatory**; Charity is **reactive + voluntary**. CSR = entire pyramid; Charity = top of pyramid. - **One-liner for Conclusion:** *"A business that takes from society but gives nothing back is not a corporate citizen — it is a parasite. CSR converts parasites into partners in progress."*
